question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the average waiting time for access to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department is considering for local businesses facing higher operating costs, due increased fuel costs.

To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the support that will be provided to households from the £17 million funding allocated by HM Treasury, including (i) the form that support will take; (ii) any eligibility criteria; (iii) the expected timeline for rollout; and (iv) when payments are likely to reach households.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the proportion of the road network, currently classified as being in poor condition, broken down by parliamentary constituency.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement that has taken place with her Department and energy suppliers, regarding potential price volatility in the coming months.

To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 43551/22-27, to detail any cross-departmental contingency plans being developed to support households and businesses in the absence of further intervention from HM Treasury.
answer_text: Following the announcement by the Chancellor that the Executive would be receiving £17.2 million to support households who rely on home heating oil, I wrote to the Minister for Communities, to ask that he urgently bring forward details as to how this funding could be used. The Minister for Communities has now brought forward proposals for a scheme which will allow eligible households to apply for a £100 prepaid card that can be used at heating oil suppliers. This scheme has been supported through an additional £19.2 million being committed by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 41735/22-27, to detail any follow up checks carried out before an applicant is removed from the waiting list due to no response to correspondence.
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 41735/22-27, whether any analysis has been carried out on patterns or trends in removals from the waiting list.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 41735/22-27, to detail the number of applicants removed from the waiting list where the reason was recorded as deceased, who were in housing stress, including the number recorded as homeless, at the time of their death.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applicants currently on the social housing waiting list who have been waiting more than (i) two years; (ii) five years; (iii) ten years; and (iv) 20 years.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought further information from NI Water to inform the response to your question.NI Water has advised that the developer for this site is engaging with the company through the wastewater impact assessment process, to find a solution that will permit this development a foul only connection to its wastewater infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 41735/22-27, to detail the number of applicants removed from the waiting list in (i) 2024-25; and (ii) 2025-26, were subsequently reinstated within six months of removal, broken down by reason for initial removal and reinstatement.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on a loneliness strategy.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer on this topic in AQW 6028/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any consideration has been given to bringing forward a Northern Ireland-specific veterans strategy.  [R]
answer_text: I continue to demonstrate a clear commitment to supporting veterans and have a number of announcements to make in the near future.  In building on this, my Department will also continue to engage closely with the Ministry of Defence and Office for Veterans’ Affairs to ensure that implementation of the UK Veterans’ Strategy 2025 meets the needs of veterans across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any representations he has made to HM Treasury seeking reductions in fuel duty or VAT on fuel to address cost pressures.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 43227/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any road maintenance schemes scheduled for East Belfast during the 2026-27 financial year.
answer_text: Officials are currently considering the 2026/27 programme of works, details of which will be included in my Department’s 2026/27 annual report to Council. Programmes cannot be finalised until such times as budgets are confirmed.In the meantime, my officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any engagement with the First Minister and deputy First Minister in relation to the introduction of post-19 Special Educational Needs legislation within the 2022–27 Assembly mandate.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will reconsider the decision to remove ring-fencing for youth services.
answer_text: Historically, the budget for youth services has been a standalone budget within the overall Education budget, allocated by my Department to the Education Authority (EA) specifically to meet the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities.Going forward, a single composite Block Grant allocation will be provided to the EA for both Schools and Youth services.  This will bring Youth funding into line with other established education budgets and will give the EA the flexibility to make appropriate, evidence-based decisions in respect of its budget in totality.I would emphasise that all spending remains subject to policy, legislation and Departmental oversight, and the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities remains.  As such I do not see the need to reconsider my decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any funding allocated to youth services, in each of the last three financial years; and (ii) the projected allocation for the next financial year, following the removal of ring-fencing.
answer_text: The table below details the funding allocated to youth services in the last 3 years.YearTotal Resource £’0002023/24 40,8452024/2541,7872025/26*43,017*Most up to date figures provided as year-end not availableThe Education Authority (EA) is unable to provide a projected allocation for the next financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the rationale for removing ring-fencing for youth services; and (ii) any evidence base used to inform that decision.
answer_text: Historically, the budget for youth services has been a standalone budget within the overall Education budget, allocated by my Department to the Education Authority (EA) specifically to meet the statutory requirement to secure adequate facilities for youth activities.Going forward, a single composite Block Grant allocation will be provided to the EA for both Schools and Youth services.  The rationale for this change is to bring Youth funding into line with other established education budgets and to give the EA the flexibility to make appropriate, evidence-based decisions in respect of its budget in totality.I would emphasise that all spending remains subject to legislation, policy, and Departmental oversight.  In short, the statutory requirement to secure adequate provision of youth activities remains.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the impact of removing ring-fencing on youth service provision, including (i) any projected changes to staffing levels; (ii) programme delivery; and (iii) access to services.
answer_text: No assessment has been made by my Department or the Education Authority (EA) of removing ring-fencing on youth service provision including (i) any projected changes to staffing levels; (ii) programme delivery; and (iii) access to services.	The EA will continue to receive funding to deliver both schools and youth services. Moving to a composite block grant simply brings youth funding into line with other established education budget processes. This gives the EA the flexibility to make appropriate, evidence-based decisions, while all spending remains subject to policy, legislation and Departmental oversight.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any safeguards in place to ensure that funding for youth services is not diverted elsewhere, following the removal of ring-fencing.
answer_text: My Department holds overall policy responsibility for the Youth Service, whilst the Education Authority (EA) has the statutory operational duty to secure the provision of adequate youth services.The primary safeguard in place in respect of funding for youth services is the statutory duty to provide youth services in Northern Ireland, established through the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, which places a legal obligation on the EA to ensure that adequate facilities and services are available for young people.This statutory duty is further strengthened by the Youth Service (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 which confirms that youth services are a recognised statutory function within the education system, not a discretionary or optional activity.This duty exists regardless of budgetary pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Ulster Scots Strategy.
answer_text: All Executive Departments are currently engaged in the development of the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy, alongside the Irish Language Strategy, through the work programme of the Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG).The CDWG continues to build on the work of each strategy’s expert advisory panel and co-design group to ensure that each strategy is coherent, costed and accompanied by a series of achievable actions within departmental remits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has presented any proposal to the Executive to increase the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme subsidy rate above 15 per cent.
answer_text: I have extended the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) beyond 1 April 2026 at the current subsidy contribution of 15%, however, as the Budgets for 2026-27, including the final allocation for the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, have not yet been set by the Executive  I am not in a position to provide any commitments to increase the percentage contribution for the NICSS.Any increase in the subsidy rate above 15% would require significant additional recurrent funding. Moving from 15% to 20% represents a one third uplift in the level of subsidy and would therefore require a broadly proportionate increase in the overall scheme budget.Proposals to increase the NICSS rate above 15% were presented to the Executive as a core element of the draft Executive ELC Strategy.  The draft Strategy sets out the Executive’s longterm ambition to subsidise over 50% of childcare costs for working families, which necessarily requires incrementally increasing NICSS beyond 15% over time.Any decision regarding future increases will be considered in the context of the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional funding that would be required to increase the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme subsidy rate from 15 per cent to 20 per cent.
answer_text: I have extended the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) beyond 1 April 2026 at the current subsidy contribution of 15%, however, as the Budgets for 2026-27, including the final allocation for the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, have not yet been set by the Executive  I am not in a position to provide any commitments to increase the percentage contribution for the NICSS.Any increase in the subsidy rate above 15% would require significant additional recurrent funding. Moving from 15% to 20% represents a one third uplift in the level of subsidy and would therefore require a broadly proportionate increase in the overall scheme budget.Proposals to increase the NICSS rate above 15% were presented to the Executive as a core element of the draft Executive ELC Strategy.  The draft Strategy sets out the Executive’s longterm ambition to subsidise over 50% of childcare costs for working families, which necessarily requires incrementally increasing NICSS beyond 15% over time.Any decision regarding future increases will be considered in the context of the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the reason why the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will remain at a subsidy rate of 15 per cent from 1 April 2026 rather, than increasing to 20 per cent; and (ii) any factors that informed that decision.
answer_text: I have extended the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) beyond 1 April 2026 at the current subsidy contribution of 15%, however, as the Budgets for 2026-27, including the final allocation for the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, have not yet been set by the Executive  I am not in a position to provide any commitments to increase the percentage contribution for the NICSS.Any increase in the subsidy rate above 15% would require significant additional recurrent funding. Moving from 15% to 20% represents a one third uplift in the level of subsidy and would therefore require a broadly proportionate increase in the overall scheme budget.Proposals to increase the NICSS rate above 15% were presented to the Executive as a core element of the draft Executive ELC Strategy.  The draft Strategy sets out the Executive’s longterm ambition to subsidise over 50% of childcare costs for working families, which necessarily requires incrementally increasing NICSS beyond 15% over time.Any decision regarding future increases will be considered in the context of the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any feedback his Department have received on the current draft of the Ulster Scots Strategy.
answer_text: All Executive Departments are currently engaged in the development of the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy through the work programme of the Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG). In addition, the Department has received feedback on the current draft through wider sector engagement, including key delivery partners, the Ulster-Scots Agency and the Commissioner for the Ulster-Scots and the Ulster British Tradition.As part of the policy in development process, the feedback received to date is being considered as the Strategy continues to develop. Further feedback shall be sought via the public consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times that transport provision to and from Edgcumbe Day Centre was cancelled, in each of the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the action plans associated with each of his Department’s strategies will be published.
answer_text: The action plan for my Department’s Warm, Healthy Homes Strategy is scheduled for publication by 31 December 2026. Action plans for all other strategies within my Department’s remit will be published as soon as possible following Ministerial and, where required, Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has undertaken any assessment of the need for fully compliant Changing Places toilets facilities across all hospitals.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any hospitals that have a registered and fully compliant Changing Places toilet facility.
answer_text: Antrim Area Hospital and the South-West Acute hospital have registered and fully compliant Changing Places Toilet facilities. The location of registered and compliant changing places toilets across NI may be found at www.changing-places.org.My Department has not undertaken any assessment of the need for fully compliant changing place toilets facilities across all hospitals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with each local council during the development of the Anti-Poverty Strategy, including; (i) the dates of those engagements; (ii) the councils involved; and (iii) the nature of the engagement.
answer_text: During the development of the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy, my Department engaged with a wide variety of individuals and organisations across Northern Ireland, including representatives from Local Councils. As part of the consultation on the Anti-Poverty Strategy which ran from 17 June - 19 September 2025, officials engaged with representatives from the Society of Local Authority Chief Executives and Senior Managers (SOLACE) on 27 August 2025 to outline the contents of the draft Strategy and to take their views and suggestions on it. This included representatives from Ards and North Down; Fermanagh and Omagh; Lisburn and Castlereagh; Newry, Mourne and Down; Causeway Coast and Glens Armagh City; Banbridge and Craigavon Councils.My Department also consulted with the Northern Ireland Local Government Association(NILGA) on 4 September 2025, engaging with representatives from Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council; Mid-Ulster District Council; Armagh Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council; Derry City and Strabane District Council; Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council; Fermanagh and Omagh District Council; Newry Mourne and Down District Council; Belfast City Council; Ards and North Down Borough Council.  Written responses to the consultation were received from Ards and North Down Borough Council; Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council; Belfast City Council; Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council; Derry City & Strabane District Council; Fermanagh and Omagh District Council; Mid-Ulster District Council, Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the planned road maintenance schedule for East Belfast, over the next 12 months, including (i) the location and nature of works; (ii) the anticipated start and completion dates; and (iii) any budget allocated to each scheme.
answer_text: Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2026/27 financial year will be published in my Department’s annual report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of complaints received by the Housing Executive in relation to routine maintenance, in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) complaint type; and (ii) time taken to resolve.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 41734/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will bring forward, before the end of this month, an emergency support plan to mitigate the impact of changes to the Local Growth Fund on affected organisations and service users.
answer_text: On 22 January 2026 the Executive wrote to Steve Reed, Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, calling on him to work with his Ministerial colleagues to rectify the profile of the Local Growth Fund here with a more favourable Resource allocation.A response was received on 2 March 2026. Disappointingly, the Secretary of State has not provided any change to the Capital/ Resource Split on the Local Growth Fund. I will now engage with my Executive Colleagues to consider the implications of this. However, the Executive’s Budget remains extremely constrained, which continues to put pressure on essential public services, leaving limited scope to mitigate this shortfall, which is as a direct result of the folly of Brexit, the loss of EU funds and poor decisions by Whitehall Government Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of potholes reported in East Belfast, in the last 12 months, broken down by (i) priority classification; (ii) target response time; and (iii) the actual response time, for each priority level.
answer_text: The Department identifies actionable defects primarily through routine highway safety inspections, through reporting on the online portal, through other customer correspondence, supplemented by ad hoc investigations where resources permit. Data on pothole reporting is published each financial year to date on the Department’s Open Data Platform https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/pothole-enquiries  and can be filtered by council area, defect recorded date, response time priority, completion date, and status. Further information on report definitions is available on the website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has taken a decision on the introduction of funding to support payment of the Real Living Wage for independent domiciliary care workers.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the risk to service continuity should independent domiciliary care providers be unable to meet increased employment costs within existing commissioning rates.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of the extent to which the 2026-27 Homecare Regional Hourly Rate of £22.13 meets the full economic cost of delivering domiciliary care services.
answer_text: My Department keeps homecare funding under active review, and the 2026–27 regional rate of £22.13 reflects the continued application of uplifts to support providers with recognised cost pressures.   I acknowledge that some providers consider that the rate is not sufficient to meet the full cost of delivering Homecare services.  My officials continue to engage closely with sector representatives on this issue.  As part of my 3 Year Delivery Plan for Reforming Adult Social Care I have committed to undertake an economic appraisal of the fair cost of adult social care and support to inform rates of payment/future charging policies.  It should be noted that as the Independent Health and Care Providers (IHCP), the main representative body of homecare and care home providers in Northen Ireland, has paused engagement with my Department on social care reform issues it may not be possible to progress the fair cost of care exercise at this point.Severe budgetary pressures meant it was not possible to implement the Real Living Wage (RLW) for independent sector care workers during 2025/26. However, I remain committed to progressing RLW implementation at the earliest affordable opportunity, and it will be a key priority for the 2026/27 planning process.My Department’s Fair Work Forum has developed an evidencebased case for RLW implementation, informed by survey responses from both social care workers and independent sector employers. The Justification Report for the introduction of the RLW was published on 9 February on my Department’s website.I remain committed to ensuring the Regional Homecare rate will be uprated to enable providers to pay the Living Wage as required by Procurement Note PRN 01/21. My officials are engaging with Trust colleagues to ensure that procurement requirements align with Departmental policy and there is consistency in relation to implementation of the RLW.Evidence gathered through the Fair Work Forum highlights that many independent providers are experiencing significant recruitment and retention pressures, with a number of employers reporting they do not pay the Real Living Wage due to cost constraints. This reflects the wider financial strain on the sector and the associated risk to service continuity. I fully recognise that rising employment costs, including increases in the National Living Wage and employer National Insurance contributions, can have a destabilising impact on providers and may limit their ability to maintain services within existing commissioning rates. My officials remain committed to continue to work with representative bodies to understand the pressures facing the sector and to explore options that support workforce stability, fair remuneration, and continuity of care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department provided any guidance to Health and Social Care Trusts regarding the inclusion of Real Living Wage requirements within domiciliary care procurement exercises.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any funding allocated in 2025-26 to independent domiciliary care providers to meet (a) the increase in Employer National Insurance Contributions from 13.8 per cent to 15per cent; and (b) the reduction in the secondary threshold from £9,000 to £5,000; (ii) any methodology used to calculate this funding; and (iii) whether the allocation reflects the full estimated cost of these changes.
answer_text: The regional home care provider hourly rate increased by 6.7%, from £20.01 per hour in 2024/25 to £21.35 per hour in 2025/26.  Part of this increase was resultant from the changes in Employer National Insurance contributions (NIC), both rate and threshold, which came into effect in April 2025.  (i) The changes introduced in April 2025 increased the rate of Employer NIC to 15% from 13.8% and also lowered the threshold above which Employer NIC is payable from £9,100 to £5,000 per year.  Both changes were applied in tandem to calculate an increase in the provider hourly rate.In 2025/26, over £16m was provided to HSC Trusts to facilitate payment of domiciliary care providers in respect of the hourly rate increase to £21.35.  Of this funding, over £4.8m related to support for the change in the Employer NIC rate and threshold from April 2025.(ii) The increase in the hourly rate in respect of the changes to Employer NIC, set out above, was calculated using a full time salary estimation, calculating the difference in the Employer NIC liability from the previous to the Employer NIC rate and threshold effective from April 2025 and using this information to derive an increase to the Employer NIC cost per hour.It should be noted that the use of the assumption of full time working hours provides a higher funding rate per hour, than using an assumption of any average part time working hours per week.(iii) It is not possible to estimate the impact of the cost of the April 2025 Employer NIC changes for each individual provider.  Whilst I appreciate that the provision of a blanket uplift will not always address the changing costs of each individual independent sector provider, I am satisfied that the level of funding provided is sufficient to cover the cost of paying staff the National Living Wage and the associated Employers National Insurance contributions at the revised rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the number of appeals to parking fines for obstructive parking that have been heard by the Northern Ireland Traffic Penalty Tribunal in each of the last three years. [R]

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any (i) local councils that have not signed up to the Fly-Tipping Protocol; and (ii) reasons provided by each local council for not doing so.
answer_text: (i) Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council and Mid and East Antrim Borough Council have not signed up to the Fly-Tipping Protocol.(ii) Although I remain supportive of all councils joining the Fly-tipping Protocol, I cannot not speak on their behalf. Accordingly, I recommend you consider contacting each council directly for clarification regarding their reasons for not participating in the Fly-tipping Protocol.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of maintenance issues categorised as (a) emergency; (b) urgent; and (c) routine; (ii) the target response and completion times for each category; and (iii) performance against those targets, including the percentage met and not met, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: Severe and unsustainable financial pressures have left my department unable to provide the Education Authority (EA) with sufficient funding to carry out routine maintenance or invest in long-term improvements. Instead, resources have had to be directed to cover statutory requirements and address the most urgent, health and safety-related repairs in a reactive approach. Consequently, all maintenance activities have been classified as urgent or emergency, with no planned preventative or routine maintenance currently being carried out.The delivery of Maintenance is one of EA’s key front-line services for schools and it has been clear in the last five years that the service did not properly meet schools’ needs for target response and completion times and for performance management generally. The EA is implementing new Term Service Contracts, which will offer significant changes, including: Completion of task orders in compliance with specified Managed Performance Measures (Key Performance Indicators) to include but not limited to:Quality of workmanship and responsiveness.Adherence to health and safety requirements.Live reporting/tracking of maintenance worksAdherence to task order response and completion timesLive integration with EA management information systems, resulting in live reporting on all task orders, whilst providing early warning of contractual non-performance and incidental building and mechanical & electrical works to avoid repeat visits and ensure works are carried out as a first fix visit.In the current financial year 2025/26, 10 of the 18 lots have been successfully implemented across the schools’ estate, with the remaining contractual lots scheduled to commence from the 1 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any practical differences the newly announced dedicated telephone and text advice service for the Armed Forces community will deliver, compared to the existing Make the Call service; and (ii) whether additional, tailored, provision will be available to serving personnel, veterans and their families. [R]
answer_text: The Veterans and Armed Forces Pathway will have its own dedicated telephone and text channels. This includes staff whose understanding of Armed Forces related issues will be strengthened through ongoing consultation with veterans and Armed Forces support groups, alongside clearer referral pathways to specialist support organisations.This pathway will help identify potential eligibility for benefits, support, and services. It will be available to veterans, service personnel transitioning to civilian life, and their families, helping to reduce the risk of any disadvantage and ensuring they can access the right support at the right time. My Department is also enhancing wider engagement and coordination across the wider Veterans and Armed Forces area. This includes:	Active participation in veterans’ roadshows to improve awareness and access to support. In addition, I have instructed officials to work in partnership with the Northern Ireland Veterans’ Commissioner to explore options for additional roadshows to highlight the full range of departmental services, including housing support, benefits advice, debt guidance, employability services, and other assistance.Improving collaboration with both the Northern Ireland Veterans Commissioner and the Office for Veterans’ Affairs through a trusted partnership relationship where representatives will liaise directly with my Senior Officials on cases already in the system across a range of issues including housing, access to benefits and employability. Establishing a strategic lead for engagement on veterans’ issues within my Department and exploring the need for a dedicated forum to identify gaps in provision, improve information sharing, and coordinate support more effectively across partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the most recent condition grading of the school estate, broken down by grade; and (ii) how that compares with five years ago.
answer_text: There is no formal condition grading system for the school estate. The Education Authority (EA) is currently in the third year of a five-year cycle of condition surveys. These surveys only report on the 'poor' condition of the building fabric and infrastructure.There is no available comparison from five years ago because there was no structured programme of condition surveys at that time, due to budget constraints. Previously, condition surveys were conducted on an ad-hoc basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Fly-Tipping Protocol, to detail (i) its objectives; (ii) the intended outcomes; (iii) how its effectiveness will be measured; and (iv) any evaluation undertaken to date on its impact on enforcement, clean-up times and inter-agency working.
answer_text: The Waste and Contaminated Land (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 conferred upon local councils powers equivalent to those held by the Department in relation to waste crime, including flytipping, as set out in the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997. The Flytipping Protocol was subsequently established to provide clarity on the respective roles and responsibilities of local councils and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA).Under the Protocol, local councils are responsible for the management of nonhazardous flytipped waste below 20m³, as well as hazardous waste that they are licensed to accept. NIEA is responsible for nonhazardous waste deposits of 20m³ or more, and for hazardous waste that local councils are not licensed to accept.NIEA does not hold information on the specific operational approaches adopted by individual local councils in addressing flytipping under the Protocol. Accordingly, this response is confined to the operation of the Flytipping Protocol insofar as it relates to NIEA responsibilities.(i) Objectives of the ProtocolThe objective of the Protocol is to provide clear, agreed operational arrangements between the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) and Local Councils for dealing with flytipping.(ii) Intended outcomesThe protocol is intended to make sure flytipping is dealt with quickly, consistently and by the right authority, by clearly setting out who does what between the Northern Ireland Environment Agency and local councils. It aims to improve cooperation, reduce confusion and disputes, manage waste flytipped waste to minimise harm to communities and the environment.(iii) How effectiveness will be measured.The protocol does not set out formal performance measures or targets, instead effectiveness is measured by the overall number of incidents cleared up and the timeliness and quality of the cleanups. The number of incidents and the related tonnage of waste reported to NIEA varies considerably from year to year.  Due to limited resources, tracking fly-tipping trends over time is challenging. However, the total amount of fly-tipped waste reported to NIEA and subsequently removed has generally declined, as shown in the table below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of maintenance issues reported across the school estate, broken down by (i) category of issue; and (ii) the length of time taken to complete repairs against the target repair time, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: Following the holding response that issued to you on 20 March 2026, the Education Authority (EA) has now collated the information you requested and the response is set out in a table which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.The information you have requested in relation to the length of time taken to complete repairs against the target repair time is not available. The delivery of Maintenance is one of the Education Authority’s (EA) key front-line services for schools and it has been clear in the last five years that the service did not properly meet schools’ needs for target response and completion times and for performance management generally. The EA is implementing new Term Service Contracts, which will offer significant changes, including specified Managed Performance Measures (Key Performance Indicators) to include but not limited to:Quality of workmanship and responsiveness.Adherence to health and safety requirements.Live reporting/tracking of maintenance worksAdherence to task order response and completion timesIn the current financial year 2025/26, 10 of the 18 lots have been successfully implemented across the schools’ estate, with the remaining contractual lots scheduled to commence from the 1 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any Barnett consequentials received by the Executive, arising from UK Government policy decisions relating to energy, fuel poverty, and household energy support, broken down by (i) policy decision or funding stream; (ii) amount;  (iii) date received; and (iv) any conditions attached, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Barnett Consequentials arising relating to energy, fuel poverty and household energy support would fall under the Warm Homes Plan.The Spending Review announced a £13.2 billion commitment to the Warm Homes Plan between 2025-26 and 2029-30. The £13.2 billion included £5 billion of financial transactions and Barnett consequentials.However, as Barnett consequentials provided at a Spending Review are identified at Whitehall department level rather than individual programme level there is no transparency on the level of consequentials relating specifically to the Warm Homes Plan. It is worth noting that the Barnett formula only applies to changes to Whitehall departments budget, announcements funded from existing departmental baselines do not give rise to consequentials.Further to that, on 21st January 2026, the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero announced a further update on the Warm Homes Plan. The plan proposes a spend of some £15 billion over the next five years. It is yet unclear what Barnett consequentials, if any, will flow to the Executive from this announcement. As Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, it is a matter for the Executive to agree how this funding will be allocated as part of the multi-year Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps he intends to take to resolve service gaps created by GP withdrawal from shared care arrangements, including (ii) who holds prescribing responsibility in the interim; and (ii) any safeguards that are in place to prevent treatment interruption or patient harm.
answer_text: Shared care arrangements have been developed and introduced in order to provide a more convenient supply mechanism for patients who require ongoing therapy from one or more ‘amber listed’ drugs, a broad range of medications which require specialist oversight but which may, at the discretion of the GP practice be supplied locally.  In instances where a GP practice is unable or unwilling to enter into a shared care arrangement with the specialist clinic, or withdraws from an existing arrangement, then supply can continue through the specialist who made the therapeutic recommendation. This will be the case for both Health & Social Care and privately provided specialist services.This being the case, interruptions in supply should be avoided as the involvement of the patient’s GP practice is a convenience rather than a necessity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GP practices that have (i) withdrawn from; or (ii) declined to enter, shared care prescribing arrangements, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The decision to enter into or withdraw from a shared care agreement, or not, is a clinical decision for individual GPs to make and as such, is not monitored by my Department at Northern Ireland or Trust level.I fully recognise and respect that as independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are ultimately responsible, clinically and legally, for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of their individual patients.  That being the case, and as outlined in my response to AQW 41104/22-27, when my officials raise this matter with the elected representatives of the GP profession, common reasons cited include:a lack of sufficient expertise within their practice to safely manage and monitor the amber listed drugs concernedinsufficient capacity within the practice to manage the additional workload and clinical responsibility required to safely manage the higher risks involved with these medicationsinsufficient confidence that the specialist making the recommendation to prescribe an amber listed drug will be in a position to meet their ongoing responsibilities under the shared care arrangement. GP representatives report that recommendations from specialists for amber listed drugs are on occasions made by private clinics including online services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any funding allocated to fuel poverty measures, broken down by (i) Department, or budget line; (ii) scheme, or intervention; and (iii) resource and capital split, in each of the last five years,
answer_text: In the last five years the Executive has provided specific funding for Fuel Poverty on three occasions. As part of the November 2020 COVID–19 Funding Allocations, £44.3 million was provided to the Department for Communities to enable a one-off heating payment of £200 to disabled people on higher rate allowances and older people in receipt of pension credit. This was in recognition of the additional cost imposed on these vulnerable groups by the COVID-19 pandemic;In January 2021, the Executive agreed an allocation of £55.0 million to the Department for Communities to establish an Energy Payments Support Scheme; andAs part of the 2024-25 October Monitoring Round, the Executive provided the Department for Communities with an allocation of £17 million for winter fuel support.You should note that, outside of these specific allocations, the Department for Communities, along with other departments, would have been able to make use of their non-ringfenced budgetary allocations to provide funding for fuel poverty schemes. It would have been for each Minister to decide how to use the funding allocated in line with their own individual departmental priorities.  It would therefore be for each individual department to provide any available information in relation to such schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the primary reasons cited by GP practices for (i) withdrawing from; or (ii) declining to enter, shared care arrangements.
answer_text: The decision to enter into or withdraw from a shared care agreement, is a clinical decision for individual GPs to make and as such, is not monitored by my Department.That being the case, when my officials raise this matter with the elected representatives of the GP profession, common reasons cited include:a lack of sufficient expertise within their practice to safely manage and monitor the amber listed drugs concernedinsufficient capacity within the practice to manage the additional workload and clinical responsibility required to safely manage the higher risks involved with these medicationsinsufficient confidence that the specialist making the recommendation to prescribe an amber listed drug will be in a position to meet their ongoing responsibilities under the shared care arrangement. GP representatives report that recommendations from specialists for amber listed drugs are on occasions made by private clinics including online servicesPlease note that my Department’s officials have reminded GPs that whilst there is no obligation on a GP to prescribe, in instances where a GP considers that the medication being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council’s (GMC) guidance on Good Medical Practice, then they may choose to prescribe.  As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medicine falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnostic pathway, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.If a GP considers that ongoing prescribing of a medicine recommended by a private practitioner is appropriate for supply through the HSC service, then under their terms and conditions they may issue an HSC prescription. A private sector diagnosis does not preclude GPs entering into shared care arrangements. However, it remains at their discretion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when confirmed funding allocations will be issued to youth organisations currently at conditional offer stage under the 2026-2029 youth funding scheme.
answer_text: I am not presently in a position to provide any commitments in relation to a timeframe for agreement of the Executive’s and thereby my Department’s 2026-27 final opening budget allocation. Given the scale of pressures facing my Department in 2026-27 and beyond, I cannot provide a guarantee of funding levels. Following confirmation of my Department’s 2026-27 final opening budget and subsequent agreement of the Department’s budget strategy, the Education Authority (EA) will receive a composite block grant budget allocation for both schools and youth services.  The EA and its Board will make decisions on how best to deploy the EA’s budget which will include consideration of youth organisations currently at conditional offer stage for the period 2026-2029. It will therefore be for the EA to consider the cost of delivering youth services, within its block grant budget allocation, in line with the EA’s regional assessment of need process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) hospital; and (ii) other health service, appointments recorded as missed where the patient cited a delay in receiving the appointment letter as the reason for non-attendance, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There is currently no regionally agreed method for recording missed appointments resulting from delayed or undelivered postal communications. Consequently, data is not available on the number of missed hospital or other health service appointments due to a delay in patient’s receiving appointment letters as a reason for non-attendance. My Department has provided recurrent funding to Health and Social Care Trusts to support a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In addition to issuing appointment letters, Trusts also use text message reminders to notify patients of upcoming appointments across most services, helping to minimise patients’ inconvenience.Furthermore, the Encompass system is now fully operational across Northern Ireland. All patients and service users are able to securely access a subset of their health and social care record via the My Care application, which provides secure online access to personal health information such as appointment letters, clinic letters, medication details, and some test results. Patients can also view appointments and track the progress of their treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of hours of domiciliary care that is currently delivered per week by private sector providers, defined as all providers outside direct Health and Social Care provision, in each Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the weekly cost amount of the privately delivered domiciliary care hours, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of hours of domiciliary care that is currently delivered per week by private sector providers, and the weekly cost amount of the privately delivered domiciliary care hours is set out in Table 1.Table 1: Domiciliary Care hours per week and weekly cost per HSC TrustTrust(i) Domiciliary Care Hours per week(ii) Domiciliary Care weekly cost£BHSCT43,818£935,514NHSCT49,248£1,051,447SEHSCT62,705£1,338,752SHSCT35,722£762,670WHSCT28,888£616,759It should be noted that:The Domiciliary Care Hours per week have been provided by HSCT Trusts using a snapshot week and the weekly cost has been calculated by applying the 2025/26 regional rate of £21.35 to the total weekly hours per Trust.The domiciliary care provider payment rate per hour includes a provision for the gross cost of those social care workers delivering this home care service and a provision for other general costs.Due to data availability within Trusts, the sample weeks used varied across datasets. In addition, Trusts applied several assumptions to calculate the Domiciliary Care hours per week. As a result, direct comparability between figures should be treated with caution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of whether the abrupt withdrawal of shared care prescribing arrangements for ADHD medication, particularly where a patient is clinically stable, may present clinical and safeguarding risks; and (ii) to detail any guidance that has been issued to GP practices regarding minimum notice periods before discontinuing prescribing.
answer_text: While there is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland, a private sector ADHD diagnosis does not preclude GPs entering into shared arrangements, however it remains at their discretion.I am aware that where a GP is being asked to provide an HSC prescription to a patient based on the recommendation of a private practitioner, there may be additional factors to consider, including requests for amber drugs such as ADHD medication.  Furthermore, I understand that where a GP chooses to prescribe or enter into shared care arrangements following a private diagnosis, there are associated clinical and legal liabilities therefore a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.   I fully recognise and respect that as independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are ultimately responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of their individual patients. Shared care arrangements between a specialist medical practitioner (ADHD) and a GP are often the most convenient for the patient concerned. When these arrangements are no longer available then the medication can continue to be provided by the specialist and clinic who initiated the therapy, so avoiding the impact of an abrupt withdrawal of treatment. Across the NHS ADHD medication is classified as ‘amber’ meaning that responsibility for prescribing may be transferred from secondary to primary care when agreed shared care arrangements for the patient have been established between the specialist in ADHD care and patient’s GP. GPs who agree to share care with a specialist in prescribing ADHD medication have access to a range of clinical guidance including the British National Formulary (BNF); the National Institute for Clinical Evidence (NICE); HSC online shared care guidance for each amber drug is accessible to all practices in N Ireland, setting out the role of the specialist, the GP and the patient (eg to attend for regular hospital monitoring appointments) in order to ensure that this medication is managed safely. At the point of prescribing GPs across N Ireland have access to OptimiseRx, a decision support solution providing patient specific guidance and safety alerts during the prescribing process e.g. alerting that a medicine is on the amber list.Should a change in medication be required for whatever reason, including discontinuation, then, in managing an amber drug a GP would normally also seek advice from the ADHD specialist involved in the patient’s care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether his Department is exploring, as part of the Disability Strategy, any steps to improve access to information for people with visual impairments, including the availability of information in accessible formats such as large print; and (ii) whether this work includes engagement with cafés and restaurants to encourage the provision of menus in large print or other accessible format [R]
answer_text: Outcome 4 of the draft Disability Strategy commits all relevant departments to take steps to ensure that Deaf and disabled people can access and participate in culture, leisure activities and sport on an equal basis to other people.Work to progress this Outcome, including the issues raised during the public consultation process and those specified in the member’s question, will be considered on a cross-departmental basis and with the continued input of Deaf and disabled people.The final decision on what is included in the Disability Strategy remains with the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment he has made of the financial impact on patients who are required to move to fully private prescribing arrangements following the withdrawal of GP shared care support.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of their individual patients. They remain responsible for clinical decisions regarding the care of individual patients and as such they should exercise their clinical judgement prior to providing any pre and/or post treatment support for individuals availing of private healthcare.In specific circumstances, where a GP is being asked to provide an HSC prescription to a patient based on the recommendation of a private practitioner, there may be additional factors to consider. The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultation.  The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications, including Red-Amber list drugs, to patients under their care. In particular, the limitations with regards to requests to GPs for transfer of ongoing prescribing of medication which for the patient’s GP may not be clinically acceptable.  This guidance has not changed.	While there is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland, a private sector ADHD diagnosis does not preclude GPs entering into shared arrangements.  However, it remains at their discretion. My Department does not monitor the financial impact on patients who decide to move to fully private prescribing arrangements following the withdrawal of GP shared care support. As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in their own skills and capacity to monitor and manage these specialist-initiated drugs; in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient by the recommending specialist; and that the patient will adhere to clinical monitoring requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment he has made of the number of GP practices that have withdrawn from ADHD shared care prescribing arrangements, in the last five years; and (ii) whether his Department monitors the impact of such decisions on patient access to medication.
answer_text: The decision to enter into or withdraw from a shared care agreement, or not, is a clinical decision for individual GPs to make and as such, is not monitored, nor numbers of shared care agreements for ADHD in operation collected. My Department does not collect information on the number of existing share care arrangements in practices. When a GP practice decides not to prescribe ADHD medication the patient may still receive their medication and ongoing monitoring from the hospital specialist who recommended their therapy. The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practices, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultations. The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to the prescribing of medications to patients under their care.   This guidance still applies and has been supplemented in September 2024 by guidance issued by my Department ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care Prescribers’. This guidance provides a reminder that the legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription, and it is the responsibility of the individual prescriber to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether post-intervention assessments are carried out for households or businesses, supported through energy efficiency or energy support schemes, overseen by her Department; (ii) any indicators that are used to assess changes in energy affordability or consumption, following intervention; and (iii) any evidence held on changes to energy bills or energy usage, following support, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI deliver the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant on behalf of my Department, supporting businesses to buy and install energy-efficient equipment. Within the scheme, applicants must provide project details including estimated savings whilst also outlining current energy use. Projections are verified by Invest NI’s technical team, with funding only released after an assessment has been carried out following final installation. Since 2023, my Department has provided additional funding to the NISEP programme, administered by the Energy Saving Trust on behalf of the Utility Regulator. Within the programme, contractors must complete percentage level quality checks on the installation of both cavity wall (25%) and loft insulation (5%) achieving set pass rates to avoid triggering an investigation. Building control inspections are also required on all loft insulation installations. Neither Invest NI or the Utility Regulator assess changes to energy bills or energy usage following intervention as this is not typical of schemes of this type given the many factors that can influence energy use. The evaluation of savings is assessed on engineering and deemed savings estimates which are verified based on previously independently monitored energy improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether post-intervention assessments are carried out for households supported through the Affordable Warmth Scheme; (ii) any indicators used to assess changes in fuel poverty status; and (iii) any evidence held on changes to household energy bills following intervention, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 40221/22-27 the information has been placed in the assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of social and affordable homes delivered; (ii) the number of households on the social housing waiting list; and (iii) the average waiting time for applicants, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 40220/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of individuals appointed to public bodies; (ii) the number of those appointees who declared a recognised disability; and (iii) the proportion this represents of total appointments, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of individuals appointed to my department’s public bodies in each of the last five years is as follows: 9 in 2021/22; 16 in 2022/23; 25 in 2023/24; 26 in 2024/25; and, 22 in 2025/26.  The NI Statistical Research Agency produces statistics on public appointments on behalf of all NICS Departments.  However, due to disclosure control considerations, it is not possible for public appointment disability statistics to be provided at a departmental level.  The latest published figures, available at https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/topics/public-appointments-reports, show that in 2021/22, where disability status is known, 6% of appointees across all public bodies declared a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department has taken to strengthen consumer protections for households reliant on home heating oil, including any engagement with the regulator or industry.
answer_text: The analysis of energy prices using publicly available sources of information, including for home heating oil, is conducted by my department in ongoing development of policy in this area.  My officials also continue to work closely with the Utility Regulator and Consumer Council across a range of issues, including energy prices.Regarding consumer protection, the Competition and Markets Authority have jurisdiction to investigate and take action to address anti-competitive practices.  Alongside this, to protect against unfair trading practices, Trading Standards Services (TSS) has the power to investigate complaints of this nature and can take enforcement action where consumer protection regulations have been breached, including around the sale and delivery of home heating oil.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any oversight arrangements in place to identify patterns of (a) repeat failure; or (b) poor quality delivery, across Departments, including any action to be taken where issues are identified.
answer_text: In addition to the governance and accountability arrangements which exist in individual departments, the NI Civil Service (NICS) Board – as the senior decision making and leadership forum for the Civil Service – provides collective oversight of and advice to the Executive on delivery. Supported by committees aimed at driving forward the development and delivery of the Executive’s Programme for Government, the NICS People Strategy and the Investment Strategy, the Board is the lead vehicle for identifying and resolving systemic, cross-cutting delivery and governance issues. The Board’s oversight role is strengthened by its non-executive members, who provide independent external challenge and scrutiny. Examples of recent actions taken by the Board to strengthen delivery include the commissioning of a system to monitor implementation of major capital projects and support necessary interventions; commissioning an end-to-end review of education capital delivery; and commissioning a new workforce model to ensure the NICS has the skills needed to serve our people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail, in each of the last five years; (i) the number of street lighting faults reported; (ii) the number of faults that required more than one attendance to resolve; (iii) the total number of repeat attendances; (iv) the time taken to restore lighting, following an initial report; (v) the total cost of street lighting repairs; and (vi) the quality assurance measures in place, where faults reoccur within a defined period.
answer_text: The number of street lighting defects reported in each of the last 5 years is shown in the table below.Financial YearStreet Lighting Defects Reported2020/202147,1562021/202237,4872022/202331,1422023/202425,7772024/202521,380Determining the number of street lighting faults that required more than one attendance to resolve, or the number of repeat attendances, would necessitate a substantial datagathering exercise across the Department. Such an exercise would likely produce information with only a limited degree of accuracy and would very likely involve disproportionate costs. However, I can assure the Member that, where an outage repair is unsuccessful, the contractor responsible is required to return and complete the repair at no additional cost to my Department.Street lighting outages are generally being repaired within the required timeframe of five working days from receipt of the initial report. However, underground cable faults, where excavation works are necessary, may take longer to locate and repair.The table below shows the costs to fund my Department’s street lighting maintenance service. This programme includes outage repairs, underground cable fault repairs, vandalism and other miscellaneous works.Financial YearStreet Lighting Resource Allocations2020/2021£4.5m2021/2022£4.6m2022/2023£3.1m2023/2024£5.0m2024/2025£3.5mWhere repeat faults occur within a defined period, my Department’s lighting management system records the warranty status of all equipment used in street lighting repairs. Where a further fault arises during the applicable warranty period, it is automatically flagged and the defective component is replaced at no cost to the Department by the contractor responsible.My officials also monitor contractor performance and undertake regular onsite inspections and audits to ensure the quality of workmanship and full compliance with contractual requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of occasions on which schools were required to close, in whole, or in part, due to heating-related issues; and (ii) any protocols in place to mitigate against school closures arising from heating-related issues. in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that there have been 67 recorded school closures due to heating-related issues. These figures do not include closures of grant maintained integrated or voluntary grammar schools. YearNumber of closures202262023132024172025202026 (to date)11Mitigating actions taken by the EA include the Maintenance Helpdesk and Out of Hours protocols as well as condition survey reporting and maintenance officer routine site visits which will initiate necessary remedial works via the response or emergency maintenance workstreams.Severe and unsustainable financial pressures have left my department unable to provide the EA with sufficient funding to carry out routine maintenance or invest in long-term improvements. Instead, resources have had to be directed to cover statutory requirements and address the most urgent, health and safety-related repairs in a reactive approach that cannot continue.There is a need for increased and sustained investment to allow for a programme of planned, preventative maintenance. I have called for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern and inclusive environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of occasions on which schools were required to close, in whole, or in part, due to non-heating related issues; and (ii) any protocols in place to mitigate against school closures arising from non-heating related issues, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: School closure days can be broken into two categories, planned and unplanned. Unplanned closures may constitute an ’Exceptional Closure Day’. This is when the school closes for a whole day, without a morning register having been taken, and usually occurs due to adverse weather. The table below shows the number of days schools recorded by the Department as an ‘Exceptional Closure Day’ in each of the academic years from 2021/22 to current.Academic Year Number of Exceptional Closure Days reported to Department of Education2021/222062022/234772023/246102024/253062025/261086Date unknown (data incorrectly entered by school)12TOTAL2,697There may be other instances where schools have closed during the day and after a morning register has been taken, for instance due to utility failure. The Education Authority’s (EA) Maintenance Service has recorded 46 closures, which were due to non-heating issues and required maintenance intervention since the 2022 calendar year. YearNumber of closures202252023820241320251320267Mitigating actions taken by the EA include the Maintenance Helpdesk and Out of Hours protocols as well as condition survey reporting and maintenance officer routine site visits which will initiate necessary remedial works via the response or emergency maintenance workstreams.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of maintenance issues reported by schools; (ii) the number which required repeat repair; (iii) the number of repeat interventions; (iv) the cost of school maintenance works, and the proportion attributable to repeat repairs; (v) the number of completed works inspected; and (vi) any quality assurance measures in place where works fail within a defined period, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information you have requested, detailing (i) the number of maintenance issues reported by schools; (ii) the number which required repeat repair; (iii) the number of repeat interventions; (iv) the cost of school maintenance works, and the proportion attributable to repeat repairs; (v) the number of completed works inspected; and (vi) any quality assurance measures in place where works fail within a defined period, in each of the last five years is not readily available in the form requested and can only be collated at disproportionate cost.However, I can advise that the Education Authority’s (EA) Maintenance Service handles over 60,000 task orders annually for school reported maintenance issues, costing approximately £50 million per year across both capital and recurrent budgets. These tasks are managed through response, statutory compliance, emergency and unavoidable workstreams, with a primary focus on keeping schools open and ensuring children's safety.The EA has confirmed that the new Term Service Contracts will emphasise key performance indicators targeting poor contractor responses, completion times, workmanship quality and reducing repetitive site visits, with a stronger emphasis on achieving first-time site fixes.Severe and unsustainable financial pressures have left my department unable to provide the EA with sufficient funding to carry out routine maintenance or invest in long-term improvements. Instead, resources have had to be directed to cover statutory requirements and address the most urgent, health and safety-related repairs in a reactive approach that cannot continue.There is a need for increased and sustained investment to allow for a programme of planned, preventative maintenance. I have called for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern and inclusive environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of complaints received in relation to Disabled Facilities Grant awards, broken down by (i) complaint type; and (ii) outcome, or resolution, in each of the last five years,
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NIHE.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s records show that it has issued formal responses to 48 Disabled Facilities Grant–related complaints at First Stage, Final Stage and Ombudsman level over the five calendar years from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2025.A complaint progresses through the Housing Executive’s internal process, beginning with the grants delivery office at First Stage and moving to the independent central grants team at Final Stage. Both stages must be completed before a complaint can be referred to the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman (NIPSO).YearVolume of DFG ComplaintsStage of complaint / resolutionFirstFinalOmbudsman2021541020222014512023128402024201120259522Total4831134The Housing Executive does not hold the above data broken down by complaint type. However, it is important to note that some complaints relate to matters outside the Housing Executive’s control. These may include issues involving Health Trust actions, disputes arising between an applicant and their chosen contractor, delays in receiving statutory approvals (such as planning permission and/or building control), payment of professional fees, or concerns regarding the standard of work carried out by the applicant’s contractor.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps (i) taken; or (ii) currently being explored, to better support Disabled Facilities Grant recipients in managing the delivery of works and ensuring that grant-funded adaptations meet the intended outcomes.
answer_text: In respect of (i), NIHE fund two Home Improvement Agencies (HIA’s) who are available to support all DFG applicants with grant paperwork and with the identification and appointment of architects and building contractors. Applicants are notified of the support available at application stage. Health Trust Occupational Therapists (OTs) are involved throughout the DFG process to ensure the recommended adaptations are met and are appropriate to meet the grant applicant’s needs. NIHE also engage with applicants before grant approval and final payment to ensure proposed and completed works fully meet the applicant’s needs. In respect of (ii), my Department, along with NIHE and the Department of Health, are currently carrying out a review of NIHE-administered Private Sector Grants, including the Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG), to identify improvements to the operation of the grant and to improve outcomes for grant recipients. The review is focusing on delivering three key outcomes:Quicker access to grant funding A more user-friendly customer journey Lived experience informs service improvements. A public consultation on proposals for change is planned for Spring 2026.Also, to ensure that grant-funded adaptations meet the intended outcomes, NIHE is collaborating with Ulster University and Bangor University, North Wales to develop and implement a Housing Outcomes model. The proposed model will enhance the evidence base for adaptation provision by completing assessment pre and post completion to establish the impact of completed works on applicant’s safety, independence and quality of life.In addition, the NIHE has reviewed the Warranted Builder Scheme and has made enhancements to improve consumer protection and encourage other providers to participate in the scheme. The scheme provides:Registration and insurance of grant-aided projects up to £70,000Dispute resolution services throughout the process Improved assurance of the contractor’s quality and financial standing. It is intended that this scheme will be publicised by the end of April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail, in each of the last five years (i) the number of potholes reported for repair, broken down by constituency; (ii) the number of those potholes that required more than one repair; (iii) the total number of repairs carried out on those potholes; (iv) the total cost of pothole repairs and the proportion of that cost attributable to repeat repairs; (v) the number of pothole repairs that were inspected post-completion; (vi) the number of inspections that identified defects, requiring further remedial work; and (vii) the contractual or quality assuran
answer_text: I refer the member to my answers to AQWs 39211/22-27, 40006/22-27 and 39446/22-27.The cost for repairing potholes falls within the Department’s budget for Patching. This term is applied to small scale works necessary to keep roads in a satisfactory state of repair, including infilling potholes but also the repair of small areas affected by cracking or settlement, as such it is not possible to provide a breakdown of the cost attributable solely to pothole repairs.My Department does not hold information on the number of potholes that required more than one repair or the number of repairs carried out on those potholes in a readily accessible format, as temporary repairs are by their nature temporary and are used in the interest of safety prior to a permanent repair being actioned. Any attempt to provide this information would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of utility reinstatements that were inspected near the end of their guarantee period; (ii) the number of those inspections that resulted in a failure; and (iii) the number of those failed reinstatements that required remedial works to be carried out by (a) the responsible utility company; or (b) her Department, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a statutory undertaker fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the undertaker is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.The number of reinstatement inspections inspected near the end of the guarantee period, Sample C inspections, the number of those inspections which failed and number of those failed reinstatements that required remedial works by the statutory undertaker in each of the last five years are detailed in the table below:24/2523/2422/2321/2220/21Sample C Inspections4,6764,5144,4944,6194,370Sample C Failed Inspections137132136150123Utility Remedial Works Required 137132136150123

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of housing repair requests received by the Housing Executive; (ii) the number of requests which required repeat attendance for the same issue; (iii) the number of repeat repairs carried out; (iv) the cost of housing repairs, and the proportion attributable to repeat repairs; (v) the number of completed repairs inspected; (vi) the number of inspections that identified defects requiring further remedial work; (vii) the quality standards applied to housing repair works; and (viii) the (a) contractual; (b) performance management; or (c)
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 35079/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.
