question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the steps his Department is taking to strengthen enforcement against fly-tipping and illegal waste disposal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any initiatives taken forward by his Department to support veterans, in each of the last five years. [R]
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to provide an answer to your question, I have arranged for this to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34883/22-27, whether he has made any progress on appointing an Armed Forces Liaison Officer. [R]
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will implement an annual top up for the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme, through the multi-year budget process.
answer_text: I recently announced the financial support that my Department will provide to the NI Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) this year. This represents a top up of £2.2m. Since 2023, my Department has provided over £5.6 million in additional support to NISEP and to date, this funding has helped 1,945 lowincome households across the North, to improve the warmth and energy efficiency of their homes.  The Utility Regulator manages NISEP and my officials will continue to work closely with them, to explore options for additional financial support within any future multiyear budget framework.  As you know, any future funding must be considered within the extremely challenging budget context we find ourselves in.  I remain committed however, to ensuring that no one is left behind as we transition to a greener, more sustainable economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any initiatives taken forward by his Department to support veterans, in each of the last five years. [R]

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of how long, at the current rate of house building, it will take to reduce the housing waiting list; and (ii) any steps he is taking to accelerate progress.
answer_text: Although over 9,000 new social homes started on-site and over 6,000 new social homes completed during the period 2020/21-2024/25, the overall number of Applicants and Applicants in Housing Stress has continued to rise. It is therefore difficult to be definitive about how long it will take for Waiting Lists to be reduced.In December 2024, I published my Housing Supply Strategy 2024-2039 which outlines a 15-year framework for the whole system change needed to deliver Housing supply across all tenures and to create a housing system that can deliver at least 100,000 homes. Subject to budget one third of these will be social homes, alongside an increased supply of intermediate homes delivered throughout the strategy lifetime.Action plans are being developed to support the implementation of the Housing Supply Strategy to support enhanced delivery of new housing across all tenures, including social housing.I have initiated distinct but interlinked reviews of the design standards and grant support for social housing delivery, with a view to bringing forward sustainable models to enhance long term delivery.I have also circulated to Executive colleagues for comment, an initial draft of an Executive paper regarding the enhanced use of public land assets for affordable housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has had any engagement with stakeholders, regarding the future of Connswater Shopping Centre.
answer_text: The closure of Connswater Shopping Centre was regrettable for the businesses and the employees that were impacted. At the time of closure, my Department's Careers Service was available to support anyone who faced redundancy or was made unemployed as a result of the closure.Our high streets and city centres are changing. I am willing to work across Departments to look at how we can support the development of strong and thriving high streets. I have provided funding to Local Economic Partnerships to work alongside Departments in developing economic plans that meet the needs of their local community. My Department has not received any proposals linked to the site of the former Connswater Shopping Centre.Urban regeneration is within the remit of the Department for Communities and you may therefore wish to also ask that Department or Belfast City Council if they have had any engagement with stakeholders on the future of the former Connswater Shopping Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) road work permits, requested by utility companies; (ii) footpath work permits, requested by utility companies; (iii) reinstatements, inspected by her Department; and (iv) those reinstatements that passed inspection, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.Statutory Undertakers (such as utility companies) have a legal right to place and maintain their apparatus in the footway and carriageway granted by the appropriate utility regulator and do not need a permit to open the road or footpath. Undertakers must notify their works on the Northern Ireland Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) which provides an essential tool for communication of works notices between the statutory undertakers and the Department.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a statutory undertaker fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the undertaker is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.The number of works, reinstatement inspections and reinstatement inspection passes identified in each of the last five years are detailed in the table below:24 / 2523 / 2422 / 2321 / 2220 / 21Number of Works (Insp units)53,39155,11953,79254,57549,323Reinstatement Inspections9,5589,63410,0629,7058,948Reinstatement Insp. Pass8,8268,6198,7278.6418,149

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has considered requesting HM Treasury to transfer the full Local Growth Fund allocation to his Department to allow greater flexibility over the balance between capital and revenue spend.
answer_text: The Executive cannot swap funds between capital and resource with permission of the Treasury, and therefore, transferring the Local Growth Fund under its current breakdown would not be of any benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the adequacy of current funding and provision for adults with learning disabilities and autism who are unable to work, particularly in relation to access to day centres and structured daily support; and (ii) any engagement his Department has had with other departments in addressing any identified gaps.
answer_text: My Department recently completed a 14-week public consultation on the draft Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) and associated Service Delivery Plan (SDP), which closed on 25 November 2025. As part of this work, we worked closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to undertake a comprehensive review of current funding levels and service provision across the region, including day opportunities and structured daily support for adults with learning disabilities and autism.This review has confirmed that demand for these services continues to outstrip available capacity and that further development and expansion of meaningful, community-based day activities will be required to support people with a wide range of needs. Reflecting this, the draft LDSM Service Delivery Plan includes an action to develop regionally agreed criteria for day care and day opportunities, ensuring that adults with a learning disability who are unable to engage in work can access purposeful and appropriate daily activities.Next steps will include publication of a summary of consultation responses and a detailed analysis of the feedback received. This will inform refinement and finalisation of the Service Model by early Spring 2026. Once finalised, the Service Model and SDP will guide decisions on the future configuration of services and on any additional investment required to support delivery, including development of an updated Learning Disability Workforce Plan. Delivery of the ambitions outlined within the Model will be subject to funding made available to my Department.My Department continues to operate within a constrained financial context and must prioritise work that can be delivered within current resources. Any future commissioning decisions will be informed by evidence of demand and a clear understanding of service need.The LDSM outlines the goals for young people with learning disabilities, including those with complex needs, to receive personalised, lifelong support that allows for choice and control. I fully recognise that alignment across services, funding and workforce planning will be essential to achieving improved outcomes.The LDSM presents an overarching system of services and interventions with the service user and their family in the centre, and has the potential to go further than other models by addressing the interfaces with housing, education and employment, which other jurisdictions are seeking to address via strategic plans and policy interventions. However, it is recognised that decisions will be required on a cross-Departmental basis and there are associated actions within the SDP to improve access to housing, employment, and transport.Collaborative work is also ongoing with officials from the Department for the Economy following Minister Archibald’s formal announcement on 16 June 2025, to submit a second call for transformation proposals around ‘Transitions and Pathways’. I recognise that effective transitions management is ultimately depend

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to support local high street businesses, including measures to (i) boost footfall; (ii) improve resilience; and (iii) support long-term sustainability.
answer_text: In March 2022 the Executive Office published its Report with recommendations on the High Street Task Force. My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. This recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Work is ongoing within my Department to progress this recommendation and this includes the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter. This work will have a positive impact across town centres in the North. My officials are also working with all eleven Councils to develop Local Economic Partnerships to address economic need on a sub-regional scale. More broadly, my Department provides a range of support for SME through Intertrade Ireland and Invest Northern Ireland who offer SME a broad mix of export, innovation, capabilitybuilding, and funding supports designed to help them grow sales, modernise operations, develop new products, and expand into new markets. Together, they provide grant funding, expert mentoring, crossborder trade assistance, and innovation programmes that enable SME to scale sustainably and improve competitiveness.Supporting our high streets is a key part of town centre regeneration, which is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he is considering any initiatives to support businesses facing financial difficulty with the cost of non-domestic rates.
answer_text: 75% of non-domestic properties are currently entitled to some form of rate relief, which amounts to around a quarter of a billion pounds worth of support.Consultation on enhancements to the Small Business Rate Relief scheme for 2026/27 closed on 29 January, my officials are currently reviewing the findings. It is important to note that this scheme already supports almost 30,000 businesses. The Draft Budget consultation includes a proposal to allocate £10 million in 2026-27 for potential enhancements to the scheme supporting small businesses. Subject to approval, this funding will assist small businesses that are integral to our local communities.  My Department has also restored and extended the Back in Business and rural ATM schemes to support businesses and town centres. Industrial derating policy also applies here, providing over £75M of support. That measure is unique to this jurisdiction and supports over 4,400 manufacturers, a sector which provides around 90,000 jobs within our local economy. The ongoing cost pressures for businesses are also recognised and reflected in my Draft Multi-Year Budget with a lower uplift for non-domestic ratepayers of 3%, an uplift below the current rate of inflation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any action her Department is taking to reduce energy prices for all consumers.
answer_text: My Department continues to work closely with the Utility Regulator and the Consumer Council, to ensure that energy costs are as low as possible. The Energy Strategy is moving us away from having to import fossil fuels over which we have no control on cost. Our overarching aim is to deliver self-sufficiency in affordable renewable energy, paying a fair price for the energy we produce locally. I will soon be publishing the Smart Meters Design Plan. Smart Meters will provide consumers with the opportunity to lower their electricity costs.For business, Invest NI is delivering the £40 million Energy Resource and Efficiency Programme, enabling companies to reduce their energy use, lowering costs and reducing environmental impact.We are also engaging with officials in London to understand how announcements in the Autumn Budget can lead to support for consumers’ energy costs here.I also provided a further £2.17m into NISEP, supporting mainly lower income households to reduce their energy costs. This brings funding to £5.67m over the past 3 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for Infrastructure on updating legislation to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking. [R]
answer_text: AnswerI have not had any engagement with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to updating legislation to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking. The legislative provision under which the PSNI operate when dealing with obstructions on a road or pavement is the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, which is a matter solely for the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any assessment his Department has made of the effectiveness of amendments to Building Regulations introduced in 2022 in relation to Changing Places Toilets. [R]
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the utilisation of the former Elmgrove Primary School site, in East Belfast.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed two classrooms within the former Elmgrove site are currently occupied by The Willow EOTAS Centre. This service was previously located in a building on the Clarawood site which was vacated for November to allow additional intake into Greenwood House School. Historically Greenwood House School catered for pupils from nursery until P2, at which point they transferred to a mainstream setting or another local special school.  In recent years the demand has been increasing for special school places, reducing capacity for those transferring from Greenwood House. As a result, September 2025 was the first year that Greenwood House School catered for pupils in P3, eliminating the need for them to transfer to other schools.Existing accommodation is limited and as a result the EA, in agreement with the school, is currently progressing a minor works scheme to develop the former Elmgrove PS site for use as a dual campus for Greenwood House School. It is anticipated that accommodation will be available for use by the school in the 2026/27 academic year, with the site developed in phases providing 4 classes initially for existing P2 and P3 pupils.This will allow the school to help meet the demand for special school places in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the options she considered to address obstructive pavement parking, including vehicles parked partially on the pavement, prior to announcing the recently proposed legislation; and (ii) the rationale for the approach she opted for. [R]
answer_text: Following the public consultation on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and publication of the findings report, my officials explored a range of options to further address inconsiderate pavement parking, including vehicles parked partially on footways. These options were assessed against legal, and operational implications.I have decided to proceed with introducing a ban on parking a vehicle wholly on the footway, parking across dropped kerbs and double parking. This approach represents the most practical and effective solution at this stage, taking account of feasibility, enforcement considerations, and legislative vires. It is my aim to introduce these changes in the latter part of 2026, subject to the legislative process. These measures are another positive step to help further address inconsiderate pavement parking across the North, making our streets safer and more accessible, particularly for disabled people, older citizens and those with prams.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 34733/22–27, to detail the legislative provisions under which the PSNI operate when determining whether a vehicle, including one parked with two wheels on the pavement, is causing an obstruction. [R]
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the PSNI which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. However, I can advise that the legislative provision under which the PSNI operate when dealing with obstructions on a road is the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993. You may wish to direct your question to the PSNI or the Department for Infrastructure for a more detailed response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has issued guidance to opticians and dental practices on the operation of the the Health and Social Care (Exemptions from Charges, Payments and Remission of Charges) (Amendment and Transitional Provision) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025, including the identification of eligible patients and the handling of evidence and claims.
answer_text: An initial letter explaining the forthcoming changes to Help with Health Costs was issued to all Dental and Ophthalmic contractors on 5 November.A further letter with additional information, including the identification of eligible patients and the handling of evidence and claims was then issued on 28 November, prior to the changes coming into effect on 1 December.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how patients will be required to evidence entitlement under the Health and Social Care (Exemptions from Charges, Payments and Remission of Charges) (Amendment and Transitional Provision) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025; (ii) any support that will be available for patients who may have difficulty providing evidence; and (iii) any action his Department is taking to promote the new arrangements and ensure that individuals are made aware of their eligibility and requirements.
answer_text: Universal Credit (UC) recipients that fall within the thresholds for automatic passporting to the Help with Health Costs scheme can show their UC award notice or their UC statement, either a hard copy or digital format on their phone or device, as proof of entitlement to help with health costs.The delivery of the Help with Health Costs scheme rests with the Department for Communities. My officials have worked closely with the Department for Communities to ensure that relevant operational processes in addition to external and internal guidance have been updated to facilitate a smooth rollout of the new policy. On 5 November 2025 I issued a press release in relation to the changes to the Help With Health Cost scheme which came into effect on 1 December 2025.In addition, updated public-facing guidance has been sent to all dental and ophthalmic contractors for display in practice. This guidance includes a link to the relevant section of the nidirect website which has been updated with advice and guidance to assist individuals with questions they may have on eligibility or entitlement to Help with Health Costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the policies implemented by his Department to tackle fuel poverty in each of the last five financial years.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the recently announced inconsiderate pavement parking legislation will be implemented; and (ii) her assessment on whether the legislation will effectively tackle all forms of pavement parking, including vehicles parked with two wheels on the pavement. [R]
answer_text: Following the public consultation on options to tackle inconsiderate pavement parking and publication of the findings report, my officials explored a range of options to further address inconsiderate pavement parking, including vehicles parked partially on footways. These options were assessed against legal, and operational implications.I have decided to proceed with introducing a ban on parking a vehicle wholly on the footway, parking across dropped kerbs and double parking. This approach represents the most practical and effective solution at this stage, taking account of feasibility, enforcement considerations, and legislative vires. It is my aim to introduce these changes in the latter part of 2026, subject to the legislative process. These measures are another positive step to help further address inconsiderate pavement parking across the North, making our streets safer and more accessible, particularly for disabled people, older citizens and those with prams.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what constitutes, the very exceptional circumstances that would trigger a review of the new benchmark costs and Housing Association Grant rates, announced in his Social Dousing Development statement to the Assembly on 18 November 2025.
answer_text: Exceptional circumstances are any unforeseen, unmitigated events beyond our control which render the continuance of the Social Housing Development Programme unviable. My officials will continue to keep the benchmark costs under review. If circumstances change, I will reassess the situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether Housing Associations will not be required to revise or increase rents as a direct consequence of the changes to Housing Association grant rates.
answer_text: My Department has no legislative power to control rent increases applied by Registered Housing Associations (RHAs). It is a matter for individual Housing Association’s Boards to determine appropriate rent levels and to consider and approve any rent increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any meetings and engagements he had with Housing Associations, that were directly related to the announced changes to Housing Association grant rates, benchmark costs and associated programme changes.
answer_text: I met the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations (NIFHA), housing association representatives and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in late October to give them advance notice of the changes that I have announced. At that meeting, I advised the sector of the new indicative benchmark costs and grant rates for new build social homes that will be effective from 1 December 2025 to the end of March 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the number of additional new social homes expected to be delivered as a direct result of the changes to Housing Association grant rates.
answer_text: The changes in the Housing Association Grant level will make my budget allocation stretch further and enable us to build more homes with the funding that is available. A 2.8% cut in the average grant paid per unit will allow that 2.8% to be spent on the construction of new homes. On a 2,000-unit programme, the change to the grant could allow an extra 50-60 homes to be built, depending on size and location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made on the current borrowing capacity of Housing Associations.
answer_text: Answer	The Department, as Regulator of Registered Housing Associations (RHAs), carries out annual regulatory assessments on individual associations to determine their compliance with the Regulatory Framework.  		Finance Standard 1 requires RHAs to manage their resources effectively to ensure financial viability is maintained in the short, medium, and longer term. 	It is for each individual RHA to decide themselves how much private finance they can borrow and how much risk they take on.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any engagement he has had with Treasury in relation to the Autumn Budget 2025.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of current staffing levels across the Civil Service; and (ii) whether these levels are sufficient to meet departmental demands.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of individual departments and their Accounting Officers to assess and determine their staffing levels and resource requirements, considering their specific priorities and the budget available to them. DoF has issued Strategic Workforce Planning guidance outlining a six-stage process to ensure consistent, evidence-based workforce planning across all departments. DoF’s Workforce Data and Reporting Team provides further support through monthly dashboards on key workforce metrics, with official statistics published by NISRA: the latest NISRA employment statistics are available online.When a department identifies a vacant post that it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise resourcing activity within allocated financial and staff resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of staff who have left the Civil Service; and (ii) any analysis that has been carried out on reasons for leaving, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The number of staff who have left the Civil Service in each of the last five years, along with analysis of reasons for leaving, is published in Figure 9 of the Personnel Statistics tables for 2020, 2021, 2022, 2023, and 2024: NICS Personnel Statistics - Tables.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans in place to improve workforce planning across the Civil Service, to ensure Departments are adequately resourced for future needs.
answer_text: Workforce planning is a key initiative under the People Strategy: a vision for the implementation of Strategic Workforce Planning (SWP) was agreed by the Civil Service Board in spring 2025. To improve workforce planning across the Civil Service, my Department has issued a Strategic Workforce Planning Guide and associated workbook.  My Department also facilitates a SWP Community of Practice to provide an informal, collaborative space for departments to share workforce planning practices, discuss challenges, and support the implementation of SWP across the Civil Service.  Further, my Department also has a SWP Working Group which is creating a cohesive, integrated approach to workforce planning across Civil Service, including the design of SWP module on the new Finance and HR system, Integr8.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any progress that has been made across the Civil Service in improving performance management since the publication of the 2019 Northern Ireland Audit Office report regarding Capacity and Capability in the Northern Ireland Civil Service; and (ii) any assessment that has been made of current staff performance across all Departments.
answer_text: The Civil Service Performance Management Policy sets out the policy framework for effective performance management, through annual objectives setting, in-year reviews and end-of-year assessments.  The policy enables line managers to provide consistent, timely and relevant feedback to staff throughout the reporting year, extending beyond the mandatory in-year and end of year reviews.Since 2019, policy improvements specifically designed to increase capacity and capability have empowered line managers with the authority to formally manage unsatisfactory performance. Improvements in how well unsatisfactory performance is managed are evidenced by a 10% increase in staff engagement scores across the Civil Service since 2019, when the authority for decision making changed. Alongside this, a performance management learning toolkit has been made available to all staff to support effective performance management; and a revised framework for Permanent Secretary Performance Management put in place.   Departments have access to management information on performance management which enables business areas to assess compliance.The Performance Management policy is currently being reviewed as part of a HR policy renewal programme being delivered under the 2025-30 Civil Service People Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any skills shortages have been identified within the Civil Service.
answer_text: The Department of Finance assists Civil Service Departments in determining the skills necessary to deliver their services and meet priorities by offering corporate strategies, frameworks, tools and training and development, including:NICSHR’s 2024 high-level generic Strategic Learning Needs Analysis identified priority gaps in leadership, communication, people management, programme and project delivery, and policy skills. Emerging needs include change management, strategic thinking, empowering leadership, critical thinking, and data and technology capability. Departments are being engaged to address these gaps.NICSHR Learning and Development offers a comprehensive, blended suite of programmes aligned to six core areas: Leadership and Management, Policy and Government, Collaboration and Collective Working, Innovation and Transformation, Health and Wellbeing and Digital Skills.Issuing guidance on strategic workforce planning.Developing and implementing a recruitment and retention allowance policy to attract and retain specialist skills in the Civil Service.Further work is underway to strengthen capability across the Civil Service including:A Job Families Framework which will define clear job families, categories and career frameworks to support effective skills matching and career development.A Professions Framework which will require each profession to assess current and future capability needs on a regular basis, as well has upholding professional standards, continuing professional development and supporting career progression through ongoing review and targeted development activity.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the passage of the Assembly motion Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, whether they will appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer within their Department. [R]
answer_text: There is no agreement to appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer in TEO.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the passage of the Assembly motion Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, whether she will appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer within her Department. [R]
answer_text: I support the armed forces and veterans’ community and commitments such as those set out in the Armed Forces Covenant. I recognise the importance of fair treatment of those who have given service, and of ensuring that they do not face a disadvantage in accessing support, healthcare, housing, and education. The transition to civilian life can be difficult, involving a loss of identity and purpose. This can sometimes create a vulnerability in respect of mental and physical health issues, which can also give rise to housing instability. I fully support any efforts made to tackle these challenges. It is not clear what role the appointment of an Armed Forces Liaison Officer would have in relation to justice issues, given there are no obvious policy areas within my Department that are specifically relevant to assist the transition to civilian life.  However, I will be meeting the Veterans Commissioner soon and am happy to explore with him whether there are areas within Justice that would benefit from such an Officer. Where my Department can assist with any aspect of the provision of support, I will of course do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the passage of the Assembly motion Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, whether he will appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer within his Department. [R]
answer_text: This matter is under consideration in my department. My officials are exploring  whether there are areas within my purview that would benefit from such an Officer.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the passage of the Assembly motion Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, whether he will appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer within his Department. [R]
answer_text: I have no plans to appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the passage of the Assembly motion Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, whether she will appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer within her Department. [R]
answer_text: While we do not currently have plans to appoint a dedicated Armed Forces Liaison Officer, the Department remains committed to ensuring that all our policies and programmes are inclusive and responsive to the needs of everyone in our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the passage of the Assembly motion Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, whether she will appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer within her Department. [R]
answer_text: At present, my Department does not have plans to appoint a dedicated Armed Forces Liaison Officer. However, we will continue to deliver our services for the benefit of everyone living, working and visiting here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the passage of the Assembly motion Supporting Service Leavers, Veterans and their Families on 4 November 2025, whether he will appoint an Armed Forces Liaison Officer within his Department. [R]
answer_text: I very much welcomed the passage of the motion on 4 November 2025. Veterans’ health and important wellbeing needs span a range of policy responsibilities within my Department.  My Department hosts an Armed Forces Liaison Forum which enables representative groups to discuss and raise issues with relevant policy leads and with Health and Social Care Trusts. I will ask that the issue of adequate liaison be raised at the next scheduled meeting of the Forum.  I have also met recently with the Northern Ireland Veterans Commissioner and the charity Help for Heroes and am supportive of their aim to establish closer engagement with Trusts to ensure that veterans do not face disadvantage in terms of access to the services they require.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether, in light of the successful passage of the Assembly motion in support of service leavers, veterans, and their families through the Armed Forces Covenant, he will appoint an armed forces liaison officer within his department. [R]
answer_text: While the Department does not have a designated Armed Forces Liaison Officer, a Senior Official within the Department holds policy responsibility for Children of Service Personnel, including oversight of the implementation of the Armed Forces Covenant, within their broader remit.I am acutely aware of the unique and often complex challenges faced by children from Armed Forces families. These can include frequent relocations, disruptions to schooling, and the emotional impact of parental deployment. Such factors can significantly affect a child’s educational experience and outcomes. I am committed to ensuring that both the Department and the Education Authority do all that they can to support these children in accessing high-quality education and in fulfilling their potential.Officials will shortly be submitting proposals concerning the implementation of the Armed Forces Covenant in areas that fall within my remit. I expect to be able to share further information in due course regarding the Department’s support measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether any guidance has been issued to local councils regarding the assessment of applications for Certificates of Lawful Existing Use or Development, relating to houses of multiple occupation (HMO) use; and (ii) how these decisions take account of (a) community impact; and (b) local HMO policy.
answer_text: Councils are responsible for assessing planning applications for HMO use and their impact, taking account of their Local Development Plan (LDP) policies, regional policy, advice from statutory consultees, public representations, and other material planning considerations. My Department has published guidance on plan making for LDPs and the processing of applications for development which is available to councils, however, to date no guidance has issued regarding HMOs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any assessment he has made of the number of properties currently operating as Houses in Multiple Occupation under Certificates of Lawful Existing Use or Development; and (ii) whether these are included when councils apply the over provision test under the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: I have not made any assessment of the number of licensed Houses in Multiple Occupation operating under Certificates of Lawful Existing Use or Development. Planning is an issue which is outside of my Department’s remit.All existing licensed Houses in Multiple Occupation in an area are included when considering whether granting a new licence for a House in Multiple Occupation would result in overprovision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment on whether the existing legislation is adequate to tackle the obstruction of footpaths, caused by vehicles parked with two wheels on the pavement; and (ii) to detail (a) the relevant passages of legislation that apply in such circumstances; and (b) the enforcement measures currently available to address such obstructions. [R]
answer_text: Under The Road Traffic Regulation (NI) Order 1997, my Department has several powers to prohibit or restrict parking in the North. In addition, The Roads (NI) Order 1993 and The Road Traffic (NI) Order 1995 provide the Department with powers relating to offences of parking and obstruction. The Traffic Management (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 also provides powers to issue Penalty Charge Notices for any contravention listed within the Order. The introduction of the Footways (Prohibition of Waiting) Order (Northern Ireland) 2023 has provided my Department with additional powers to enforce against vehicles found parked on footways at certain locations and this legislation has been a positive step forward in deterring inconsiderate pavement parking. However, my Department cannot take enforcement action against vehicles parked on a pavement and causing an obstruction where there is no parking restrictions marked on the carriageway; this is a matter for the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether, in light of the successful passage of the Assembly motion in support of service leavers, veterans, and their families through the Armed Forces Covenant, he will appoint an armed forces liaison officer within his department. [R]

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the number of occasions during the 2022–2027 Assembly mandate that a proposed Executive agenda item has not received consensus approval to be placed on an Executive meeting agenda.
answer_text: There have been 48 meetings of the Executive Committee since May 2022. On each of these occasions an agenda has been prepared from the proposals put forward by Ministers.  A decision not to include a particular matter may be for a variety of reasons unrelated to the absence of joint agreement between the First Minister and the deputy First Minister.  As it is not possible to identify the particular circumstances relating to each paper considered for these meetings, it is therefore not possible to respond to the question in the terms in which it is asked.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has made any assessment of the impact of Planning Appeals Commission decisions to overturn houses in multiple occupation refusals, on community balance and public confidence in the licensing regime.
answer_text: Planning considerations are a matter for local councils and the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has reviewed recent cases where council refusals for houses of multiple occupation were overturned on appeal.
answer_text: My Department has not reviewed decisions on houses in multiple occupation, these are matters for local councils and the relevant appeal body.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of houses of multiple occupancy related planning applications declined by local councils, that have been overturned by the Planning Appeals Commission, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: This question was transferred to my office as the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) is an independent body under the sponsorship of the Department of Justice.I am advised by the PAC that there have been 7 appeals of local council decisions in relation to houses of multiple occupancy, overturned by the PAC, in the last 5 years.The PAC has provided a breakdown of the appeals allowed, below, by case reference (including year) and by planning authority/local council area.2021/A0059	Belfast City Council2022/A0197	Belfast City Council2023/A0077	Derry City & Strabane District Council2024/A0044	Belfast City Council2024/A0045	Belfast City Council2024/A0137	Belfast City Council2025/A0025	Belfast City Council

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any engagement he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure, and the Planning Appeals Commission, regarding the overturning of council refusals for houses in multiple occupation applications, on appeal; and (ii) how such decisions align with the intent of the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: I have not had any engagement with the Minister for Infrastructure or the Planning Appeals Commission regarding houses in multiple occupation (HMOs). The Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 sets out that any new applications for HMOs must have the relevant planning permission in place. Planning policies are matters for local councils, and therefore decisions about applications and appeals are not for my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the consideration given by the Planning Appeals Commission to local houses of multiple occupancy policy; and (ii) the over-provision test, when determining appeals against local council refusals.
answer_text: This question was transferred to my office as the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) is an independent body under the sponsorship of the Department of Justice.The operation and decisions of the PAC are the responsibility of the Chief Commissioner; however, I am advised that the PAC considers appeals in the evidential context. A Commissioner will consider any reference to policy or over-provision as argued in the appeal papers as presented. The Commissioner's reasoning in respect of any decision is explained in their decision letter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider legislative, or policy, changes to enable local councils to restrict new houses in multiple occupation applications, in areas already deemed saturated.
answer_text: I am not considering any legislative or policy changes. As I detailed in my statement to the Assembly, the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 already specifies that when councils are considering new applications for HMOs, a council may only grant an HMO licence only if it is satisfied of certain criteria, for example, whether the granting of a licence would result in overprovision. This means that when considering an application for a new HMO, councils must have regard to the number of HMOs already in the locality.		As I also highlighted to the Assembly, councils can have their own specific policies about HMO provision and set restrictions on numbers or percentages of HMOs in certain areas. For instance, Belfast City Council’s Local Development Plan has caps for HMOs and other high-density housing, such as, flats and apartments, with the aim of helping to facilitate sustainable housing growth. 	I have written to all council Chief Executives to remind them of council responsibilities for HMOs and the powers they have available to them to manage this type of housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on actions being taken to maximise the use of publicly owned land for the development of social and affordable housing.
answer_text: I will be circulating an Executive paper in due course, setting out a new approach for making better use of publicly owned land for affordable housing.Alongside this, my Department launched a three-year pilot with the Housing Executive, called Housing Executive Land Acquisition (HELA), which aims to identify sites which can provide sufficient land to deliver 100 units in areas of acute housing need.  The pilot aims to identify and acquire land primarily in public ownership to increase the portion of Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) developed on public sector land.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to strengthen coordination between the Department for Communities, the Department for Infrastructure, and Belfast City Council in addressing clustering of houses in multiple occupation.
answer_text: Consideration on whether to grant a licence for a house in multiple occupation (HMO) is a matter for councils. The Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 already stipulates that councils must have regard to the number of HMOs already in the locality and ensure the relevant planning permission is in place.	Councils can also have their own specific policies about HMO provision and set restrictions on numbers or percentages of HMOs in certain areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any mechanisms in place to monitor and evaluate the social and housing impacts of high concentrations of houses in multiple occupation, on local communities.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Houses in Multiple Occupation (NIHMO) unit, based in Belfast City Council hosts a monitoring group meeting every 6 months to discuss relevant issues involving the management of HMOs. This meeting is attended by representatives from those councils with higher numbers of HMOs, departmental officials and other stakeholders, such as, the NI Fire and Rescue Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the proposed commencement date for the Health and Social Care (Exemptions from Charges, Payments and Remission of Charges) (Amendment and Transitional Provision) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 as discussed when officials briefed the Committee for Health on 11 September 2025.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment he has made of the effectiveness of the current Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, and associated regulations in managing houses in multiple occupation density, and community impact.
answer_text: I have not made any specific assessment of the effectiveness of the current Act in relation to these matters. However, as I detailed in my statement to the Assembly, councils have significant powers under the Act to manage issues associated with this type of accommodation. Specifically, in relation to the density of houses in multiple occupation (HMOs), in considering all new applications for licences the council must take account of the number of HMOs already in the area and the need for this type of accommodation. Councils have the powers to set limits or caps on the numbers of HMOs through their local development plans.The Act provides a stringent legislative framework to ensure any negative effects on local areas can be managed. These include the need for robust anti-social behaviour management plans, and licence conditions impose restrictions on numbers of tenants. There are 21 offences within the Act, these include: operating an unlicensed HMO, breaching licence conditions (for example, failing to take reasonable steps to prevent anti-social behaviour), overcrowding and using an HMO subject to a hazard notice. These can carry fixed penalties of £5,000 and fines of up to £20,000.I have written to council Chief Executives to remind them of council responsibilities for HMOs and the extensive powers they have to manage this type of housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any (i) recent representations; or (ii) data, his Department has received indicating an increase in the number of houses in multiple occupation within East Belfast; and (iii) action he plans to take to address community concerns about overconcentration.
answer_text: My Department has not received any representations which specifically detail an increase of houses in multiple occupation in East Belfast. I did however receive correspondence from Joanne Bunting in September which highlighted the growing concerns of local residents about the impact that high concentrations of HMOs were/are having on the character of their communities, and that current policy may not be sufficient to prevent further clustering of this type of housing in areas already experiencing significant pressures. My Department does not hold any data indicating an increase in houses in multiple occupation in East Belfast.It is the responsibility of Belfast City Council to ensure that any new licences granted for houses in multiple occupation would not result in overprovision in the East Belfast area.  I have written to all council Chief Executives to remind them of council responsibilities for HMOs and the extensive powers they have available to them to manage this type of housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department plans to review the existing houses in multiple occupation licensing framework to determine if it remains fit for purpose.
answer_text: A review of the HMO Act was conducted in 2021. The findings highlighted mainly operational issues and teething problems with the administration of the Scheme, including issues with the IT system and the impact of the lockdown on services. A few minor legislative changes were suggested, such as amending timescales for licence transfers on the death of a licence holder and permitting fees for temporary exemption notices. I have no plans to undertake a further review to assess the effectiveness of the HMO Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department is working with Belfast City Council to ensure that the licensing and approval of houses in multiple occupation does not lead to oversaturation.
answer_text: My Department is not involved in making decisions on licences for houses in multiple occupation. As detailed in my stated to the Assembly, the Houses in Multiple Occupation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides councils with a key statutory tool to prevent future over concentrations of HMOs through the overprovision test. This means that when considering an application for a new HMO, councils must be satisfied that granting the licence will not result in overprovision of this type of accommodation in an area. Councils can have their own specific policies about HMO provision and set restrictions on numbers or percentages of HMOs in certain areas.I have written to all council Chief Executives to remind them of council responsibilities for HMOs and the extensive powers they have to manage this type of housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the current concentration of houses in multiple occupation, across East Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has not made any assessment of the number of houses in multiple occupation (HMOs) across East Belfast. My Department receives data on HMOs broken down by local council area and not on a constituency level.	Councils when deciding whether to grant a new HMO licence must consider whether this would result in the over provision of this type of accommodation in the area. Moreover, as detailed in my statement to the Assembly, it is ultimately up to each council to develop and implement their own HMO policies, including those to set a limit on the percentage of HMOs in a certain area.	I have written to all council Chief Executives to remind them of council responsibilities for HMOs and the extensive powers they have to manage this type of housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when the Health and Social Care (Exemptions from Charges, Payments and Remission of Charges) (Amendment and Transitional Provision) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 will be implemented.

To ask the Minister of Health why the period a patient waits to see a consultant before being placed on a surgical waiting list cannot be taken into account as part of the eligibility under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme mirrors both the previous EU Cross Border and Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Schemes. The current initiative is targeting long terms waits for treatment and therefore the parameters of the scheme have been set to reflect this.  The waiting time is based on the timeframe a patient has been assessed by an HSC consultant and boarded for surgery. Whilst I fully appreciate the frustration for those who do not meet the eligibility criteria, there is no current plan to amend the criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31049/22-27, to detail any steps, beyond the waiting list reimbursement scheme, his Department is taking to reduce the waiting list.
answer_text: The Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan details how the £215m ringfenced funding will be invested across different areas to support waiting list reduction and build core capacity. These initiatives include increasing in-house and IS capacity to target some of the longest waiting patients, expanding trauma capacity to reduce the risk of elective cancellations, investing in mega clinics, increasing HSC training places and expanding pre-operative assessment capacity to help improve theatre throughput. These measures are designed to address the pressing challenges currently facing healthcare waiting lists in Northern Ireland.I acknowledge that the current waiting times are unacceptable. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Fuel Poverty Strategy.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he will expand community-based eye care services, such as (i) PEARS Plus; (ii) glaucoma management; and (iii) urgent care pathway, in order to cut waiting lists and reduce preventable sight loss.
answer_text: Primary care optometrists play a vital role in inclusive healthcare delivery, helping people to see, within the prevention and early-intervention arena, and in delivering care closer to home; care that would have traditionally been delivered in the acute hospital setting.The Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service (NI PEARS) manages non-sight-threatening eye conditions in almost all community optometry practices, freeing capacity in general practice and unscheduled Emergency Department and eye casualty settings. Outcomes, and experience measures are sustainably high for the > 4,000 patients who access the service each month.NI PEARS Plus, which offers the opportunity to build on the NI PEARS service by using the extended skills of a network of suitably-qualified non-medical (independent) optometrist prescribers, has the potential to build on the service offering. Work is ongoing within my Department to develop the regional ophthalmic commissioning priorities. NI PEARS Plus is included in considerations, forming part of the wider commissioning capacity building piece. The Community Optometry Ocular Hypertension (OHT) Monitoring Scheme has been in place since 2021 and has seen over 3,000 patients discharged from hospital to community review, freeing capacity in secondary care. This is long-term care and monitoring, with the 3,000+ patients receiving annual review in community over the course of the lifetime of this long-term condition. Taken together with regional enhanced services around repeat measures (LES I) and enhanced case finding (LES II) these commissioned services are already having a positive impact at both managing patient demand, and reducing waiting lists within the glaucoma pathway.The community optometry cataract post-operative review service similarly offers the potential to free over 4,000 eye department appointments each year.The UK General Optical Council health regulator (GOC) Public Perception survey shows high levels of user satisfaction (91%) with the optometry services being offered in Northern Ireland (and confidence in receiving a high standard of care – 93%). That 44% of people surveyed in Northern Ireland would consult an optometrist with an acute eye problem is good. This demonstrates that investment in NI PEARS has created safe, accessible, effective and cost-effective acute eye care, treating people closer to home, and freeing capacity in general practice and ED. These positive developments have been welcomed by patients, by the profession, and by the system, and my Department is committed to continuing this support and enablement.While it is clear that these services are working well, consideration will always be given to opportunities to enhance or improve services however, any decisions will need to be made in face of the £600m deficit for my Department which limits our ability to make the investments we would all like to see.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department intends to take to support investment in the training and professional development of optometrists.
answer_text: Optometry contractors have, to date, embraced reform and pathway reconfiguration, adopting new models of care to treat and manage more people out of the hospital setting and closer to home and hope to create an operating environment that both supports and facilitates community optometry to continue this journey, and indeed to be integral partners in delivering a viable Neighbourhood Model.Having an engaged, motivated, and integral optometry workforce is key to this and that the skills and training to undertake both traditional roles, and to embrace extended roles around new models of care are also essential.Tangible and practical support for the training and professional development for Northern Ireland optometrists is multifactorial, comprising: an individual continuous professional development (CPD) grant of £605 per optometrist (total funding £395k in 2024/25), provision of a CPD programme of online CPD events, tailored to reflect NI strategic priorities around elective care, demand-management, and community-based care. Fifteen of these sessions were provided in 2024/25,funded access to UK-wide Directorate of Optometric Continuing Education and Training (DOCET) resources for all NI optometrists and,investment in clinical placements for optometry independent prescribers.However, I recognise that, with the Regulator changing how and where training is being delivered, more support may be required to ensure that our optometrists are equipped and facilitated to deliver 21st century eyecare.My officials are considering what opportunities may exist for primary care officials to expand their training and skills.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the additional resources required to support a long-term, sustainable model of community-based optometry.
answer_text: Due to the strong position of General Ophthalmic Services (GOS) in Primary Care, it’s not felt that a strategic reset is required. I’m aware of the excellent work that Primary Care Optometry is doing in the realm of neighbourhood healthcare delivery through the NIPEARS, NIPEARS plus, glaucoma monitoring and post op cataract schemes.These schemes demonstrate how a Neighbourhood Model can work for delivering more eyecare closer to home, outside of hospital, where it is safe and appropriate to do so.Even with a service that is working well, consideration will always be given to opportunities to enhance or improve services however, any decisions will need to be made in face of the £600m deficit for my Department which limits our ability to make the investments we would all like to see.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment has been made of the impact that moving more eyecare into the community would have on reducing waiting lists and preventing avoidable sight loss.
answer_text: Primary care optometrists play a vital role in inclusive healthcare delivery, helping people to see, within the prevention and early-intervention arena, and in delivering care closer to home; care that would have traditionally been delivered in the acute hospital setting.The Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service (NI PEARS) manages non-sight-threatening eye conditions in almost all community optometry practices, freeing capacity in general practice and unscheduled Emergency Department and eye casualty settings. Outcomes, and experience measures are sustainably high for the > 4,000 patients who access the service each month.NI PEARS Plus, which offers the opportunity to build on the NI PEARS service by using the extended skills of a network of suitably-qualified non-medical (independent) optometrist prescribers, has the potential to build on the service offering. Work is ongoing within my Department to develop the regional ophthalmic commissioning priorities. NI PEARS Plus is included in considerations, forming part of the wider commissioning capacity building piece. The Community Optometry Ocular Hypertension (OHT) Monitoring Scheme has been in place since 2021 and has seen over 3,000 patients discharged from hospital to community review, freeing capacity in secondary care. This is long-term care and monitoring, with the 3,000+ patients receiving annual review in community over the course of the lifetime of this long-term condition. Taken together with regional enhanced services around repeat measures (LES I) and enhanced case finding (LES II) these commissioned services are already having a positive impact at both managing patient demand, and reducing waiting lists within the glaucoma pathway.The community optometry cataract post-operative review service similarly offers the potential to free over 4,000 eye department appointments each year.The UK General Optical Council health regulator (GOC) Public Perception survey shows high levels of user satisfaction (91%) with the optometry services being offered in Northern Ireland (and confidence in receiving a high standard of care – 93%). That 44% of people surveyed in Northern Ireland would consult an optometrist with an acute eye problem is good. This demonstrates that investment in NI PEARS has created safe, accessible, effective and cost-effective acute eye care, treating people closer to home, and freeing capacity in general practice and ED. These positive developments have been welcomed by patients, by the profession, and by the system, and my Department is committed to continuing this support and enablement.While it is clear that these services are working well, consideration will always be given to opportunities to enhance or improve services however, any decisions will need to be made in face of the £600m deficit for my Department which limits our ability to make the investments we would all like to see.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that optometry contracts reflect the true cost of delivering high-quality eyecare, while also helping reduce pressures on secondary care.
answer_text: Making sure people can access high quality eyecare is a priority for me.  I recently announced the additional funding of £1m to help support with the increased cost of the employer National Insurance contributions, along with the 6% uplift to all fees, allowances, and enhanced services for 2024/25.  I have also approved a 4% uplift for 2025/26 subject to funding being made available.My Department is working to strengthen primary care delivery, including optometry, by bringing services closer to communities through a neighbourhood care approach. This aims to improve access and help reduce pressures on secondary care.The three-year strategic plan (2024-2027) and the 2025 Reset Plan outline efforts to invest in primary and community care. These investments implicitly support high-quality optometry services by:Funding early intervention and primary care capacity.Supporting workforce sustainability in clinical services, which includes optometrists.Integrating digital tools and care systems for better assessment and referral pathways, potentially easing pressures on ophthalmology and secondary services.I trust you’ll agree that these measures illustrate that my Department continues to value General Ophthalmic Services (GOS), and will continue to look at new ways to support and improve Optometry in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently waiting for knee surgery, broken down by length of time on the surgical waiting list.
answer_text: Regionally at the end of August 2025, 5,787 patients were waiting for primary knee surgery. The breakdown of these by time band is provided in Table 1 below.Table 1: August 2025 figures for Primary Knee Surgery waits by Timeband<=13 wks>13-52wks>52wksTotal Waiting500152437635,787Source: Elective Care Framework Monitoring figuresA revised Elective Care Framework was published on 24 May 2024, setting out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times.  The framework includes the allocation of £40m non-recurrent funding to help address some of the longest waiting procedures, including patients waiting for primary knee surgery.  In addition, the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, which was launched in June 2025, has been expanded to include patients waiting over one year for surgery and patients can now also use health care providers across the EU, not just UK providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the number of complaints received by the Northern Ireland Trading Standards Service in each of the last five years.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the number of people that have been supported by the Victims and Survivors Service in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any monitoring in place to ensure providers delivering the Waiting list Reimbursement Scheme treatments meet clinical and regulatory standards equivalent to those in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This is a patient led scheme, in choosing to access healthcare in the Republic of Ireland and European Union, patients are effectively stepping outside of the HSC system and using their rights to claim reimbursement under this temporary cross border scheme.  At this point, the patient is taking individual responsibility for ensuring that the service they obtain is appropriate and safe within the laws of the Republic of Ireland and European Union for treatment (not under UK legislation). The Department is not formally commissioning services from providers in the Republic of Ireland or wider European Union and therefore will not be liable for the outcome of the treatment provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any discussions he has had with Executive colleagues to ensure cross-party support for Northern Ireland’s inclusion in the UK's 2035 FIFA Women’s World Cup bid; and (ii) whether this has been communicated through official channels.
answer_text: The Minister for the Economy recently tabled an Executive paper regarding the 2035 FIFA Women’s World Cup. I will engage with my colleagues in the Executive to ensure cross-party support for the inclusion of Northern Ireland in the bid, however I consider the matter is primarily the responsibility of both the Minister for the Economy and her arm’s length body, Tourism NI.As I stated in my response to AQW 30829/22-27 and AQW 30831/22-27, I do expect my Department and I will have a critical role to play in any successful bid and that we will provide the necessary support when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for the Economy on maximising the potential tourism and economic impact of a successful UK bid to host the 2035 FIFA Women’s World Cup.
answer_text: The Minister for the Economy recently tabled an Executive paper regarding the 2035 FIFA Women’s World Cup. I look forward to engaging with the Minister for the Economy as plans are developed, however I consider the matter is primarily the responsibility of both the Minister for the Economy and her arm’s length body, Tourism NI.As I stated in my response to AQW 30829/22-27 and AQW 30832/22-27, I do expect my Department, and I will have a critical role to play in any successful bid and that we will provide the necessary support when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will publish monthly data on the number of applications received, approved, and rejected under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, broken down by procedure type and waiting time.
answer_text: Since its launch the operational management of the Scheme is carried out through the Department of Health’s, Strategic Planning and Performance Group. The SPPG Waiting List Reimbursement team provide weekly monitoring to my Department.  Uptake measurement and reporting is carried out through a Power BI dashboard. It provides an interactive, up-to-date overview of current volumes, geographical spread, application volume and status, speciality, procedure type and financial committal at a glance to inform scheme delivery and decision making. The decision to publish the data will be a decision I will review over time, once proper implementation of the first and second phase of the scheme has been established.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with his Government counterparts regarding the UK bid to host the 2035 FIFA Women’s World Cup.
answer_text: The hosting of the FIFA Women’s World Cup 2035 is primarily a matter for the Minister for the Economy and her arm’s length body Tourism NI, and I have not yet had any related engagement with my counterparts in the Department for Culture, Media and Sport.I expect that my Department will have a role to play in planning for the event itself and will provide the necessary support where required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time (i) from GP referral to consultant outpatient appointment; and (ii) from consultant appointment to being added to a surgical waiting list.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for general surgery are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.The Northern Ireland Outpatient Waiting Time is published on the Department’s website and provides patients with average waiting time information by Health and Social Care Trust and by Specialty across Northern Ireland. This information varies between Trusts, specialisms and reporting time frame.  The majority of referrals are from a GP; however, some referrals may also be received from other sources.I am unable to provide the information in relation to the average waiting time from consultant appointment to being added to a surgical waiting list at present.  This information is currently not readily available due to ongoing data validation work being completed by Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of applications to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme that have been (i) received; (ii) approved; and (iii) rejected since its launch in June 2025.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently on surgical waiting lists who have waited more than two years for a first consultant appointment before being added to the waiting list.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for general surgery are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.I am unable to detail the number of patients currently on surgical waiting lists who have waited more than two years for a first consultant appointment before being added to the waiting list at present.  This information varies between Trusts, specialisms and reporting time frame and is currently not readily available due to ongoing data validation work being completed by Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, what consideration has been given to introducing a mechanism to cover treatment costs upfront for patients who cannot afford to self-fund before reimbursement.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which allowed patients to get reimbursed for healthcare treatment outside Northern Ireland and GB.  As I outlined in my statement there is other work ongoing with Independent Sector Providers in Northern Ireland as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs. This scheme has been established as a reimbursement scheme similar to that of the Republic of Ireland reimbursement Scheme and the European Directive for Cross Border treatment.In respect of your request for some information in relation to uptake, since the Scheme launched on 2 June 2025 until 15 September 2025, there has been a total of 331 applications received; 243 of those applications have been successfully prior approved. The other 97 applications are pending due to not submitting the clinical evidence to support their application or where they do not meet the criteria. A budget of £10 million has been a to the scheme, of which £1.9 million has been committed to date.It is hoped that the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme will remove approximately 1400 patients who have been waiting longer than 104 weeks (2 years) or more for surgery from HSC surgical inpatient waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any estimate made of the number of patients who will benefit from the £10 million allocated to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which allowed patients to get reimbursed for healthcare treatment outside Northern Ireland and GB.  As I outlined in my statement there is other work ongoing with Independent Sector Providers in Northern Ireland as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs. This scheme has been established as a reimbursement scheme similar to that of the Republic of Ireland reimbursement Scheme and the European Directive for Cross Border treatment.In respect of your request for some information in relation to uptake, since the Scheme launched on 2 June 2025 until 15 September 2025, there has been a total of 331 applications received; 243 of those applications have been successfully prior approved. The other 97 applications are pending due to not submitting the clinical evidence to support their application or where they do not meet the criteria. A budget of £10 million has been a to the scheme, of which £1.9 million has been committed to date.It is hoped that the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme will remove approximately 1400 patients who have been waiting longer than 104 weeks (2 years) or more for surgery from HSC surgical inpatient waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department is ensuring consistency of shared care provision across all Health and Social Care Trust areas.
answer_text: Consistency of shared care provision, in terms of prescribing of medicines, across all Health and Social Care Trust areas is the remit of the Regional Group on Specialist Medicines supported and facilitated by the Interface Pharmacist Network for Specialist Medicines.
