This is a network of pharmacists established to provide cohesive care for patients in relation to specialist medicines, particularly at the interface between primary and secondary care.The Regional Group on Specialist Medicines membership comprises of the Department of Health, including the Strategic Planning & Performance Group, the five HSC Trusts, Local Medical Committees, the Northern Ireland General Practitioners Committee and Community Pharmacy NI.The focus of the Group is the safety aspects of prescribing, supply and administration of specialist medicines relative to their management in secondary care, primary care or jointly (shared care) within HSC Northern Ireland.  It is also responsible for:Ongoing review of local Red (hospital only) and Amber (shared care) medicines management arrangements and the utilisation of this categorisation to emerging therapies.Horizon scanning to identify emerging specialist medicines and undertake adjudication as a medicine comes to market.Informing a regional approach to the establishment and introduction of the Red/Amber list ensuring consistency across Northern Ireland.Recommending a process for the prescribing, dispensing and administration of specialist medicines into the service taking account of the need to ensure high quality patient care and the professional and medico-legal implication of any recommendations made.Considering, and commissioning as necessary, the development and review of shared care arrangements for specialist medicines classified as Amber.This helps ensure:• Safer systems, with the responsibility for prescribing specialised medicines sitting with the most appropriate practitioner.• Better communication between primary and secondary care leading to better working relationships and enhanced patient care.• Consistency in approach across Northern Ireland.Regional Shared Care Guidelines (SCGs) for a large number of medicines are available in Northern Ireland to support patient specific shared care arrangements.These regional SCGs follow a standard format and contain information on indications, dose, route of administration, adverse effects, monitoring requirements and responsibilities, and clinically relevant drug interactions. Regional SCGs are used across all HSC Trust areas in NI.

Their CAMHS provision will undertake ADHD assessments if required for those children and young people open to CAMHS with a co-morbid mental health condition. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how GPs and independent providers are supported to ensure smooth transfer of responsibility in shared care cases.
answer_text: The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultation.  The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications, including Red-Amber list drugs, to patients under their care.In particular, the limitations with regards to requests to GPs for transfer of ongoing prescribing of medication which for the patient’s GP may not be clinically acceptable.  This guidance has not changed.	To further facilitate appropriate shared care arrangements, my Department issued ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care in September 2024. This guidance (Microsoft Word - SPPG guidance - Request to prescribe following private consultations September 2024 FV) provides a reminder that the legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription, and it is the responsibility of the individual prescriber to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice.Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to prescribe, in instances where a GP considers that the medication being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) guidance on Good Medical Practice, then they may choose to prescribe.As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the systems his Department has in place to monitor shared care arrangements across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.  The decision to enter into a shared care agreement, or not, is therefore a clinical decision for individual GPs to make and as such, is not monitored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department has taken to develop shared care arrangements between primary, secondary and community care providers.
answer_text: Development of shared care arrangements and guidelines, in terms of prescribing of medicines, is the remit of the Regional Group on Specialist Medicines, supported and facilitated by the Interface Pharmacist Network for Specialist Medicines. This is a network of pharmacists established to provide cohesive care for patients in relation to specialist medicines, particularly at the interface between primary and secondary care.The Regional Group on Specialist Medicines membership comprises of the Department of Health, including the Strategic Planning & Performance Group, the five HSC Trusts, Local Medical Committees, the Northern Ireland General Practitioners Committee and Community Pharmacy NI.The focus of the Group is the safety aspects of prescribing, supply and administration of specialist medicines relative to their management in secondary care, primary care or jointly (shared care) within HSC Northern Ireland.  It is also responsible for:Ongoing review of local Red (hospital only) and Amber (shared care) medicines management arrangements and the utilisation of this categorisation to emerging therapies.Horizon scanning to identify emerging specialist medicines and undertake adjudication as a medicine comes to market.Informing a regional approach to the establishment and introduction of the Red/Amber list ensuring consistency across Northern Ireland.Recommending a process for the prescribing, dispensing and administration of specialist medicines into the service taking account of the need to ensure high quality patient care and the professional and medico-legal implication of any recommendations made.Considering, and commissioning as necessary, the development and review of shared care arrangements for specialist medicines classified as Amber.This helps ensure:• Safer systems, with the responsibility for prescribing specialised medicines sitting with the most appropriate practitioner.• Better communication between primary and secondary care leading to better working relationships and enhanced patient care.• Consistency in approach across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the buildings and land surplus to requirement within the ownership of his Department or arm's-length bodies.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline whether there are any buildings or land surplus to requirement within his Department’s ownership.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Carrier Bag Levy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average cost to repair a pothole.
answer_text: The cost for repairing potholes falls within the Department’s budget for Patching which, while including potholes, would also include areas of cracking, depressions etc. Also, there is no single method or technique used in the repair of surface defects and materials, specifications, quantities and methods vary from location to location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the (i) Job Start Scheme, launched in 2021 for 16 to 24 year olds; and (ii) Job Start 50+ Scheme, launched in 2024 for those aged 50 and above.
answer_text: I am very pleased with the outcomes from both the JobStart 16-24 Scheme and the Jobstart 50+ Scheme.77% of participants who completed their JobStart 16-24 opportunity moved into sustained employment following their participation on the Scheme. The JobStart 50+ Scheme was a “test and learn” pilot to explore the concept of this group entering or re-entering the workforce. I am pleased to advise that 52% of participants who completed their opportunity moved into sustained employment following their participation on the Scheme.   An independent evaluation of the JobStart 50+ Scheme is underway and is expected by end of July 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to reintroduce the (i) Job Start Scheme, launched in 2021 for 16 to 24 year olds; and (ii) Job Start 50+ Scheme, launched in 2024 for those aged 50 and above.
answer_text: I am in the process of agreeing my Department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support to help people of all ages to overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.  My Budget allocation plans will be announced in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the role of the Utility Regulator.
answer_text: Question 14 AQO 1823/22-27The Utility Regulator is an independent non-ministerial government department, governed by a Board of Directors and directly accountable to the Northern Ireland Assembly. It is largely funded through annual licence fees paid by the electricity and gas licensees and water and sewerage undertakers. Electricity and gas licensees recover this cost from their customers.The Utility Regulator is the independent economic regulator for electricity, gas and water in the north.  Its role in relation to electricity and gas is to ensure that the utility industries in the north are regulated and developed as set out in its statutory duties in various pieces of legislation including the Energy (Northern Ireland) Order 2003.In carrying out this role the Utility Regulator has a principal objective to protect electricity and gas consumers here and must have regard for a number of things, including, that the companies they regulate can finance their activities, vulnerable customers are protected, and the need to deliver a secure, diverse, viable and environmentally sustainable long term energy supply.As my Department develops new energy policies to meet its decarbonisation goals, including its contribution to statutory targets, each accompanying piece of new legislation will include any necessary statutory powers for the Utility Regulator to enable it to carry out any new regulatory duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the final iteration of any anti-poverty strategy will contain clearly defined measures by Department.
answer_text: The draft Anti-Poverty Strategy which I have presented to my Executive colleagues details indicators and measures which will be used to determine the effectiveness of departmental interventions and the Strategy overall. This will allow progress throughout the lifespan of the Strategy to be measured and allow further action to be targeted where necessary.Engagement with stakeholders and service users will take place throughout the implementation of the Strategy and consideration will be given to existing measures and indicators ensuring they remain relevant and appropriate. As an Executive Strategy, it will be for the Executive to agree which indicators and measures are included in the final iteration of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of staff working on an anti-poverty strategy in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: As the Department for Communities was established in 2016, staffing figures for the Poverty Policy Branch who have been working on the Executive’s Anti-poverty Strategy can only be reported back to that point. The table below contains the staffing levels from 2016 to present:YearNumber of staff2016320174201842019420204202142022420235202452025 (to date)5It should be noted that in 2024 and 2025 one staff member has been temporarily promoted to another team working on Social Inclusion Strategies. The member of staff remains on the staffing figures as they will return to Poverty Policy Branch when their temporary promotion ends.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support her Department can provide to (i) businesses; and (ii) employees impacted by the imminent closure of Connswater Shopping Centre.
answer_text: The closure of Connswater Shopping Centre is regrettable for the tenant businesses and the employees impacted. Many of the issues facing our retailers, including the former business tenants of Connswater Shopping Centre, are outside the remit of my Department or of the Executive, for example the impact of the British Government’s decisions on National Insurance Contributions in particular are having an impact on businesses across the north.  The Executive does not have the financial capacity to compensate for decisions made on reserved taxation matters. However, I am very aware of the real and pressing issues facing our retailers.  These concerns are being examined as part of a Cost of Doing Business research project which I commissioned in my previous capacity as Minister of Finance and which is being led by the University of Ulster’s Economic Policy Centre.  The outcomes of this research will assess the impact of increased costs and identify particular sectors that are at most risk.  My Department’s Careers Service is available to support anyone facing redundancy or who has been made unemployed as a result of the closure of Connswater Shopping Centre. Careers advisers can provide personalised job searching support including advice on how to transfer existing skills and experience to current job opportunities. Advisers also have access to up-to-date labour market information provided by the Department’s economists which they use with the client to inform careers guidance discussions about reskilling, training, apprenticeships further and higher education. This support is professional, free, specific and tailored to the needs of each individual, with the aim of supporting as many employees as possible back into employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support his Department can offer to charities located within Connswater Shopping Centre who are directly impacted by its imminent closure.
answer_text: My Department does not have any open funding streams to which charities located within Connswater Shopping Centre may currently apply.Please find attached a link to the Supporting Communities NI funding bulletin - a free, monthly resource providing details of open funding programmes from a wide range of funders which may be of use:https://supportingcommunities.org/funding-bulletin The Fundraising Advice Officer within NICVA can also provide advice on securing funding alongside providing details of available grant funding through the NICVA grant tracker.  Contact details can be accessed at this link: https://www.nicva.org/services/fundraising.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress to implement the changes needed to enable individuals, in receipt of Universal Credit, to be passported for assistance with health costs.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19490/22-27, to detail the steps his Department is taking to (i) improve the accuracy of the data received; and (ii) understand the condition of dwellings.
answer_text: The Department of Finance’s Land & Property Services (LPS) provide my Department with data in relation to housing stock properties recorded as vacant on the LPS rating system. In line with the statutory rating assumptions the internal repair and fit out of domestic property held by LPS is assumed to be average for all property. There is no issue with the accuracy of the data received in terms of its primary function, that being information required for rating purposes by LPS.		To establish a structure which would  investigate and survey the condition of every dwelling recorded as vacant by LPS is beyond the resource capabilities of my Department. 		My Department noted from the most recent data a very small increase in the recorded number of empty homes of 0.58%. A comparative analysis using figures from previous years demonstrates a constantly shifting position with an annual turnover of around 20% of properties recorded as vacant on the LPS rating system, which is likely to cause some fluctuations in the figures from year to year.		Given the small extent of this change, that comparative analysis makes it clear that many homes are recently vacated and will soon be occupied again, and that while for others no intervention available to the Department will bring them back into use (e.g. ongoing court proceedings).		A significant issue of note is the fragmentated nature of the vacant dwellings, with the majority situated in areas of low housing need and only 1.99% in areas of high housing need		To address housing challenges my Department published a new Housing Supply Strategy on 9th December to address the wider challenges facing housing supply. It recognises the need to consider actions around the use of existing buildings to support housing supply. The detail of specific actions required will be considered as part of the action planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the steps his Department is taking to tackle child poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any recent assessments carried out on passenger satisfaction with public transport services.
answer_text: My Department monitors the performance of Translink, on a quarterly basis, against a series of key performance indicators (KPI’S).  As part of the monitoring process, KPIs in respect of customer satisfaction for Metro, Gilder, Ulsterbus, Goldliner and Rail services were introduced in June 2024. The first two monitoring reports to include the customer satisfaction KPI show that Metro has fallen below target in both reports and Glider once.  The other services, Rail, Ulsterbus and Goldliner have met the target each time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department (i) monitors; and (ii) enforces performance standards for Translink bus services.
answer_text: My Department monitors the performance of Translink via a series of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) which are set in accordance with the Public Service Agreement. The KPIs are monitored by the Performance Monitoring Working Group and if needed, action taken to address performance issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans in place to improve the (i) reliability; and (ii) frequency of bus services in East Belfast, particularly during peak hours.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that the reliability of services is a key focus for Translink. They continue to monitor the reliability of all services daily, and there are plans in place to ensure they provide an attractive and reliable service for passengers. These measures include enhanced recruitment plans, plans to improve the quality and availability of the Bus fleet, as well as working very closely with key stakeholders including my Department to help improve bus priority measures across the City and manage any traffic congestion that impacts on the reliability and punctuality of our services. Translink also continue to monitor service frequencies and passenger journeys across Metro and Glider and will increase the service level as the demand increases. During 2025 Translink plan to increase the service level across some East Belfast services to help manage increasing demand during the evening peak as well as the increased demand for leisure travel, particularly over the weekends. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) new; and (ii) expanded bus route requests submitted to Translink for the East Belfast area in the last five years, including a breakdown of whether each request was facilitated.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm Translink have received a number of requests from one source regarding the Metro 3 corridor. In 2020 and in response to a request a section of the Holywood Road on the 3J route was reinstated on Saturday mornings and Sundays, which had been lost due to a previous change. More recently, Translink have received requests to extend the G4B Glider feeder service from Coopers Mill through to Millmount Village. This was implemented in September of this year, which also included improvements to frequency and hours of operation. There have been frequent requests for coverage in Millreagh. Although a review within the last year did not indicate a strong level of support, Translink are keeping this under review. Services were also re-introduced along Old Dundonald Rd, in response to long standing requests. There is a further request to include Millmount Village on Metro 4C. While this is not possible in the current timetable, Translink may be able to reconsider in the future. There have been several requests regarding enhanced services on the Ravenhill Road. Again, although the recent review of patronage levels did not support an increase in service provision, it will be kept under review. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the strategies or programmes his Department has in place to address social isolation.
answer_text: To address social isolation, my Department delivers the People and Place Strategy that supports projects including the Good Morning Services for elderly people, to help reduce feelings of fear, isolation and loneliness.  Other projects under this Strategy target women and vulnerable children who may also be at risk of social isolation.On behalf of the Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime, my Department takes forward the Developing Women in the Community Programme, providing an opportunity for participants to be involved in positive community programmes and services and at times encourages women out of isolation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the vacant land under the ownership of the Housing Executive, broken down by (i) location; and (ii) the number of years the land has been vacant.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19493 22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any measures being implemented to reduce dependency on temporary accommodation; and (ii) any specific targets that have been set.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In addressing specific challenges regarding temporary accommodation, the Housing Executive has initiated a Housing Solutions Task and Finish Group which is making progress looking at options for alternative and more financially sustainable options for temporary accommodation.The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to minimising temporary accommodation is outlined in its Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation (SAPTA) which can be found here https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/fd320853-cf6e-4403-908f-d0a0b7dbbaea/Temporary-Accommodation-Strategic-Action-Plan-2022-27.pdf.  The Housing Executive is currently implementing the SAPTA Year 3 Action Plan which contains 26 actions that support the delivery of objectives.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Universal Credit applications processed in each of the last five years, broken down by length of wait for their first payment.
answer_text: The information requested is:YearNo. of new UC applications processed% of applications paid in full at the end of the first assessment period**2020116,00098.2%202153,00098.2%202253,00098.0%202351,00097.8%2024*74,00097.7%*The 2024 figure is up to 30th November 2024.** The main reason for not paying everyone in full is due to further evidence/information required not being provided by the claimant on time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the progress made in identifying and repurposing vacant properties to address housing shortages.
answer_text: The Department of Finance’s Land & Property Services (LPS) maintain a register of Vacant Domestic Properties.		LPS provides my Department with information that enables insight into the location of vacant properties, however they routinely caution that their figures should not be relied upon for accuracy. Since the introduction of Rating of Empty Homes in October 2011 there is no financial advantage or requirement for homeowners/ ratepayers to notify LPS that the property is vacant. As such, data provided for any period after October 2011 may not be wholly accurate.		Furthermore, the unknown condition of these dwellings presents additional complications. Many may require substantial renovations, lack essential utilities, or be involved in ownership disputes or court proceedings.		My Department has also published a new Housing Supply Strategy on 9th December to address the wider challenges facing housing supply. It recognises the need to consider actions around the use of existing buildings to regenerate additional housing supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the Universal Credit contingency fund budget; (ii) the number of applications to the fund; and (iii) the total spend, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer to this topic in AQW 13298/22-27I refer the Member to my previous answer to this topic in AQW 14159/22-27My Department publishes an annual report which details Discretionary Support performance, including expenditure relating to the Universal Credit Contingency Fund. The annual reports can be accessed via the following website www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctions

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how Universal Credit applicants are notified of the contingency fund.
answer_text: All new claimants to Universal Credit are sent a welcome note on their online journal highlighting that the Universal Credit Contingency Fund may be available and signposts them to the freephone number to check their eligibility and apply.A weblink to the Contingency Fund information page and online application portal is also included in the ‘How to manage your Universal Credit Claim’ area of a claimant’s Universal Credit online journal account.Claimants who make a phone claim will be notified of the Contingency Fund during the application process.Every claimant that is required to visit their local Jobs and Benefits office within their first assessment period will also be provided with information regarding the Contingency Fund.People who are asked to Move to UC will receive a Migration Notice letter from the Department for Communities which provides information about the Universal Credit Contingency fund and explains the help available at www.nidirect.gov.uk/migration-notice-universal-credit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Universal Credit applicants that received (i) a contingency fund payment; (ii) an advance payment; and (iii) both of these payments, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department publishes an annual report which details Discretionary Support performance and expenditure during each financial year, including the number of Universal Credit Contingency Fund (UCCF) awards made. The annual reports can be accessed via the following website www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctions My department publishes quarterly Universal Credit statistics, including the number of households availing of a Universal Credit New Claim Advance. The statistics can be found in the supplementary tables available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/universal-credit-statistics-august-2024Given previous Discretionary Support system limitations, it is not possible for the Department to match data on those receiving both advances and UCCF payments for the period requested. However, this information will be available from 2024/25 onwards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has requested to include the ability to apply for a contingency fund payment within the Universal Credit portal.
answer_text: The Universal Credit Contingency Fund is unique to Northern Ireland and is administered through the Department's Discretionary Support Service. Similar to the Universal Credit online journal, the portal to make an online Contingency Fund application is available 24/7. 		My Department has not requested to include the ability to apply for a contingency fund payment within the Universal Credit portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Universal Credit five-week wait; and (ii) whether he has engaged with the Department for Work and Pensions to remove the five-week wait.
answer_text: Universal Credit (UC) is paid in arrears and entitlement is calculated at the end of an assessment period of one month. This allows for a UC award to be adjusted on a monthly basis to reflect an individual’s circumstances, e.g. earnings, capital, or income from other benefits. Payment is then issued and will be credited to the claimant’s bank account the following week. Consequently, a person has to wait at least five weeks before they receive their first payment. The assessment period and payment structure of UC is integral to the overall design of the benefit. However, help is available for those waiting on their first UC payment:Universal Credit Contingency FundClaimants can apply for a UC Contingency Fund non-repayable grant, which is only available to claimants living in Northern Ireland.Advance PaymentThey can also apply for an Advance Payment, which is up to 100% of their indicative award. Advance payments are repayable and will normally be deducted from the claimant’s UC award. The repayment period can be extended up to 24 months.   Two Week “Run On”People who move to UC from an existing legacy benefit, including Housing Benefit, may continue to receive their legacy benefit for a period of two weeks after moving to UC. These payments do not need to be paid back and essentially act as a grant to help people with the transition to UC. I have not had any engagement with the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions about Universal Credit assessment periods.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to facilitate shared care prescribing of medication.
answer_text: The Red-Amber (‘shared-care’) list of medication has been designed for use in Health and Social Care only, and some of the difficulties presented with private shared care agreements include concerns with assurance of private outpatient follow-up, accessibility to private consultants if advice is needed, and availability of documentation from the private sector.The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultation. The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications, including Red-Amber list drugs, to patients under their care. In particular, the limitations with regards to requests to GPs for transfer of ongoing prescribing of medication which for the patient’s GP may not be clinically acceptable.  This guidance still applies.To further facilitate appropriate shared care arrangements, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) issued ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care in September 2024 with a further document from the.  The new guidance (attached) provides a reminder that the legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription, and it is the responsibility of the individual prescriber to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice.The prescription of any medication associated with private healthcare should be managed by the private provider. Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to prescribe, in instances where a GP considers that the medication being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) guidance on Good Medical Practice, then they may choose to prescribe.  Supplementary advice to GP practices, relating to pre and/or post treatment support in primary care individuals procuring private healthcare services, was issued by my department 20th November 2024.  It advises that, where a GP is made aware of an individual’s plans to procure private healthcare in a private capacity, it is recommended that:The individual is encouraged to ensure all pre and post treatment costs are included in the overall package;The individual is informed that they may have to pay for ongoing treatment or medications which are recommended.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Universal Credit rates rebate claimants have been informed of a rates overpayment in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Rate Rebate is a rate relief scheme available to Universal Credit claimants who have a liability to pay Rates for the property they live in. Liability for the payment of Rates is determined by Article 18 and Article 20 of the Rates (NI) Order 1977 (as amended).Rate Rebate can be provided to:	Claimants who own the property in which they live (Owner Occupiers) and is credited as an annual award to their rate account for the full year ahead. 		Rental sector claimants, relief is provided monthly in arrears and is credited to their own or their private landlord’s rate account. For Housing Association/Housing Executive tenants, Rate Rebate is paid to the landlord. 	Where entitlement to Universal Credit ends or a monthly entitlement is calculated as nil, the rate rebate award is adjusted to reflect the change in circumstance. When this occurs, Land & Property Service issue a notification to the claimant indicating an overpayment has occurred and a revised Rate bill will issue.		A claimant’s entitlement to Universal Credit can fluctuate many times during the year and this will result in claimants receiving an overpayment notification each time their Universal Credit entitlement ends or is calculated as nil.The table below shows the number of Rate Rebate claimants who were notified of an overpayment of Rate Rebate each year since 1 January 2020.Calendar YearOwner OccupiersRental Sector1 January 2020 to 31 December 2020234427121 January 2021 to 31 December 2021657247301 January 2022 to 31 December 2022482253171 January 2023 to 31 December 2023461056321 January 2024 to 5 December 202448075488

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Community Pharmacy post costs claw back on medicines
answer_text: Firstly, I wish to assure you that the Department of Health and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within it acknowledge the very important work that community pharmacists and their teams deliver for our population. Following a consultation completed in March 2022 on proposals to reform aspects of the NI Drug Tariff, including review of discount clawback scales, revised discounting arrangements were introduced on a phased basis from January 2024. These arrangements aim to ensure that clawback is more appropriately applied to pharmacy contractors, whilst maintaining cost neutrality, and are being introduced over an 18-month period to minimise any impact on contractors. In the first 9 months of these transitional arrangements, over £1.27m less has been clawed back from pharmacy contractors overall under the new arrangements than legacy arrangements, and my Department remains committed to monitoring the impact of these new arrangements on all contractors and to taking actions as needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how rates rebates entitlement for those in receipt of Universal Credit is calculated; and (ii) how often each application is assessed.
answer_text: Rate Rebate entitlement is calculated based on a claimant’s liability to pay rates for the property they occupy and their Universal Credit entitlement in the relevant assessment period for that claimant. A claimant’s relevant period can be up to three months prior to their application for Rate Rebate. Regulation 10 of The Rate Relief Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017, provides for the calculation of rate rebate as follows:(UC Monthly Payment plus Earned Income) less half of the UC Work Allowance = Total Income Total Income less UC Maximum Amount (the maximum amount applied to the Universal Credit award in accordance with Article 13(2) of the Welfare Reform Order not including any Transitional Protection amount) = Excess Income If Excess Income is less than or equal to £0.00, then no further calculation is made and an applicant is eligible for a full monthly award of Rate Rebate covering in full the Monthly Rates Liability. If Excess Income is more than or equal to £0.01, a further calculation is made to multiply the Excess Income by 15% = 15% Taper The 15% Taper is then deducted from the Monthly Rates Liability to leave a partial monthly award of Rate Rebate. An application is assessed once, and if entitlement is determined, the claimant will receive an annual award in line with Regulation 10 of The Rate Relief Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 (see also Regulation 2 of The Rate Relief Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 for an explanation of “relevant date”). If the claimant remains entitled to UC, the Rate Rebate award will be reviewed 13 months after the start date of the initial claim. This will review will reoccur every 12 months thereafter. If there is a significant change of circumstance, for example, a claimant’s UC entitlement ends, Rate Rebate entitlement ends from the same date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) his assessment of the Cost of Service Investigation for Community Pharmacy in Northern Ireland final report; and (ii) the funding and policy decisions informed by the review.
answer_text: (i) PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) was commissioned by the Department to undertake a “cost of services” investigation (CoSI) of community pharmacy services relating to the period 2011/12. The report was published in November 2017.  At the time the Department made it clear that the CoSI report would help to inform the levels of funding for community pharmacy services alongside other sources of information, including an ongoing Margins Survey. It was not intended that the CoSI report would set the level of funding required to fund community pharmacy.   (ii) Informed by the CoSI report, a core financial envelope of £104m per annum was provided for the delivery of community pharmacy services, plus additional funding for trialling and testing new services which resulted in a closing funding position of circa. £116m and £113m in 2018/19 and 2019/20 respectively.  Funding for community pharmacies in NI has increased further in recent years. By 2023/24 the opening position was £128m and in April 2024 it was increased further to £147m. This represents a £43m (41%) increase in six years from 2018.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of homes delivered in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) number of bedrooms; and (ii) housing area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19227/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps he is taking to resolve traffic congestion in Belfast.
answer_text: To minimise the impact of traffic congestion in the run up to Christmas, I recently announced a pause of non-essential roadworks on key routes into Belfast, which commenced on Monday 25th November. This has been carefully planned to ensure that only the most urgent works are carried out on the identified arterial routes into the city centre between 25th November and 2nd January.A strategic oversight group comprising of my Department, Belfast City Council, PSNI and Translink has been established to consider what more can be done to ease congestion at peak times while work is ongoing.   The group is considering options which include traffic management interventions, real-time monitoring to improve the flow of public transport, and advice to commuters and others coming into the city centre on the alternative transport and travel options available to them.Mitigating the congestion issues will need a huge collective approach, with everyone considering how they can play a part to help everyone better share out the limited road space available in a smarter way. I note Translink’s recent statement that there have been over 400,000 more journeys on public transport in this last two months than there were in the same two months in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of complaints that have been received in relation to the non-compliance of the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 in the last five years.
answer_text: In the last five years, the Department has received one complaint concerning non-compliance with the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015. These regulations define the technical standards which apply to licensed Class B taxis and are intended to ensure that disabled people are carried in these vehicles in safety and reasonable comfort.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of applicants taken off the housing waiting list; and (ii) the number of these applicants who reapplied in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19228/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he intends to increase the number of (i) social homes; and (ii) affordable homes delivered each year.
answer_text: I have recently secured Executive endorsement on the Housing Supply Strategy which identified the need for a whole system approach to address housing supply issues. The Strategy recognises there is not one single overarching challenge impacting on supply and that several key levers are needed to address supply chain issues including infrastructure, the planning system, finance, land availability and construction skills.  Addressing these challenges will depend on Executive commitment to invest in the actions required to deliver the supply we need.  The construction of new social homes will be dependent on the available budget. I have been able to secure additional funds in the June and October monitoring rounds and have been able to increase the number of social homes we plan to start from a beginning position of 400 to over 1,400 new starts for this financial year. My Department held a two-day Strategic Insights Lab in June, which brought together key stakeholders in the social housing sector to share knowledge and experience on how to increase social housing delivery in the current challenging environment.  Co-Ownership remains my Department's primary delivery partner for intermediate homes for sale with £158 million in Financial Transactions Capital funding to help over 4,400 households into homeownership over the period 2020/21 to 2024/25. A new business case is currently under development.Further to this, I am preparing to fund the delivery of new affordable Intermediate Rent homes, levering-in additional private finance through a private Operator.  My Department has developed a grant funding programme to support the delivery of intermediate homes for sale which are part of Mixed Tenure (mainly social) developments throughout Northern Ireland in areas where market failure can be demonstrated.  This funding will be available to Registered Housing Associations subject to available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he plans to bring forward further residents-only parking schemes; and (ii) to detail the criteria that will be used to determine their introduction.
answer_text: i) My Department will continue to develop residents' parking schemes already commenced such as in the Bogside in Derry and Iveagh area of Belfast. My Department will also give consideration to schemes to address the emerging pressures with on-street parking in the vicinity of the new Ulster University campus in Belfast and the Magee campus. Other requests for schemes will be considered against competing priorities and budget availability.ii) The development of any current residents' parking scheme must align with my Department's Policy & Procedure Guide: RSPPG_E042 - https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/residents-parking-rsppg-e042v5.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has been carried out on the effectiveness of the Rugby Road and College Park Avenue residents-only parking scheme.
answer_text: My Department recently completed a review of residents’ parking schemes which considered the Rugby Road scheme as well as our approach to residents’ parking in general.  The review report is publicly available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/residents-parking-review-report

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how lessons learned from previous residents-only parking schemes will inform the design and implementation of future schemes.
answer_text: The Residents’ Parking Review Report published on 29 November 2024 sets out several conclusions and recommendations for any future Residents' Parking Scheme. The outcomes of this report will inform the design and implementation of any future schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Electricity Networks 123 per cent increase to the Standard Connection Charge for a new build house by 2025.
answer_text: My Department does not approve the Standard Connection Charges for connections of more than 12 new dwellings.  That is the independent Utility Regulator’s role.NIE Networks consulted on increases to the Standard Connection Charges for connections of more than 12 new dwellings  publicly in August 2023.  It published a decision paper in May 2024.  NIE Networks said that the charges reflect the actual cost increases which NIE Networks has faced.  These connections are “contestable”.  Developers may engage independent contractors for most of the works if they choose.The Utility Regulator scrutinised and approved NIE Networks’ proposed charges in November 2024, but asked that NIE Networks introduce them in a phased manner to allow builders time to adjust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how feedback from residents and stakeholders is being incorporated into the evaluation and planning of residents-only parking schemes.
answer_text: Consultation is one of the most important stages of any potential residents' parking schemes development, with schemes currently only being introduced where they have the support of a significant proportion of residents.The recent Residents’ Parking Review included consultation with, and feedback from, residents and business owners on the operation of the Rugby Road/College Park Avenue residents’ parking scheme. Views were also sought from: Belfast City Council; The Police Service of Northern Ireland; and The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. This feedback informed the conclusions and recommendations within the Review report, which will help shape any future residents' parking schemes. The report can be accessed - https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/residents-parking-review-report-nov-2024.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planned revisions to the (i) Taxi Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015; and (ii) Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: I currently have no plans to revise either the Taxi Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 or the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of taxis, currently operating in Northern Ireland, that meet the standards as set out in the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: The Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 apply to Class B licensed taxis, which are wheelchair accessible vehicles.  Taxi vehicle licensing figures form part of the National Statistics and are published on a quarterly basis, subject to pre-release protocols. This information is available via the following link: Driver & Vehicle Agency Activity Statistics | Department for Infrastructure. The latest published figures show that as of 30 June 2024, there were 352 licensed Class B taxis, which represents 4.9% of all licensed taxi vehicles (7,163).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support available to taxi operators to help meet the requirements of the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: There is no provision for financial support under the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.  I am also not aware of any relevant grants or support available from other departments but my Officials will make further enquiries and provide you with an update at your meeting with them, early in the New Year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the (i) Taxi Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015; and (ii) Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: The Taxi Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 continue to make provision for the testing and licensing of vehicles used to provide taxi services. These Regulations provide a legislative framework for the licensing of taxis and the technical standards they are required to meet. In particular, the Regulations provide a classification system based on the manner in which a taxi is operated, and a range of conditions for each class of taxi licence with which the owner is expected to comply.The Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 apply to Class B licensed taxis and provide a legislative framework for disabled people to use such taxis in safety without unreasonable difficulty and in the case of wheelchair users, to do so while remaining in their wheelchairs. The Regulations also provide for disabled people to be carried in these vehicles in safety and reasonable comfort and the Schedule to the Regulations provides technical requirements to be met by regulated taxis setting out wheelchair accessibility requirements and general accessibility requirements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any challenges implementing the Executive Office's equality responsibilities.
answer_text: The Executive Office must comply with the duties set out in Section 75 of the NI Act 1998 and is committed to ensuring that equality is at the centre of policy making and service delivery across the Department.How the Department fulfils these obligations is set out in our Equality Scheme and Disability Action Plan.There are a number of challenges faced in discharging these responsibilities. For example, TEO has been working with NISRA to explore ways to improve the availability and accessibility of data for use by officials, stakeholders and the general public.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of the vehicles assessed under (i) the Taxi Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015; and (ii) the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, detailing (a) the number that were certified, and (b) the number declined, including the reasons for declining since the regulations came into force.
answer_text: The Taxi Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 and the Taxi Accessibility Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 came into operation on 31 May 2016.  The following table shows the number of taxis that passed and failed an annual roadworthiness test (full test) from 2016-17 onwards. Financial YearTests conductedPassFailPass rate (%)2016-17*90077131187679.22017-1889507014193678.42018-1986816918176379.72019-2085257014151182.32020-2184870514383.12021-2269595625133480.82022-2371365932120483.12023-2473016040126182.7* Includes taxi tests conducted in April and May 2016 under the previous regulationsThis information has been taken from the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) National Statistics publication ‘DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator and Enforcement Statistics,  which can be found at Driver & Vehicle Agency Activity Statistics | Department for Infrastructure.The year-end Quarter 4 publications detail the annual test pass rates for the relevant financial year.  From 2018-19 onwards, Table 1.15 shows ‘Vehicle Test Pass Rates by Test Category and Test Centre’.  For 2016-17 and 2017-18, this information is provided in Table 1.20.  Whilst the DVA holds the defect information that would result in a taxi failing the vehicle test, it is not held in a readily accessible format and would take a significant amount of time and cost to produce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to increase capacity in dentistry.
answer_text: I am aware of the challenges currently being experienced within General Dental Services and I am committed to taking meaningful action to ensure the service works better for both patients and dental professionals. This is why for many months now, the focus of my Department has been on improving access to General Dental Services, particularly for high-need patients, and establishing options to support the sustainability of dental practices – such as the £9.2m investment announced earlier this year to reestablish the Enhanced Child Examination (ECE), establish the Dental Access Service (DAS), and fund the 30% enhancement to the fees paid for fillings.The DAS commenced on 1st August 2024. There are currently 30 practices participating in the scheme, details of which (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  https://online.hscni.net/dental-access-scheme/. In the first ten weeks the scheme has enabled the treatment of more than 4700 patients with an urgent or pressing dental condition.From June to November 2024, 18,100 newly registered children have benefitted from the Enhanced Child Examination scheme.Early indications are that these measures have had a positive impact and my Department will be monitoring the results closely.While interventions such as the DAS and ECE have been necessary to stabilise the service, long-term change will require sustained effort, rather than a single, one-off intervention. My officials will be engaging with the British Dental Association on how best to achieve these strategic aims, and address their concerns, as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Health Costs 1 application process administered by his Department.
answer_text: The Health Costs scheme is owned by the Department of Health.  I have not conducted an assessment of the HC1 application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will bring forward a proposal to update the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to increase capacity within General Practice.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs.  My Department has taken forward a number of wide ranging actions to address the challenges facing GPs in order to increase capacity within general practice.  These include:  Taking action to progress the 2023/24 Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Board recommendation for GPs and applying a 6.0% uplift for GP pay.  In addition, a 6% uplift for practice staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift for other expenses (3% recurrent) were also applied.Agreeing the new 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract.  A key aim of these new contract arrangements is to provide GPs with greater certainty over their income throughout the year, as well as reducing administrative burdens and associated costs to practices. Focussing on building the GP workforce through funding a number of initiatives, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, GP Retainer Scheme, GP Mentor Scheme and GP Fellowship Scheme.Taking action to streamline and update the recruitment processes for GPs who have qualified in the Republic of Ireland and other countries, as well as introducing a Streamlined Returner process with the aim of removing unnecessary barriers to those wishing to return to the GP workforce.  Increasing the annual number of GP training places from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121 (an increase of 86%).  Increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams (MDTs) and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists.Working with the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) on innovations to the local GP training programme with the objective of enhancing the recruitment and retention of trainees in Northern Ireland.In relation to future GP training numbers, the GP Training Group will make recommendations on the number of places required for 2025/26.  It will also progress work to project the number of GP training places required over the next 5 years.   The MDT programme remains the key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.  I want to be able to further invest in MDTs, so more people can benefit from prompt access to a range of support across primary care.  Accordingly, a bid for transformation funding to support the further rollout of the MDT Programme across the five-year span of the transformation fund has been submitted. Despite the continuing challenging budgetary position, I remain committed to supporting general practice so that it can continue to provide vital services both now, and in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will set out the next steps to address inconsiderate pavement parking.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing the next steps for my consideration and a formal response will be made public when I have determined the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his engagement with the Minister for Communities on the development of a fuel poverty strategy.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the other options considered by his Department to help and support the pensioners impacted by the loss of the Winter Fuel Payment, as detailed in his statement to the Assembly on 19 November 2024.
answer_text: I considered a range of options to support pensioners impacted by the loss of the Winter Fuel Payment, some of which were not within budget, could not be resourced, or introduced an element of inequality and unfairness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the announcement of the Emergency Fuel Support 2024-25 payment, to detail any steps his Department is taking to also provide support to non-pensioner households impacted by the higher cost of living.
answer_text: My Department provides financial support through the Discretionary Support Scheme that can offer help towards short-term living expenses or with the purchase of household items for those people in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation.   Funding has also been allocated to my Department’s Social Supermarket Scheme to help those in or at risk of food poverty.  My Department also provides help through the Affordable Warmth scheme to those who are eligible.From April 2024 benefits were increased by 6.7%, in line with the September 2023 rate of inflation, whilst the state pension was increased by 8.5%. The UK Government has announced that from April 2025 the basic and new state pensions, and the standard minimum guarantee in pension credit, will be increased by 4.1%. All other benefits will be increased by 1.7%. I will bring forward legislation to introduce corresponding increases in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to set out the full criteria for the Emergency Fuel Support 2024-25 payment.
answer_text: The criteria for the one-off Emergency Fuel Payment are as follows:A payment will be made to pensioner households in Northern Ireland who are not in receipt of Pension Credit or other means tested benefits and are not eligible for the Winter Fuel Payment. A payment of £100 will be made to a single pensioner living alone, or a payment of £50 will be made to each eligible member of a household (if there is more than one eligible pensioner in a household). Those who are in residential care throughout the qualifying week, and for the period of less than 12 weeks immediately before the qualifying week, will be eligible for a payment of £100.Those who are in residential care throughout the qualifying week, and for the period of 12 weeks immediately before the qualifying week, will be eligible for the payment of £50.A person in prison during the qualifying week will not be eligible for the payment.There will be no right of appeal.There will be no application process in most cases.In cases where those eligible for payments have not received them, eligible pensioners will need to contact a dedicated team within the department and provide the relevant information to prove eligibility. Payments will then be made when eligibility is confirmed. Payments will be made to those eligible via existing payment channels.Payments will be made before the end of March 2025.The eligibility criteria will apply during the same qualifying week as the Winter Fuel Payment. The qualifying week for the 2024 Winter Fuel Payment was Monday 16 September to Sunday 22 September 2024, and the pensioner must have been born on or before September 23, 1958, to be eligible. A pensioner is ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland; or habitually resident in countries covered by the Withdrawal Agreement; EEA EFTA separation agreement; Swiss citizens’ rights agreement or the Convention on Social Security between the UK and Ireland and able to demonstrate a genuine and sufficient link to the UK.The payment will not be means tested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any learning from the former Living Over the Shops Scheme.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 18197/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the former Living Over the Shops Scheme.
answer_text: A Living Over The Shops (LOTS) scheme was first piloted in 2002 by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and closed to new applications in 2009 due to budgetary constraints and because it did not deliver on anticipated outcomes.A review of the Living Over The Shops (LOTS) scheme was carried out by the Public and Corporate Economic Consultants (PACEC) in 2016. This report highlighted the limited impact of the scheme but also recognised opportunities for government intervention and private investment to regenerate town and city centres. The review identified numerous issues with creating homes in vacant spaces above retail units which the provision of a small grants scheme did not significantly overcome such as significant costs to adapt, planning constraints insurance issues, layout of buildings and potential lack of access to amenities to name a few.  It also noted that the Scheme had not significantly contributed to housing supply or regeneration and therefore there are currently no plans to invest resources in a future scheme.However, a broad programme of work is planned to deliver transformative change across housing, including the delivery of the Housing Supply Strategy. The draft Strategy includes several commitments and actions which will include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and the reuse of existing buildings to generate additional supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has considered delivering a new Living Over the Shops Scheme on foot of the 2016 public and corporate economic consultants report.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 18197/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will receive funding through the monitoring round to provide support to households struggling with the high cost of living.
answer_text: Following the extension of the Household Support Fund in England, my Department bid for £17 million of Barnett Consequential funding for winter fuel support.  Allocation of funding against this bid was agreed by the Executive on Monday 11 November 2024.  While this £17 million of funding will not fully alleviate the recent decisions taken at Westminster, it will provide a level of support from the detrimental impacts of Winter Fuel Payment changes on pensioner households this winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department (i) has developed; and (ii) is working on any cost-of-living proposals to provide support to households struggling with the high cost of living.
answer_text: My Department provides financial support through the Discretionary Support Scheme that can offer help towards short-term living expenses or with the purchase of household items for those people in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation.  From April 2024 benefits were increased by 6.7%, in line with the September 2023 rate of inflation, whilst the state pension was increased by 8.5%. The UK Government has announced that from April 2025 the basic and new state pensions, and the standard minimum guarantee in pension credit, will be increased by 4.1%. All other benefits will be increased by 1.7%. I will bring forward legislation to introduce corresponding increases in Northern Ireland. My Department provides help through the Affordable Warmth scheme, as well as the Winter Fuel Payments to those who are eligible. Funding has also been allocated to our Social Supermarket Scheme to help those in or at risk of food poverty. I urge those pensioners impacted by the recently announced changes to the Winter Fuel Payment scheme by the Chancellor to contact my Department’s Make the Call Wraparound Service which puts people in touch with any benefits, supports and services to which they may be entitled. Finally, I am pleased to have secured £17m to help mitigate the shortfall felt by many of our pensioners losing out on the Winter Fuel Payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the number of water pollution incidents in East Belfast in the last five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his engagement with His Majesty's Government regarding changes to the eligibility criteria for Pension Credit.
answer_text: I, and my Executive colleagues, wrote to the Prime Minister, Sir Keir Starmer, on 30 August urging an immediate review of the Pension Credit threshold, following the UK Government’s changes to the Winter Fuel Payment Scheme I believe that the eligibility criteria for Pension Credit should be reviewed to ensure more low-income pensioner households, who are not financially secure can access Pension Credit. Liz Kendall MP, Secretary of State for Work and Pensions responded on behalf of the Prime Minister in a letter dated 4 October, which I received on 24 October, stating that the UK Government is prioritising support for those who need it most, supporting pensioners on low incomes, and helping all pensioners through their commitment to the triple lock.I understand that at present there are no immediate plans to change the eligibility criteria for Pension Credit.I am committed to doing all I can to support pensioners who are on a low income, and I will continue to urge the UK Government to review the financial support available through the social security system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the amount of Financial Transactions Capital returned to HM Treasury in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Financial Transaction Capital (FTC) can only be used to provide loans to or equity investment in the private sector.  This funding is ring-fenced for this purpose and cannot be used for anything else.It is therefore more challenging for departments to identify suitable projects that advance the Executive’s policy aspirations and fulfil the criteria of loan or equity investment.  I have consistently encouraged Departments to consider how they can use this funding. My Department has agreed with the Treasury that any FTC underspends not carried forward may be used to offset the required repayment of 80 per cent of FTC allocations. Therefore, the £323.4 million underspend from 2019-2024 reduces future liabilities.The breakdown in the five years from 2019-20 to 2023-24 was: £72 million in 2019-20; £26.4 million in 2020-21; £1.4 million in 2021-22; £213.3 million in 2022-23 and £10.3 million in 2023-24. The 2023-24 amount is based on departments’ Provisional Outturn returns and will be confirmed when all Final Outturn returns have been submitted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the number of unadopted alleyways in East Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for the inspection or maintenance of unadopted alleyways that are in the ownership of other parties and therefore does not collate or hold information relating to the number of unadopted alleyways and footways in East Belfast.  While my Department does not have direct responsibility for unadopted alleyways, it does have powers to adopt roads and alleyways in certain circumstances.The criteria for adopting roads or alleyways generally require that the infrastructure meets certain standards, including road surface quality, drainage, lighting, and accessibility.  These standards are in place to ensure that once adopted, the infrastructure is sustainable and safe for public use. If property owners or councils in East Belfast wish to have unadopted alleyways brought up to adoptable standards, my Department would be willing to assess any formal applications or discussions on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her Department's policies to address anti-social behaviour.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the new strategy for victims and survivors.
answer_text: Meeting the needs of victims and survivors continues to be a priority for us, and we are pleased to advise that we have recently approved the new Strategy for Victims and Survivors for 2024 to 2034.   The new Strategy was developed by TEO in co-operation with the Victims and Survivors Service, the Commission for Victims and Survivors, and a variety of stakeholders including funded groups and individual victims and survivors. It will ensure the right trauma informed and victim centred services are in place to empower and support victims and survivors and contribute to building a shared and peaceful future. It also sets out the specific issues which require particular focus.Given the role of other departments in delivery of some of the commitments within the Strategy, and the importance of continued partnership across the public sector, we sought Executive endorsement which was obtained on 26 September 2024. The strategy has now been published along with a Written Ministerial Statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the financial position facing his Department.
answer_text: The Department of Health is facing significant financial challenges not just for 2024-25 but in future years.While the £122m received in June monitoring made some contribution to reducing the deficit position, a shortfall of £143m still remained to cover existing spending.With an additional push for savings from our ALBs and an assumption that we will receive an additional £130m through in-year monitoring I hope this gap can be bridged.However, this still means that there is currently no budgetary provision for 2024-25 pay settlements to staff, the cost of which is currently estimated to be in the regions of £300m based on latest pay announcements in England.The level of savings measures required to fund a pay award in 2024-25 would require the implementation of some measures that will be contrary to the interests of health and social care, requiring us to actively shrink the HSC and resulting in more pressures on services and staff and yet more gaps in provision at a time when the clamour is understandably to invest in more doctors, nurses and other health professionals. Our expectation for 2025-26 is that the funding position will not significantly improve for Health and further reductions to services may have to be considered and it will be extremely challenging to secure funding for new activity.  Therefore, we will need to focus on making best use of the resources we already have through greater levels of productivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many young people were engaged by the Youth Justice Agency in each of the last three years.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 5 - AQO 741/22-27		ANSWERIn the last three years the Youth Justice Agency engaged with:1,938 individual young people in 2020/21. 2,243 in 2021/22; and2,460 in 2022/23 across its core services.These figures are available on the Department’s website within the Agency’s annual Performance Impact Reports.  A further breakdown of individuals involved in the Agency’s different community-based services or admitted to custody in the Juvenile Justice Centre are available within the annual Youth Justice Agency Annual Workload Statistical bulletins produced by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA).  These annual publications can also be found on the Department’s website.  The next publication covering the 2023-24 year will be available on 19 September.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps are under consideration to improve information management across the Executive Committee.
answer_text: The business of the Executive Committee involves the production and circulation among Ministers of a considerable volume of papers. This work is undertaken centrally by the Executive Secretariat. The management of this information within the Executive Office is handled strictly in accordance with the department’s records management policy and procedures. Each department is separately responsible for ensuring that appropriate arrangements are in place to manage and protect information provided to their Minister for the conduct of Executive business.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their Department’s policy in relation to the retention of information on Ministerial devices.
answer_text: The Executive Office reflects NICS policy in respect of the retention of records.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the measures undertaken by her Department to tackle paramilitarism and organised crime.
answer_text: From support for the police, courts, probation and prisons, to management of offenders, criminal justice reform, PCSPs, ARCS funding, legislation on serious and organised crime, community safety initiatives, plus much more, we are serious about addressing crime and ending the harm it causes to people and communities. But to be clear, we cannot police our way out of this complexity – organised crime may need a justice sector response but it doesn’t start there. Justice is at the tail end of a long chain of vulnerabilities, child criminal exploitation, trauma, poverty, mental health, violence and, in many cases, unmet needs that criminals are always ready to exploit. We need early intervention and a whole of government response. That’s why my Department is happy to host the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime. Through over one hundred evidence-based interventions there is consistent pressure on gangs, support for victims and reduced risk of criminal exploitation. There is always more to be done, but this is working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of breed-specific legislation for dogs.
answer_text: I appreciate that the views of individuals and organisations differ on the effectiveness of breed-specific legislation and I have heard the arguments made for improving responsible dog ownership to reduce dog attacks.  All dogs can be dangerous.
