I strongly support the view that dog owners are accountable for the actions of their dog irrespective of breed.However, I have had to balance the views of those who are opposed to breed specific legislation with my responsibility as Minister to act in the interests of public safety.  Therefore, I have ageed to introduce restrictions on XL Bully ownership due to the number of attacks and fatalities attribiuted to this type of dog across the United Kingdom, noting there has been a significant increase in this breed being licensed in Northern Ireland.I remain committed to working with stakeholders and interested parties to look at any improvements that could be made to animal welfare and dog control legislation in the time ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the implementation of the commitments in New Decade, New Approach.
To ask the Assembly Commission whether there are plans to consider further adaptations to Parliament Buildings to improve accessibility.
To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5040/22-27, for an update on the plans for each of the sites listed.
answer_text: The Education Authority is retaining both sites for possible future educational use and is currently considering their suitability, subject to funding, for development for children with special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any plans for new special educational needs facilities in Belfast; and (ii) expected timelines for completion.
answer_text: A Development Proposal (DP 703) was approved by the Permanent Secretary on 13 October 2023 to establish a new 3-19 Special School in the Belfast City Council area, to provide 275 special school places on a phased basis, with effect from 1 September 2024, or as soon as possible thereafter. The Education Authority (EA) is now progressing a project to establish this new school, however until feasible options are identified and assessed, and a Business Case is completed to identify the preferred option to be taken forward it is not possible to provide an accurate timeline for completion of the project at this time.In addition to this new build school the increase in specialist accommodation required in special schools and mainstream settings is being actively addressed through the delivery of School Enhancement Programme and Minor Works projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Waste Prevention Programme for Northern Ireland - Stopping Waste in its Tracks.
answer_text: I want to begin by saying I recognise the importance of waste prevention. The more we can prevent, the less waste we have to deal with, and the less of the Earth’s resources we use. The Programme’s actions support the transition from a linear to a circular economy, which is an essential tool in mitigating against climate change and contributing towards our net zero ambition. The aim of the Programme is to drive change by looking beyond recycling to focus on waste prevention and reduction at source, reuse and repair. There have been lots of successes but I will briefly mention a couple.The establishment of Northern Ireland’s first reuse and repair network - Northern Ireland Resources Network, has helped to promote reuse and repair across the country and I know that East Belfast Mission in the Member’s constituency is a very active member within that network. There have also been many successes relating to reducing and preventing food waste, which can both save the environment and save money. In fact, Food Waste Action Week takes place from the 18th  to 24th March and I would encourage Members to get involved with this important initiative aimed at reducing food waste in the home.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many unexplained wealth orders have been issued since they were introduced.
answer_text: The Criminal Finances Act 2017 introduced the powers for specified agencies to apply to the High Court for an unexplained wealth order (UWO) to be made. The relevant Northern Ireland provisions of the 2017 Act were brought into operation on 28 June 2021.The National Crime Agency (NCA) is the lead agency for UWOs in Northern Ireland.  I understand that the NCA has used UWOs on five investigations to date across Great Britain and Northern Ireland which were all granted. The value of assets identified in those five investigations totals £145m.  A sixth UWO application was made in the High Court in Northern Ireland in June 2023 and completed at the end of October 2023.  Judgment has not yet been handed down in the case.UWOs are a useful investigatory, rather than a recovery tool.  A UWO can be sought without any civil or criminal proceedings having begun.  While they have the potential to enhance the ability of enforcement agencies to take steps to recover the proceeds of crime, using a wider range of asset recovery powers, they are not in themselves a means to recover the proceeds of crime.Other asset recovery tools are used successfully in Northern Ireland on an ongoing basis, including applications by police and the National Crime Agency for Account Freezing Orders and Listed Asset Orders, which were also introduced by the Criminal Finances Act 2017.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to address Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services waiting lists.
answer_text: My Department recognises that the number of children and young people awaiting Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) has increased since 30th June 2019.  Statistics published by my Department on the 14th February 2024 highlighted that as of 31st December 2023 the total number of young people awaiting assessment across Northern Ireland was 2,095 an increase of 532 since 30th June 2019. All Trusts are reporting recruitment and retention issues along with more complex presentations and both of these factors are having an impact on capacity. Action 32 of The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed in creating a workforce that will meet the needs of the population and which is fit for the future. A second implementation planning co-design workshop is planned for the 27th April, the purpose of which is to refine a workforce review implementation plan.The Mental Health Strategy recognises that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health Budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.My Department oversees a Service Delivery Plan process which monitors activity and performance across all Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCT). Officials have set targets for new and review contacts within CAMHS and these are reviewed on a monthly basis. If a HSCT is underperforming against its target, my officials require explanations from them on why this is the case. When necessary HSCT’s are required to submit a service improvement plan that details what steps they will take to improve performance against set targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the statutory responsibility his Department has in relation to the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The statutory responsibilities of my Department in relation to the Housing Executive are set out in legislation. The overall governance framework in which the Housing Executive operates is explained within the new Partnership Agreement between the Department for Communities and the Housing Executive. The Partnership Agreement was introduced last year, in line with ‘Partnerships between Departments and Arm’s Length Bodies: NI Code of Good Practice’. The Agreement includes details of the Housing Executive’s establishment and purpose, its governance arrangements, the role of the Department and the Assurance Framework. The applicable legislation is detailed at Annex 1 of the Agreement. The Partnership Agreement has been published on both the Department’s and Housing Executive’s websites.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the financial transactions capital utilised by his Department in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The Department has utilised financial transaction capital mainly for Co-Ownership housing in the last 3 years as follows:Financial YearFinal Outturn (£'000)2020-2139,2502021-2245,7502022-2336,250There was no financial transaction capital budget allocated to the Department in the 2 previous financial years 2019-20 and 2018-19. The ONS classification of Registered Housing Associations to the public sector at this time made them ineligible for access to financial transaction capital.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of referrals to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services, broken down by the length of time waiting for an appointment, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department collects and publishes waiting time statistics for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services, in the format of; total number waiting and, those waiting over 9 weeks, at quarter end. This information is available on the  Department of Health website Children and adolescent mental health service (CAMHS) statistics | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has given any consideration as to how planning applications for housing new builds could be expedited.
answer_text: The two-tier planning system established in 2015 transferred responsibility for processing the majority of planning applications, including those for new housing, to local councils.  Although my Department has an oversight role in respect of the planning system and reports on system activity and performance, it does not interfere with councils’ rights and responsibilities in terms of the processing of planning applications.  I remain committed to ensuring that our planning system continues to work in an efficient and effective way and intend to take some time to review the progress that has been made to date. I will continue to work closely with key stakeholders to collectively improve processes and increase the efficiency of the system which in turn should support local councils in expediting all planning applications.  Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline when they expect a Programme for Government to be finalised.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has decided to undertake further stakeholder engagement on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer, beyond what the previous Minister had completed.
answer_text: My officials will continue to engage with stakeholders throughout the lifetime of this Programme. Over the coming months officials will work with stakeholders to consider needs in detail, design proposals for a scheme to disperse funding to clubs and to establish structures to ensure construction projects are properly overseen and successfully delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Anti-Poverty strategy.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he intends to support and promote the arts.
answer_text: I recognise the contribution that the arts, in all its forms, and the broader creative industries make to the quality of life of people here.  They can draw people together and can create common ground across our communities.		Work on the development of the Executive’s first Culture, Arts and Heritage strategy is underway in my department.  This strategy is intended to set the course for support to those sectors over the coming decade.   		I will continue to provide funding to the arts sector, including through the Arts Council of Northern Ireland, and was pleased to have been able to provide additional grant to bolster its musical instruments programme.  		Decisions on future funding for the arts, as with all the other valuable calls for investment, will be considered in the light of decisions on the budget. The Budget for 2024-25 has not yet been settled.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6176 22-27, to detail the forecasted cost of the IT system.
answer_text: As addressed in my response to your AQW 6176/ 22-27, a software change of this nature would require the IT development team in the Department of Work and Pensions to complete a full cost analysis to determine the resources, scope, and ongoing maintenance costs of making this change. As this work has not been commissioned, we are unable to provide forecasted costing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps his Department is taking to make society more accessible for those with disabilities.
answer_text: My Department takes a number of steps to make society more accessible for those with disabilities including:	Employability support provisions such as Workable NI, Access to Work NI and the Condition Management Programme that help people with disabilities to access and remain in work;	The ‘Active Living’ Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland that includes goals to ensure that sport and physical activity is inclusive and offers equality of opportunity for disabled people; 	Working with Disability Sport NI and other key stakeholders in the disability sport sector to open up participation opportunities for people with a disability to get involved in sport and physical activity that is suitable to them; 	The Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s Disabled Facilities Grant scheme that supports people living with disabilities where they require adaptations to be carried out to their homes to ensure their safety and that they can maintain their independence;	The publication of the Ministerial Advisory Group’s (MAG) principles document on ‘Inclusive Places’ that illustrates key and practical design principles for disabled people advocated by MAG and a range of design professionals;	The Disability and Carers Service that is responsible for helping disabled peoples access benefits and services for people that are sick or have a disability and their carers; and	My Department pays a number of benefits to help those with disabilities including; Personal Independence Payment, Disability Living Allowance, Attendance Allowance and Carer’s Allowance.	In addition, I recently outlined my plans for the development of Sign Language in Northern Ireland and I am currently considering the next steps to be taken regarding the development of the Executive Disability Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of making housing a standalone strategic outcome within the Programme for Government.
answer_text: The inclusion of any potential strategic outcome as part of a Programme for Government will be a matter for the Executive to collectively prioritise and consult upon. However, the delivery of good quality, affordable and sustainable homes is a priority for me and I will continue to highlight this in any discussions around a Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has had any engagement with the Office for Veterans Affairs on ensuring initiatives to support Veterans are available to the service community in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials engage with the Office for Veterans Affairs as and when issues relevant to the Department’s remit are raised.	My Department offers a range of supports for people experiencing issues with benefits, employment or housing; however, there are no supports targeted specifically for veterans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the reduction in the social housing development programme funding.
answer_text: The reduction in Social Housing Development Programme funding in 2023/24 is reflected in reduced starts for this programme year. In 2022/23 my Department met its target for starts on 1,950 new social housing homes. If the budget bid for 2023/24 had been met in full, it would have been expected to deliver starts on 2000 new social housing homes. The budget allocation for 2023/24 is expected to deliver starts on 1500 social homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5614/22-27, to detail the reasoning behind the policy change regarding the response to inquiries concerning Housing Executive-related statistical information.
answer_text: This approach was adopted to ensure clarity between the Department’s policy responsibilities and Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s (NIHE) operational remit, as well as to ensure that MLAs receive accurate, timely and full responses.Following the Speaker’s comments in Assembly plenary on 12 March 2024, I will seek operational information from the NIHE Chief Executive and provide it in response to Assembly Questions.Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of housing new starts that were impacted by a lack of (i) water; and (ii) sewerage infrastructure in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: The number of housing new starts impacted by a lack of water and sewerage infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has forecasted a financial year end underspend.
answer_text: At this stage in the financial year, no year-end underspends are forecast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown of the £14.7 million allocated to his Department for pay awards.
answer_text: The Executive allocated £14.7m Resource DEL to DAERA to fund pressures of £2.0m for bovine TB compensation (which is a statutory obligation for the Department) and £12.7m for a potential pay award.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide a breakdown of the £12 million allocated to his Department for pay awards.
answer_text: As outlined in the Minister of Finance’s Written Ministerial Statement of 15 February 2024, the Minister of Finance has given officials a negotiating remit to engage with Trade Unions on the 2023 pay award for those staff on Northern Ireland Civil Service terms and conditions.   The costs associated with the proposed pay award for my department are included within the £12.1 million allocation for pay and other pressures. Negotiations on pay are still ongoing so it is not yet decided how the £12.1 million allocated to my Department will be broken down.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a breakdown of the £6.7 million allocated to her Department for pay awards.
answer_text: The £6.7 million was allocated to my department for the following pay awards:NICS progression + 5 % 					£1.5 millionNICS one-off non-consolidated payment of £1500	£5.2 millionTotal								£6.7 million

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a breakdown of the £3.4 million allocated to their Department for pay awards.
answer_text: The department has been allocated an additional £3.4m in the Spring Supplementary Estimates which will be used to meet departmental pressures including pay.  Negotiations are ongoing in respect of pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a breakdown of the £550.6 million allocated to his Department for pay awards.  .
answer_text: HSC pay covers a number of pay groups, including Doctors, Dentists, Agenda for Change and Senior Executives. The £550.6m will be required to meet all pay awards within HSC, and will be appropriately allocated on the successful completion of negotiations with health Trade Unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of the £296.8 million allocated to his Department for pay awards.
answer_text: The allocation to the Department of Education consists of a contribution towards forecast non-pay overspends for 2023-24, three years of non-contractual Teaching pay awards, and the 2023-24 pay award for NICS staff.  The Department will determine how the £296.8m allocation will be utilised against these pressures in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of the £19.4 million allocated to his Department for pay awards.
answer_text: On 15 February 2024, my Department was allocated £19.4m for pay costs.  Whilst the 2023 Pay Award is still subject the agreement, the allocation provides funding to cover estimated pressures associated with pay negotiations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown of the £75.3 million allocated to her Department for pay awards.
answer_text: The £75.3 million has been allocated to my Department as a total Resource DEL allocation to cover the current level of my Department’s forecast overspend, including pay pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of the £87.8 million allocated to his Department for pay awards.
answer_text: My Department has been allocated an additional £87.8m to meet departmental pressures relating to the continued provision of essential services and 2023-24 pay awards. Negotiations for 2023-24 pay awards are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacancies across the civil service, broken down by (i) department; and (ii) business area.
answer_text: ANSWERDetails of the total vacancies in each department is not held centrally by Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a department identifies a vacancy it needs filled, the request is referred to NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As at the end of January 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 2517 vacancies across the Civil Service. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the location of potholes reported across East Belfast, broken down by repair status, in the last 2 years.
answer_text: Following the reform of local government in 2014, the Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the new district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.This data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect type, geographical area and status.In addition, official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the fuel poverty strategy.
answer_text: A Fuel Poverty and Just Transition Strategy to provide a long-term framework for addressing fuel poverty and its impacts is in development,  with intensive pre consultation stakeholder engagement underway.  We are engaging with and bringing together people from a wide range of organisations to help shape the strategy to alleviate fuel poverty and assist with meeting Net Zero targets while ensuring a Just Transition.This phase will be followed with a public consultation on a draft Fuel Poverty Strategy in autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people receiving Universal Credit waited more than 5 weeks for their first payment, in the last 2 years.
answer_text: Between January 2022 and January 2024,  operational records indicate that 97.8% of households claiming Universal Credit in Northern Ireland received either their first payment in full (96.2%) or in part (1.6%), at the end of their first assessment period. Delays in making all full payments are due to claimants taking more time to provide the information needed to process their claim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will address the loophole which prevents British military veterans from accessing treatment through the Robert Jones and Agnes Hunt Orthopaedic Hospital.
answer_text: Although the Armed Forces Act 2021 received Royal Assent on 15 December 2021 and applies to the whole of the UK, including Northern Ireland, responsibility for implementing the Act lies with the First Minister and deputy First Minister. As such, my Department is awaiting further instruction from the Executive Office before considering future services for veterans.At present, veterans will continue to have access to the full range of services within the health and social care sector. Should a consultant feel that a veteran needs specialist treatment that is not available through health and social care facilities, he may wish to consider using the extra contractual referral process. However, this can not be used solely to avoid waiting lists or to enable veterans to avail of priority schemes for veterans that exist in other parts of the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of repairs carried out by contractors for the Housing Executive; and (ii) the number of quality inspections conducted in respect of the repairs in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The number of repairs carried out by contractors for the Housing Executive, and the number of quality inspections in respect of the repairs, is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should, therefore, direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the traffic calming measures are under consideration in East Belfast, broken down by priority.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of traffic calming and road safety measures and am committed to exploring solutions that deliver better for communities and improve lives across the North.My department receives many requests for traffic calming and I would like to be in a position to fulfil all valid requests. However, particularly in the context of severe and challenging budgets, those areas requiring most urgent attention must be prioritised. Details of the planned capital improvement schemes, including traffic calming schemes, for the 2023/24 financial year in the East Belfast area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Belfast City Council; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf  The programme for 2024/25 will be developed once future budgets are agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people waiting for a care package.
answer_text: As at 31 January 2024, the number of clients assessed and waiting for a FULL package of Domiciliary Care was:Total clients awaiting full packageAs at 31 January 2024HSC TrustNo. of clientsBelfast323Northern1,157South Eastern194Southern623Western260Northern Ireland2,557Source: Monthly returns supplied by HSC Trusts to PSSI, SPPGTotal clients awaiting partial packageAs at 31 January 2024HSC TrustNo. of clientsNorthern553South Eastern106Southern71Western398Northern Ireland1,542Source: Monthly returns supplied by HSC Trusts to PSSI, SPPGDomiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the compensation paid out due to defective (i) roads; and (ii) footpaths, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20182019  Table 7 in the above link provides details of the compensation paid during the last five full financial years.  However, please note that the NICS accounting system, Account NI, does not record whether compensation relates accidents that occurred on roads or footpaths.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he intends to address health waiting lists, broken down by the estimated timeframe for each.
answer_text: Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  It is, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.In June 2021 I published the Elective Care Framework which sets out a detailed plan for addressing hospital waiting lists. Much good work is underway to tackle the backlog of patients waiting, for example through the development of elective care centres, rapid diagnosis centres and mega clinics.  In addition, my Department is working with Trusts to increase productivity and efficiency through the use of a wide range of best practice service improvements.While implementation of the Framework is continuing, it is currently under review to ensure that it takes account of changes within the overall HSC environment in the 3 years since publication and is updated to reflect them.   Improved outcomes will only be achieved with significant recurrent investment to transform the delivery of elective care on a sustainable basis and address the central issue of demand outstripping the current capacity. The timescale within which waiting times can be improved is dependent on these factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to ensure that people have timely access to mental health services.
answer_text: All Trusts are experiencing significant pressures as a consequence of increased demands upon services, including increased numbers of new referrals, increased complexity and acuity of people presenting to the service and significant workforce pressures as the result of increased vacancy levels.AdultsBelfast Health and Social Care Trust Within the Belfast Trust Community Mental Health staff are engaged in outpatient modernisation work to create capacity in Community Mental Health Teams through the use of professional initiated pathway for patients who are currently stable and not requiring active tertiary level intervention.  The objective of this work is to reduce review clinics and create capacity for new patient appointments and facilitate speedy re-entry into treatment without the need for GP referral.Acute inpatient services continue to work diligently to reduce delayed discharges, to reduce unnecessary bed days thus creating swift admission to inpatient treatment at the point of need.Northern Health & Social Care TrustWithin Northern Trust, early intervention to people with Mental Health needs is most frequently provided through Community Mental Health Services and Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Services.  Northern Trust Community Mental Health Team’s triage all referrals received on a daily basis, urgent referrals are seen within 5 days, and routine referrals within 9 weeks.  Community Mental Health Teams collaborate with services across the local community as appropriate to promote early intervention and to enable recovery.  Community Mental Health Teams maintain close working relationships with primary care colleagues and mental health staff within the GP federation. The Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Team operates a 24 hr/7day multidisciplinary mental health service available to adults who are experiencing an acute deterioration in their mental health. The Crisis Resolution service seeks to safely manage the immediate needs of service users not currently known to services or those known to community mental health services who require support outside normal working hours. In addition, the Mental Health Liaison Service provides a comprehensive mental health assessment and intervention to service users referred from acute hospital wards and service users who present to the Emergency Department across both Antrim Area and Causeway Hospital sites. Individuals are directed to attend Emergency Departments usually by their GP where there is a requirement for assessment of physical health and mental health care needs. In the absence of a physical health care need, individuals experiencing an acute deterioration in their mental health are directed to Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Team by Emergency Department staff.The Mental Health Liaison Service and the Crisis Resolution Home Treatment Team access to inpatient MH services, if following assessment, it is felt that a period of inpatient treatment is required.South Eastern Health & S

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will consider introducing grants for households to install (i) solar panels; (ii) heat pumps; and (iii) other energy efficiency measures.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity as outlined in the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2022 and 2023. My officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects. Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions. My department is also preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps. My department is currently preparing a consultation on evidence-based options for a domestic energy efficiency programme in line with Action 4 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 20231 . The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support. In the interim, the department is working with partners such as the Utility Regulator to assess the options for further scaling of up existing energy efficiency programmes. Homeowners may wish to seek independent advice on solar panels, heat pumps and other energy efficiency matters from organisations such as the NI Energy Advice Service, the Energy Saving Trust and Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps his Department is taking to reduce the cost of energy for (i) households; and (ii) businesses.
answer_text: My Department along with the Utility Regulator (UR) and the Consumer Council for Northern Ireland (CCNI) continues to work closely to ensure that energy is affordable for all consumers.The Path to Net Zero Energy Strategy aims to move away from the imported fossil fuels that put us in the position of a “price-taker”.  One of the key objectives of the strategy is to ensure that all consumers, business and domestic, have access to essential and affordable energy to enable an adequate standard of competitiveness. The focus is now on priorities for delivery to ensure that we maximise the benefits for our society and economy as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what plans she has to bring forward legislation to regulate property management companies.
answer_text: I will engage with officials within the Department to assess how this issue can be addressed. This is a complex matter requiring significant expertise and future work will be dependent on competing priorities and available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the estimated IT and administrative cost of introducing the Carer’s Allowance Supplement; and (ii) whether this IT and administrative cost is one-off or recurring.
answer_text: Introducing an equivalent payment to the Carer’s Allowance Supplement paid in Scotland was one of the recommendations made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022.The recommendations made in that report and any future mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.  Therefore, my Department is not currently in a position to provide an estimate of the cost for the IT or administration necessary for the delivery of a Carer’s Allowance Supplement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether all clauses of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 are in operation.
answer_text: Sections 155, 157, 159, 160 and 161 of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (“the Act”) came into operation on Royal Assent on 27 April 2022.  Section 137, which provides for Guardians ad Litem, who are appointed to represent the interests of children in specified proceedings, to be known as Children’s Court Guardians, came into operation on 13 February 2023.The Act is being implemented on a phased basis over five years, from 2022-2027, and the remaining provisions will be commenced in accordance with the agreed phasing schedule.  In April 2025, my Department will publish and lay before the Assembly its first report on the implementation of Parts 1 and 2 of the Act, in accordance with its duty under section 158.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cost to change the Universal Credit IT system to maintain the £20 per week uplift which was introduced during the Covid-19 pandemic.
answer_text: Universal Credit (UC) claims are maintained and processed on a UK wide computer system owned by the Department for Work and Pensions.  In addition to both legislative and technical barriers which would take considerable time and cost to resolve, there would also be a significant annual cost to pay Northern Ireland claimants a different rate of UC than the rest of the United Kingdom. All of these costs would need to be met from the NI Block Grant.		Since the £20 uplift was removed in October 2021, Universal Credit, in line with all other benefits was uprated by 3.1% in April 2022, 10.1% in April 2023 and is due to be uprated by 6.7% in April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the process he is taking to seek a Northern Ireland specific change to the Universal Credit IT system; and (ii) the cost to change the system.
answer_text: The Universal Credit IT system is owned and developed by the Department for Work and Pensions for the delivery of Universal Credit on a UK wide basis. The time and cost to develop Northern Ireland specific changes to the system would be dependent on the scope and functionality required. Northern Ireland specific system developments would also be reliant on dedicated funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cost of Housing Executive temporary accommodation, broken down by (i) constituency; (ii) local council area; and (iii) District Electoral Area, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: Figures in relation to the cost of temporary accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures are under consideration to reduce social housing waiting lists.
answer_text: Fundamentally the need to add to our supply of social housing.  My Department met its target for 1,950 new social housing starts last year, and is aiming to start building another 1,500 in 2023/24.   The ability to increase the supply of social housing is constrained by budget.  My Department continues to work with the NIHE to bring forward measures to maximise the Social Housing Development Programme.  One of the critical issues is the provision of suitable land in areas of housing need.  My officials are working to identify public lands held by Departments that can potentially be used for the development of social housing.  They are also looking at ways in which we can make delivery timelier; bring more stability to the programme; and to explore how issues and pressures associated with existing delivery might be addressed.Work is ongoing to implement changes to the Housing Selection Scheme. These changes will ensure that we make the best use of our existing stock by improving and streamlining processes; enabling the waiting list to move more effectively.My Department also provides funding for intermediate housing, to complement social housing supply.  In April 2021 the Department published a new definition of Affordable Housing. This allows for the development of a broader range of intermediate housing products, the purpose of which is to contribute to an increase in overall housing supply and a reduction in housing stress.In parallel, my Department has developed a draft Housing Supply Strategy that aims to provide a long-term framework for policies that can increase supply across all tenures. I am currently reviewing this and considering options on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will update the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004.
answer_text: Preparatory work has recently commenced in advance of updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004. The focus of work is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive free sight tests and free Health Service dental treatment through the Help with Health Care (HwHC) scheme following the migration of their benefits to Universal Credit by the Department of Communities (DfC).The Department, in consultation with DfC, has implemented an interim solution to help ensure that people do not lose out on their entitlement. However, this is no longer an automatic system. It is paper based, and individuals must apply for an annual certificate.  This has obvious implications for some of our most vulnerable people. While the Department is currently liaising with officials in DfC to explore options for streamline this process, it is clear that there is an urgent need to update our legislation to reinstate automatic passporting to those eligible.The Department will seek to maintain, as closely as possible, the caseload of recipients who are eligible for HwHC, balancing the continued protection of the health of members of society who need additional support without adding pressures on HSC budgets.   My Department is currently liaising with other NI departments and colleagues in GB. This will inform the decision on setting the income threshold for UC becoming an automated passport benefit. A short public consultation will be conducted in the coming months on proposed changes to our legislation in relation to UC, with the associated legislative process commencing in due course thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the process for determining how temporary accommodation is selected by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: Temporary accommodation selection is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the recommendations from the Welfare Mitigations Review to introduce (i) a Carer’s Allowance Supplement; and (ii) a Young Carer Grant.
answer_text: I recognise the enormous amount of work done by those who have responsibility of caring for others. There is no doubt that they play an important role within the wider community, as well as providing incredible support to the people for whom they are caring.I have noted the recommendations with regard to providing additional support for carers that were made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022. The recommendations made in that report and any future mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the rationale for the recent Housing Executive rent increase; and (ii) what steps are being taken to maintain affordable rents.
answer_text: The decision to increase NIHE rent follows an annual process. NIHE rent levels are considered by taking into account inflation, projected running costs, spending requirements and tenant affordability. Rent pays for all the services tenants require including maintenance and investment in homes. My Department is committed to ensuring NIHE rents remain affordable for tenants. Affordability modelling is central to the rent setting process. NIHE rents continue to be more affordable than any other alternative.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount spent on Carer’s Allowance; (ii) this spend as a proportion of the social security budget; and (iii) this spend as a proportion of the Department for Communities budget, for each year from 2019 to 2023.
answer_text: The table below sets out the information requested: YearCarers Allowance Spend£’000Carers Allowance Spend as % of Social Security BudgetCarers Allowance Spend as % of DfC Budget2018/19170,8432.7%2.4%2019/20176,2172.7%2.3%2020/21181,6172.5%2.1%2021/22177,5812.4%2.1%2022/23182,4492.4%2.0%		The above % figures are derived from published statistics.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Disability Strategy.
answer_text: I am committed to working and delivering for disabled people in our communities right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to an Executive Disability Strategy and I will communicate these once I have considered all options on how best to progress the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households presenting to the Housing Executive as homeless due to the loss of their private rented accommodation, in each of the last five years, broken down by (a) reason for the loss of private rented accommodation; (b) Housing Executive local area office; and (c) local council area.
answer_text: Figures in relation to households presenting to the Housing Executive as homeless due to the loss of their private rented accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households on the social housing waiting list (i) with full duty applicant status; (ii) in housing stress; and (iii) with less than thirty housing points, broken down by (a) religion; (b) sex; (c) sexual orientation; (d) age; and (e) household type, as on 1 February, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of households on the social housing waiting list and any breakdown of the waiting list are operational matters for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what is the financial spend of the Housing Executive to store the personal property of individuals found to be homeless, or threatened with homelessness, in each of the last five financial years, including 2023/24.
answer_text: Spend in relation to the storage of personal property of individuals found to be homeless or threatened with homelessness is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cost of taxi provision for applicants in need of temporary accommodation broken down by (i) local council area; and (ii) Assembly constituency, for each of the last ten financial years.
answer_text: Taxi provision for applicants in need of temporary accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department’s review of the use of the Vagrancy Act 1824 and the Vagrancy (Ireland) Act 1847.
answer_text: Work on the review of the Vagrancy Act 1824 and the Vagrancy (Ireland) Act 1847 is ongoing. I remain committed to repealing the provisions in these Acts that apply to Northern Ireland in this current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average length of stay for households placed in temporary accommodation on 1 February in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) Assembly constituency; (ii) Local Council area; and (iii) District Electoral Area.
answer_text: The length of stay for households placed in temporary accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many (i) households; and (ii) individuals were living in temporary accommodation on 1 February in each of the last five years, broken down by (a) age; (b) sex; (c) local council area; (d) District Electoral Area; and (e) type of temporary accommodation.
answer_text: Figures in relation to households and individuals living in temporary accommodation is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many children were living in temporary accommodation on 1 February, in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) age; (ii) sex; (iii) Assembly constituency; and (iv) local council area.
answer_text: Children who were living in temporary accommodation and associated figures is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many (i) households; and (ii) individuals were on the social housing waiting list on 1 February in each of the last ten years, broken down by (a) Full Duty Applicant status; (b) housing stress; (c) local council area; (d) district electoral area; and (e) length of time on the waiting list.
answer_text: The number of applicants on the social housing waiting list, broken down by the criteria requested are operational matters for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of social housing allocations made by NIHE local offices broken down by Common landlord area; and (ii) the mean and median points at the point of allocation by (a) local office; and (b) common landlord area, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: The number of social housing applications made by Housing Executive local offices, and the mean and median points at the point of allocation are operational matters for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to address obstructive pavement parking.
answer_text: During November 2023 the Department made legislation that added to the list of locations where parking wholly or partly on a footway is prohibited to now include beside bus lanes, bus stops, mandatory cycle lanes and mandatory school keep clears.   The Department routinely reminds drivers to ‘think before you park’ and continues to take action against vehicles that are in contravention of parking restrictions, including those that apply to footways.On 6 December 2023 the Department released a Findings Report on the responses received to the consultation on inconsiderate pavement parking.  I plan to issue a formal response to the consultation exercise when I have had time to considered the Findings Report

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on work to revitalise the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.
answer_text: The NIHE faces a significant investment challenge.  I am carefully considering advice and options to transform it onto a sustainable footing and to increase investment in homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the rollout of the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for football is an important flagship Executive commitment and I recognise the valuable contribution it can deliver for football and wider society. I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not yet been delivered. Investment in local football grounds will help to support the legacy of the Euros and I look forward to progressing this programme and delivering football facilities that are modern, safe and meet the needs of our communities.My officials are working with a range of stakeholders to develop proposals. I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the disability strategy; and, (ii) whether an update to disability legislation is being considered.
answer_text: I am considering the next steps to be taken in the development of an Executive Disability Strategy. This will include consideration of the need for a review of disability legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures are under consideration to support households impacted by the rising cost of living.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has been at the forefront of delivering support to households impacted by the rising cost of living.  In response to the cost-of-living and energy crises, the UK Government announced a package of one-off payments for disabled claimants, pensioners and those on income-related benefits. Northern Ireland claimants are included in these payments which are intended to provide additional financial support in response to the rise in the cost of living. The most recent Cost of Living Payments have been delivered throughout 2023/24 with a final payment of £299 to eligible people in receipt of an income-related benefit to be paid in February 2024. Further information is available at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/cost-living-support My Department provides financial support through the Discretionary Support Scheme that can offer help towards short-term living expenses or with the purchase of household items for those people in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation.  Depending on personal circumstances applicants could be offered either an interest-free loan or a non-repayable grant.In addition, from April 2023 benefits and state pension were increased by 10.1%, in line with the September 2022 rate of inflation. From April 2024 benefits will be increased by 6.7%, in line with the September 2023 rate of inflation, whilst the state pension will be increased by 8.5% in line with the increase in average earnings between May and July 2023.My Department also provides help through the Affordable Warmth scheme, as well as the Winter Fuel Payments to those who are eligible. Funding has been allocated to our Social Supermarket Scheme to help those in or at risk of food poverty.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5401/22-27 and AQW 5404/22-27, to detail how disability access was factored into the work.
answer_text: From the outset, the tender specification for the refurbishment of Assembly committee rooms included the requirement to design tables to be suitable for both standard chairs and manual wheelchairs. During the design phase of the project the suppliers reviewed room layouts and table placement to optimise accessibility within the available space in each room.For those with hearing difficulties, every room continues to be equipped with induction loops. Additionally, a remote participation option can be offered, ensuring Assembly committee work remains accessible to those unable to physically attend Parliament Buildings.Rooms 29 and 30 received further enhancements: relay screens and improved sound reinforcement allow clearer viewing and hearing for visitors in the public gallery and all committee rooms have speakers added to the tables and headphone access to provide Members and witnesses with enhanced audio quality.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5401/22-27 and AQW 5404/22-27, to detail the disposal process for the furniture that was previously used in Assembly Committee rooms.
answer_text: The furniture previously used in Assembly committee rooms was donated to ‘Business in the Community’, to be re-used by local organisations and businesses.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5401/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the cost of (i) labour; (ii) IT equipment; and (iii) furniture.
answer_text: The breakdown of budget costs in AQW 5401/22-27 is as follows:Design, Build and Installation of Broadcasting Equipment and Systems (£1.75m)Labour - £192,935            IT equipment - £1,214,857Furniture - £342,208Refurb and Building Works (£300k)Labour - £63,512Furniture - £43,098Plant, materials and subcontractor fees - £193,390

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5395/22-27, (i) whether it has received a request to repair the dishwasher in the canteen kitchen; and (ii) when is it scheduled for repair.
answer_text: There are currently no calls logged with the helpdesk for repairs to the canteen kitchen dishwasher.  No repairs have been scheduled.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5393/22-27, to detail the number of staff, not employed by the Assembly Commission, that presented an ID pass to the electronic card readers in Parliament Buildings during the same period.
answer_text: As mentioned in the response to your previous question AQW 5393/22-27, details of building occupancy are only available from 28 July 2023, when the visitor management element of the new Parliament Buildings Security System (PBSS) went live.The table below indicates the number of staff not employed by the Assembly Commission who presented an ID pass to electronic card readers in Parliament Buildings each day during the period 28 July 2023 to 25 September 2023.  numbers shown in the table are made up from staff from contracted companies, political parties, Office of the Legislative Council, Northern Ireland Civil Service and students.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to provide a breakdown of the cost of refurbishing the Assembly Committee rooms.
answer_text: Until 2010, the Senate Chamber was the only broadcast-enabled committee room in Parliament Buildings. In 2010, Room 29 and Room 30 were also converted into broadcast-enabled committee rooms. While new cameras were installed at that time, the audio systems were repurposed from decommissioned audio-only committee rooms (Room 135 and Room 144) that had been in place from 1998. Since then there have been no significant upgrades to the broadcasting equipment and infrastructure in the committee rooms.By 2022, the broadcasting equipment in the committee rooms was at the end of its useful life. Some of the audio equipment was over 30 years old, and the number of broadcasting faults and failures was increasing. Given the outdated nature of the existing broadcasting infrastructure, coupled with the significant risk of failure in delivering essential broadcasting services to the Assembly, it became imperative to initiate a robust upgrade programme that also included the creation of an additional broadcast-enabled committee room, Room 21. This programme of work was approved by the Assembly Commission in June 2022 and work commenced in January 2023. This project is ongoing and to date work has been completed in Room 21, Room 29 and Room 30, with the final phase of work in the Senate Chamber due to begin shortly. As a result of this programme of work the Assembly will have resilient, contemporary, efficient, reliable, and flexible systems to meet both current and future broadcasting requirements.The breakdown of the elements involved in this project is as follows:Video SystemsInstallation of broadcasting-standard video facilities, including cameras, robotics and camera control systems, video conferencing/remote contributions and supporting equipment in each room.Audio SystemsInstallation of broadcasting standard digital sound systems, incorporating modern microphones, loudspeakers, and digital processing units in each room.Access to interpretation for simultaneous translation in each room.Audio visual presentation facilities in each room.New committee tables to house the new technology seamlessly in each room.Central Equipment and Control RoomsNew control room spaces for each committee room and the fitout of those rooms with technical furniture to house the new technology seamlessly.New computer room (required new racks, electrics and cable management) to house the new equipment cabinets and management server computers for efficient storage and management of the new systems.Cabling and termination, including all local and long-distance cabling for specialist video and sound.Integration with existing equipment, to ensure seamless transition and functionality with existing infrastructure.Network switches for audio over IP, enhancing connectivity and flexibility.General Building Works and Room Refurbishments in all Committee RoomsRoom layout changes.Core drilling from the ground floor into the basement for cable access, chasing/cutting out of concrete floors and

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether a health and safety evaluation has been conducted to assess the well-being of staff working in Parliament Buildings’ kitchen and canteen, as a result of action arising from issues with the maintenance of the roof.
answer_text: As a result of ongoing issues in respect of the roof of Parliament Buildings, the defects are inspected and monitored on a regular basis and relevant health and safety measures are put in place as deemed necessary, including in the canteen and kitchen.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the arrangements for servicing and maintaining the catering equipment in Parliament Buildings' canteen.
answer_text: All maintenance and repair works in Parliament Buildings, including servicing and maintaining catering equipment, is carried out by outside contractors under a Department of Finance framework agreement, to which the Assembly Commission is a named party.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to provide an update on the work to upgrade constituency office printers.
answer_text: The current constituency office multi-functional printers are supported by the manufacturer until the end of February 2024.  The IS Office plans to have new printing and scanning arrangements in place by the beginning of March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for (i) an update on plans to remove the office resources facility; and (ii) the rationale for the decision.
answer_text: The new contract for the provision of stationery supplies affords the opportunity to modernise and streamline the process for ordering and paying for stationery, as well as making the service to Members more flexible. Options for availing of the new processes under the contract are being considered and Members will be provided with information on these in due course.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the number of staff working in Parliament Buildings for each day in the last three months.
answer_text: Details of staff entering and exiting Parliament Buildings are recorded when they present their Assembly ID passes to electronic card readers situated at each of the building entrances.  Those details are then stored within the visitor management component of the Parliament Buildings Security System (PBSS), which is managed on a daily basis by the Usher Services business area.The PBSS replaced an older legacy security system earlier this year, and a record of staff occupancy is only available from 28 July 2023 when the visitor management element of the PBSS went live. The table below indicates the number of staff who entered Parliament Buildings each day during the period 28 July 2023 to 25 September 2023.  can be seen from the table, staff numbers reduce significantly on Saturdays and Sundays.  This is due to the fact that only control room staff routinely work in Parliament Buildings on those days, though additional staff or contractors may also occasionally be present dependent upon business need. It should also be noted that the Assembly Commission’s Hybrid Working Policy enables staff to work up to 60% of their contracted hours from home, subject to business need. During recess periods greater flexibility may be considered, again subject to business need.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail what guidance is provided to organisers of events in Parliament Buildings to ensure the event is accessible to all.
answer_text: As part of the event booking information capture process, the Assembly Commission’s Events team discuss any specific access requirements with the organisers. The team has extensive experience and offers sound advice to organisers to ensure that the space booked in Parliament Buildings is appropriate to the needs of their invited guests. It is the responsibility of organisers to decide their requirements and to ensure the materials and activities they use at their event are suitable for their invited guests.  The Events team also direct organisers to the ‘Planning your visit’ page of the Assembly website. This page provides information about getting to Parliament Buildings, parking and security. Specifically, it also includes:An ‘easy read’ guide on visiting Parliament Buildings;Guidance on making the online information itself more accessible; Provision for parking for Blue Badge holders and how to access it;Additional resources for those who are blind or have a visual impairment; Additional resources for those who are deaf or hard of hearing; andSupport for those with autism.Facilities that may be of help to those requiring assistance or additional support are also provided on the Assembly website, including:Wheelchair access routes;Accessible bathroom facilities;Changing Places facility;Baby changing facilities; andSupport for the Breastfeeding scheme.The page also contains a phone number, textphone number and email for any visitors who wishes to ask questions regarding their visit to Parliament Buildings. Finally, the Events team also ask organisers, at the point of booking, if anyone in the group is likely to require assistance in the event of an evacuation, in order to ensure that appropriate Assembly Commission staffing and other support is in place.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the health and safety measures taken as a result of roof maintenance issues.
answer_text: As a result of ongoing issues in respect of the roof of Parliament Buildings, the defects are inspected and monitored on a regular basis and relevant health and safety measures are put in place as deemed necessary.		These have included:		Removing visible loose material from the roof;Erecting an extended cordon to the rear of Parliament Buildings;Closing all of the Basement Courtyards; and Cordoning off the areas below the skylights in the Blue Flax restaurant and kitchen area on a temporary basis while work is ongoing to install protection under the rooflights.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the impact that the roof maintenance issues have had on staff and other building users.
answer_text: In order to ensure the ongoing health and safety of building users, some minor temporary access restrictions have been put in place in the Basement area and at the rear of the building.  Apart from the minor inconvenience that this entails, there has been very little impact to date on staff and building users.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what contingency measures are in place to facilitate events sponsored by Members of the Assembly to proceed in the event the Assembly is dissolved.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission will review arrangements for Member-sponsored events at the point at which the Assembly is dissolved. However, in the event that the Assembly is dissolved, it would be the intention of the Assembly Commission to sponsor and facilitate those formerly Member-sponsored events which are not potentially germane to the election campaign, should the organiser wish to proceed with the event.  Many events are organised within Parliament Buildings to provide an opportunity to engage with Assembly Members, therefore if the Assembly is dissolved, it is possible that some event organisers will wish to postpone their events to a later date.

answer_text: Please find attached the current list of all Member-sponsored events currently scheduled in Parliament Buildings from 28 October 2022 to 31 December 2022.
