question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the measures her Department has introduced to (i) support people with disabilities into employment; and (ii) sustain employment for people with disabilities.
answer_text: My Department recently hosted the very successful international Harkin Summit in Belfast with a focus on the provision of accessible, inclusive, sustainable and progressive employment for people with disabilities.The Department’s JobStart scheme is extended to nine months for young people on Specialist Pathways including those with a Disability. To date 443 have joined the scheme with 60 out of 90 completers having found full time employment. My Department provided JobStart opportunities for 18 young people with disabilities, 9 of these have accepted a full time position within the Civil Service.I recently launched the Labour Market Partnerships (LMPs) with the aim of achieving employability outcomes and positively impact the labour market by utilising local knowledge and encouraging collaboration through multi-Agency Partnerships. A Local LMP has already been established within each of the 11 Council areas. One of the key thematic areas for local LMPs is the delivery of initiatives that support people with disabilities into employment or self-employment.In addition to this new provision, the Department’s existing range of work preparation and in work support programmes remain available to people with disabilities who wish to enter employment, need assistance to retain their job or indeed to progress in their career including the Condition Management Programme, Access to Work and Workable.My Department also continues to provide £1.5m matched funding to 17 existing European Social Fund projects in 2022/23 which also supports people with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the terms of reference for the reconvened emergencies leadership group.
answer_text: I re-established the Voluntary and Community Sector Emergencies Leadership Group on 1 June, as part of my strategic response to the current cost of living crisis.  This Group was a model of best practice in community engagement to inform our Covid 19 Emergency Response.I have invited members to work with me to  shape the mitigation measures that I can deliver to meet the cost of living challenges that are deepening every day and impacting on the lives of workers, families and communities here. 	Following this initial meeting, the Group will co-design its Terms of Reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the discussions her Department has had with the Department for the Economy on creating a one stop shop approach as detailed in the Energy Strategy.
answer_text: The Housing Executive delivers the Energy Advice Service (NIEAS) on a cross-tenure basis, via funding from DfC.This service provides free and impartial advice as a ‘one stop shop’ for energy advice and information such as energy efficiency grant signposting; renewable energy advice; energy saving tips; guidance on how to switch energy provider; debt assistance; fuel poverty advice and oil-buying club signposting for householders.	The Housing Executive has advised that it has engaged with the Department for the Economy on the one stop shop approach including discussions to explore how its current facility might be enhanced as part of a DfE led action on the Executive’s Energy Strategy Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether consideration has been given to providing additional funding to the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme.
answer_text: This question has been passed to me for response from the Department for Communities. My Department is responsible for delivery of energy efficiency policy in Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) is administered by the Northern Ireland Authority for Utility Regulation. There are no plans to provide additional funding to NISEP in 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the current rate of fuel poverty in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is a global fuel crisis and it is having a major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced. However, I am committed to doing all I can to support to those who are experiencing real hardship.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she intends to broaden the membership of the emergencies leadership group to include fuel poverty organisations and the Utility Regulator.
answer_text: I re-established the Voluntary and Community Sector Emergencies Leadership Group on 1 June 2022, as part of my strategic response to the current cost of living crisis.  This Group was a model of best practice in community engagement to inform our Covid 19 Emergency Response.Membership of the Group is under review to ensure that the mitigation measures delivered represent a collective response and are informed by a wide range of community interests.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when it last carried out a disability access audit.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is very aware of the need to ensure that Parliament Buildings is accessible for people with disabilities and significant physical alterations and improvements have been carried out in Parliament Buildings for that purpose in recent years.The Assembly Commission has delivered a wide range of initiatives as part of its Disability Action Plans. The Actions Plans stretching back to 2011-12 can be found at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk/about-the-assembly/corporate-information/equality-and-good-relations/disability-action-plan-2016-21/.The Assembly Commission’s Disability Action Plans are subject to public consultation including on accessibility issues. The most recent Plan from 2022-26 contains actions related to establishing the views of people with a disability. For example, action number 3.2 in the Plan has an intended outcome “To gather information on the real-life experience of visitors with a disability to ensure that the services and facilities on offer at Parliament Buildings are accessible and fit for purpose.”Specific initiatives to enhance accessibility that have been delivered by the Assembly Commission are set out in the reply to AQW 773/22-27.The Assembly Commission will continue to give due consideration to opportunities to improve accessibility in Parliament Buildings.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the number of (i) applications received for advertised vacancies from individuals with a disability; and (ii) individuals successfully employed who have a recorded disability, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: In line with the Assembly Commission’s Retention and Disposal Schedule, recruitment information is only retained for a defined period and is therefore not held for 2017 and 2018. In 2019, three competitions were run with 45 applications received. Five applications were received from persons declaring a disability, of which none were appointed. In 2020, 11 competitions were run with 574 applications received. Forty-two applications were received from persons declaring a disability, of which one was appointed.  In 2021, 13 competitions were run with 1,046 applications received. Sixty-nine applications were received from persons declaring a disability, of which three were appointed.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what work it has undertaken to assess whether there are any barriers to employment for individuals with disabilities within Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is an equal opportunities employer and is committed to the principle that recruitment should be based solely on merit. The Assembly Commission welcome applications for employment from all eligible applicants, irrespective of religious belief, political opinion, race, age, gender, disability, marital status, sexual orientation or people with dependents or without.The process for appointing employees is fair and applied consistently to ensure equality of opportunity to all, including those with a disability. The Assembly Commission’s recruitment and selection process includes a Guaranteed Interview Scheme, which is designed to assist applicants with a disability who have demonstrated in their application form that they meet the essential criteria for the post, to be offered a guaranteed interview. The Assembly Commission is proactive in asking applicants to identify any adjustments they might need at every stage of the recruitment process. Such adjustments can include information being provided in an alternative format, additional time when completing recruitment tests/exercises and consideration of where an interview is held to avoid accessibility problems. Our policies and procedures, including those relating to recruitment and selection, have been equality screened and have included consultation with the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland in the screening process. This process requires the Assembly Commission to consider its policies and procedures in tandem with the Section 75 information that is held to consider the impact of the operation of the policy on all Section 75 groups, including those with a disability. Policies and procedures are kept under review by the Human Resources Office to ensure that they reflect good practice.In addition, the Assembly Commission’s Equality Manager engages proactively with a number of external groups, including groups representing persons with a disability. The consultation topics with these groups are wide-ranging and provide consultees with an opportunity to raise issues regarding barriers to employment for persons with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the steps being taken to promote itself as an inclusive employer.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is an equal opportunities employer and is committed to the principle that recruitment should be solely on the basis of merit. The Assembly Commission welcomes applications from all eligible applicants, irrespective of religious belief, political opinion, race, age, gender, disability, marital status, sexual orientation or people with dependents or without. The Assembly Commission has taken a number of steps to promote itself as an inclusive employer, including:Inclusive and accessible human resource policies and procedures, including recruitment and selection, that reflect good practice and are subject to equality screening including consultation with the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland. As part of the planning for every recruitment competition, equality information is considered and welcome statements are used as appropriate to attract applications from under-represented groups. Also at the planning stage for recruitment, the selection panel considers whether the post may be suitable for flexible working and this in included in the published information for applicants.Employment opportunities are communicated widely to attract applications for all parts of society. This includes using a variety of means to advertise, including the three main local newspapers; local job search sites, specialist/professional sites or journals; and university job portals. Opportunities are also advertised on the Assembly Commission’s recruitment website, which contains full information on the accessibility of the website and how an applicant may get in contact if they require specific advice or assistance.The Assembly Commission’s Recruitment and Selection Procedures include a Guaranteed Interview Scheme (GIS) for applicants with a disability. The purpose of a GIS is to assist applicants with a disability to secure an automatic interview, provided that they demonstrate the essential criteria for the post. The Assembly Commission has operated a GIS since 2011. When completing their application form, the Assembly Commission also asks applicants to indicate if they require reasonable adjustments at any stage during the recruitment process.To contribute to making our visitors feel welcomed to Parliament Buildings, our front-line staff who meet and greet visitors are offered training on a range of areas including hidden disabilities, sign language and alternative languages.   The Assembly Commission will continue to give consideration to opportunities to improve its status as an inclusive employer.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the implementation of its disability action plan.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission approved its current Disability Action Plan for the 2022-25 period on 9 February 2022, following a wide-ranging consultation with the public, disability organisations, the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland, Section 75 consultees, Assembly Commission business areas and Trade Union Side. This new Disability Action Plan 2022-25 contains 40 actions which demonstrate due regard to the need to promote positive attitudes towards disabled persons, and to the need to encourage participation by disabled persons in public life, as required by Section 49A of the Disability Discrimination Act 1995 (as amended by Article 5 of the Disability Discrimination (Northern Ireland) Order 2006). A copy of the Disability Action Plan 2022-25 can be found at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk/about-the-assembly/corporate-information/equality-and-good-relations/disability-action-plan-2022-25/disability-action-plan-2022-25/ The Disability Action Plan 2022-25 follows on from the Disability Action Plan 2016-21 which is now closed. Of the 54 actions in the Disability Action Plan 2016-21, 49 were achieved, two were not achieved and three were suspended. A copy of the closure report and the associated Action Plan can be found at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk/about-the-assembly/corporate-information/equality-and-good-relations/disability-action-plan-2016-21/disability-action-plan-2016-21-november-2021-final-status-update-and-closure-report/.Bi-annual updates on progress against the Disability Action Plan 2022-25 will be published on the Assembly website at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk/about-the-assembly/corporate-information/equality-and-good-relations/disability-action-plan-2022-25/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how funding will be sought to address the issue of there being 14 per cent more demand than there is supply within the Supporting People Programme.
answer_text: I refer you to AQW 927/22-29

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the departmental programmes at risk in the absence of an agreed budget.
answer_text: In the absence of a functioning Executive and an agreed 2022-23 Budget, my Department is operating within a constrained Contingency Planning Envelope.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she is taking, in the absence of an agreed budget, to provide additional funding for Supporting People providers given pay parity issues, inflation and the increasing energy costs.
answer_text: Despite the challenges created by the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget, all options available to my department are being explored

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps her Department is taking to implement the provisions of the Armed Forces Act 2021 which relate to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department along with other Government Departments has been liaising with the Ministry of Defence on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to implement the provisions of the Armed Forces Act 2021 which relate to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Responsibility for implementing the Armed Forces Act, and taking forward the New Decade New Approach commitment, lies with the First Minister and the deputy First Minister and, as they are not currently in post, my Department is awaiting further instructions.In the meantime, my officials facilitate a quarterly Armed Forces Liaison Forum, which provides an appropriate forum to discuss any difficulties that serving members of the armed forces and veterans may have in respect of healthcare provision in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applications made for Discretionary Support in each of the last 12 months, broken down by the time taken to process.
answer_text: The number of Discretionary Support applications received and processed with average days to clear for each of the last 12 months is shown in the table below:ReceivedProcessedAverage Actual Clearance Timein DaysJun-21850883862.2Jul-21865681032.8Aug-21932290174.4 Sep-21954999723.0Oct-21910893373.2Nov-211075994643.4Dec-21952787565.9Jan-2212941122326.8Feb-2210860120036.7Mar-2211009117914.4Apr-221026688075.1May-221034296507.8 The management information included in this response while accurate at the time it was provided may change when subjected to final reconciliation/verification checks prior to publication as applicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement she has had with Her Majesty’s Government regarding the rollout of the Energy Bills Support Scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Finance Minister, Conor Murphy is committed to working with Treasury to ensure the Energy Bills Support Scheme will provide assistance to citizens here given the absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget.I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need and I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether a Barnett consequential has been received for the Energy Bills Support Scheme announced in February 2022.
answer_text: I can confirm that £164.8 million of Barnett consequentials has been received for 2022/23 as a result of the Energy Bills Rebate which would provide a £200 repayable discount for domestic electricity customers in Britain. The distribution of this funding will require an Executive decision.  As announced by the Chancellor on 26 May, the rebate in Britain has now been made non-repayable in the form of a Household Energy Bills Support Scheme and increased to £400. A Barnett consequential has not been provided for this uplift.Following this latest announcement, I spoke with the Chief Secretary to Treasury and committed to work together to ensure the equivalent support is available to people here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps her Department is taking to implement the provisions of the Armed Forces Act 2021 which relate to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department complies with all relevant legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why a fuel poverty task force has not been established.
answer_text: My officials continue to have engagement with the Utility Regulator, Consumer Council and other stakeholders in relation to increased energy costs and wider issues relating to fuel poverty. These include exploring further options to mitigate the problems being faced.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what further steps it intends to take to make Parliament Buildings more accessible for people with disabilities.
answer_text: (AQW 773/22-27)The Assembly Commission is very aware of the need to ensure that Parliament Buildings is accessible for people with disabilities. Consequently, there is a wide range of accessibility initiatives in place in Parliament Buildings. These initiatives are set out on the Assembly’s website at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk/visit-and-learning/planning-your-visit/ to enable visitors to Parliament Buildings to utilise the accessibility arrangements. These arrangements include disabled parking spaces; wheelchair access to the building and to the public areas in the Chamber and in Committee rooms; a braille tour guide; accreditation under the “Louder than Words” charter (until its withdrawal in February 2022 but the principles and practices are still applied); and the adoption of the Autism Access Award to enable a more autism-friendly experience in Parliament Buildings.Additionally, the Assembly Commission has produced a sub-titled video (specifically designed for visitors on the autism spectrum) showing how to get to the building and what to expect on arrival.Physical alternations to Parliament Buildings have included the installation of external and internal access ramps, alterations to staircases and lifts, the installation of the ‘Changing Places’ facility, the installation and upgrading of hearing loop systems and the provision of automatic-opening doors in corridors ground and first floor corridors. Continuous transmission of Assembly proceedings is now available via the headphones in the Chamber and public gallery during Assembly business.All of these works have been carried out in a manner that is in keeping with the listed status of the building. Looking forward, the Assembly Commission approved its Disability Action Plan 2022-25 in February 2022. That Plan will ensure that the Assembly Commission continues to promote positive attitudes towards people with disabilities and encourages participation by people with disabilities in public life. The Plan contains 40 actions that will be monitored on a regular basis to ensure that they are delivered over the lifetime of the Plan. The Assembly Commission will continue to give due consideration to opportunities to improve the facilities in Parliament Buildings.Yours sincerelyJohn Blair MLA(On behalf of the Assembly Commission)

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the number of accessibility adaptation requests received in each of the last five years; (ii) the number approved; (iii) the number declined; and (iv) the number still being considered.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is very aware of the need to ensure that Parliament Buildings is accessible for everyone and endeavours to be proactive in terms of accessibility adaptations.As there is no formal process for accessibility adaptation requests, it is unfortunately not possible to report on numbers that have been received, approved, declined or still being considered. However, staff within the Human Resources Office, Building Services and the Equality Manager all work closely to ensure that the necessary arrangements are in place for Members and Assembly Commission staff to allow them to carry out their work. This includes, but is not limited to, recommendations following Occupational Health Assessments, such as stand-up desks and specialist chairs, adaptations to facilitate Personal Emergency Evacuation Plans (PEEPs) and the purchasing of equipment following Display Screen Equipment assessments.  Arrangements also include a braille tour guide; accreditation under the “Louder than Words” charter (until its withdrawal in February 2022 but the principles and practices are still applied); and the adoption of the Autism Access Award to enable a more autism-friendly experience in Parliament Buildings.Additionally, the Assembly Commission has produced a sub-titled video (specifically designed for visitors on the autism spectrum) showing how to get to the building and what to expect on arrival.Physical alternations to Parliament Buildings have included the installation of external and internal access ramps, alterations to staircases and lifts, the installation of the ‘Changing Places’ facility, the installation and upgrading of hearing loop systems and the provision of automatic-opening doors in corridors ground and first floor corridors. Continuous transmission of Assembly proceedings is now available via the headphones in the Chamber and public gallery during Assembly business.All of these works have been carried out in a manner that is in keeping with the listed status of the building. Looking forward, the Assembly Commission approved its Disability Action Plan 2022-25 in February 2022. That Plan will ensure that the Assembly Commission continues to promote positive attitudes towards people with disabilities and encourages participation by people with disabilities in public life. The Plan contains 40 actions that will be monitored on a regular basis to ensure that they are delivered over the lifetime of the Plan. The Assembly Commission will continue to give due consideration to opportunities to improve the facilities in Parliament Buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the selection criteria used to identify the 150 organisations who each received £1,500 energy payment from her Department.
answer_text: To be eligible for this support an organisation had to be in receipt of funding via the Neighbourhood Renewal Investment Programme or my Department’s Voluntary & Community Division programmes, and either:currently receive direct support through these programmes to meet energy/fuel costs: or be in receipt of direct support for overheads/running costs (not including energy/fuel costs) but NOT receiving funding support for energy/fuel costs from any other department or local government organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total amount paid out through her Department's £500 COVID Recognition Payment.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that £1.239m has been paid out to date for the Supporting People and Homelessness Recognition Scheme. It is anticipated that a total of £2.83m will be paid out for the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many individuals working in Supporting People projects and homelessness services have received the £500 COVID Recognition Payment; and (ii) how many of those individuals were working in those Supporting People and homelessness service projects at the time the payment was made.
answer_text: The Housing Executive have advised that payments for approximately 4000 eligible staff have been forwarded to employers following the receipt of approved Recognition Payment claims. The employers are then required to pass the payment onto employees through their own payroll process. The Housing Executive have also advised that the approved process does not allow them to quantify the number of individuals working in projects when the payment was made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many Civil Service staff are currently absent due to ill health, broken down by length of absence.
answer_text: The table below provides figures on numbers of NICS staff on sickness absence at the date of the request.Absence Length Breakdown Number of Staff 0 – 20 days32120 days – 6 months 4626 months – 12 months 11412 months – 18 months2418 months – 24 months1224 + months 08TOTAL SICKNESS ABSENCE941

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has started work on a dedicated fund for Changing Places toilets.
answer_text: My Department has provided funding for Changing Places through its Access and Inclusion Programme, delivered by local Councils and the  Museums Council, to improve access at arts, culture and active recreation venues for people with disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department is developing any further cost of living support measures.
answer_text: I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need and I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what proposals her Department is developing in preparation for an incoming Executive to deliver support for households who do not qualify for the Chancellor’s cost of living support package.
answer_text: I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need and I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail the current number of staff vacancies in each Department, broken down by grade; and (ii) what steps are being taken to fill the vacancies.
answer_text: Each Civil Service Department is responsible for managing their resources – both financial and staff. Details of the total civil service vacancies are not held centrally, only the caseload of posts each department is seeking to fill.Table 1 provides a breakdown of total vacancy caseload by analogous grade and department as at the end of May 2022.Table 1: NICSHR Vacancy Requisition Caseload as at end May 2022Analogous GradeDAERADEDfCDfEDfIDoFDoHDOJPPSTEOTotalSCS32110 9310 929G61445121456  51G74151417842151918170DP1299343219502029719348SO121201055329854055229539EO18926318814014017342EO258142351826531934213472AO751326267738679102568AA1 10 4612 47 80Prison Grades       215  215Total5316972914628834310948230872,814	Steps being taken to fill vacancies	The NICS workforce is complex in that it consists of c400 grades across four main occupation groups – General Service, Professional and Technical (P&T), Prison Uniform staff and Industrial.  The P&T occupation group also contains a number of separate professions – computing, accounting, legal, mechanical and electrical engineers, etc (approx. c.60 in total).		Recruitment activity is being prioritised based on the current vacancy requisition caseload: 60% (1,693) are General Service vacancies, 29% (817) are P&T, 8% (215) are Prison Service Uniformed grades, and 3% (89) Industrial.		At end of May, 25% (714) of our vacancy caseload is at offer stage. This means we have allocated candidates from existing supply to fill the vacancy and the candidates are at various stages of the offer process.		Work has continued at pace to move to supply for General Service grades and the current position is as follows:	There are live competitions with supply from the 2019 AO, 2021 EO2 and 2021 EO1 open recruitment volume campaigns from which we are filling any vacancy requisitions across these three grades.	In March 2022, we launched an SO and a DP open recruitment volume campaign to generate new supply of candidates to fill current vacancies at this grade and create supply for future vacancies arising. Over 5,000 people applied for the SO and 3,300 for the DP. The selection process is ongoing for both competitions.	We are launching a new NICS Graduate Management Programme in September 2022.	Recruitment for the other three main occupational groups is continuing on a rolling basis, creating supply to fill the wide range of job roles across the NICS. As well as continuing to draw from existing supply to fill vacancies arising, there is a rolling recruitment plan to create additional supply. We currently have a number of competitions in progress for these three occupation groups:	41 for Professional and Technical jobs	10 live competitions for Prison Service Uniformed grades	2 live competitions for Industrial Grades		In addition to the above, competition planning and design work is underway in relation to a number of other occupations / grades, including:		Competition plans are at an advanced stage for G6 and G7 Gene

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Fair Start Action Plan.
answer_text: Work is on-going within my Department to take forward the actions within the Fait Start Report. There are currently forty-five actions being considered for delivery or are already being delivered this year. A budget of £6.1m (Other Resource) has been allocated to take forward this work.  A Capital budget will be bid for separately.My officials will be providing a 6-monthly update on progress to the NI Executive and NI Assembly by 30 June 2022 and this will be published on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she intends to move forward with a call for evidence on potential changes to the current homelessness legislation.
answer_text: All options to improve homelessness legislation are under consideration.  Given the pressure that homelessness services are currently under, and in the absence of functioning executive and budget, I have written to the Minster for Health and Minister for Justice requesting an urgent meeting to consider immediate steps that can be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to identify possible mitigations to allow existing home care dog boarding providers to operate while legislation is brought forward to update the Animal Boarding Establishments Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1974.
answer_text: It is acknowledged that complying with existing licensing requirements can be difficult for dog home boarding businesses. The Department has not, therefore, required businesses undertaking this activity to cease trading but works closely with operators to encourage them to meet licencing conditions where they are able to do so.My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes, during this mandate. To this end, my Officials are presently planning a schedule of animal welfare related legislation to be progressed during this mandate. This will include action on this matter.Please be assured, however, that, in the meantime, any dogs cared for in a home setting continue to be protected by the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 which provides enforcement agencies with substantial powers to act in situations where an animal is subject to unnecessary suffering in any setting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many children have not been offered a post-primary place, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) publishes details in respect of children for whom a post-primary application was submitted, but who are yet to secure a place.  The information is updated regularly and can be found by using the following link: https://www.eani.org.uk/publications/breakdown-of-statistics-re-p7-pupils-who-have-yet-to-be-placed-0

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to review and update the Animal Boarding Establishments Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1974 to recognise home care dog boarding.
answer_text: My Department is committed to reviewing and updating the licensing system for animal boarding establishments, including those that provide boarding from homes, during this mandate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what impact the lack of an agreed 2022/23 Executive budget will have on his Department.
answer_text: The Minister of Finance’s proposed Draft Budget 2022-25, as circulated, would have a devastating impact on my Department’s ability to deliver vital services that support economic recovery, investment in skills, innovation, tourism and green growth, all of which are crucial to delivering a 10X economy.It would have resulted in a cut in real terms to my Department of 8% in Year 1 rising to 13% in Year 3, and totalling £259 million across the three years.  In the absence of an agreed budget by the Executive, a budget planning envelope has been communicated by the Department of Finance to all Departments.  My Department has made the necessary plans to live within that planning envelope for 2022/23, until a budget is agreed. I would hope that a future Executive budget will prioritise economic recovery, investment in skills, innovation, tourism, and green growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail what impact the lack of an agreed 2022/23 Executive budget will have on her Department.
answer_text: Following the resignation of the First Minister and the subsequent lack of an Executive, a Budget for 2022-25 could not be finalised. The Finance Minister wrote to departments to set out a way forward in the absence of an Executive to agree a Budget. This process involved the Department of Finance issuing departments with contingency planning envelopes for the 2022-23 financial year only. These envelopes provided departments with an assessment of the minimum funding they could reasonably expect for 2022-23 and allowed departments to plan for expenditure until such times as a Budget could be agreed.My Department has previously published a Budget 2022-25 Consequences Document, which sets out the potential impacts of the current proposals in the 2022-25 draft Budget which remain relevant in the absence of an agreed 2022-23 Executive Budget, and can be found at https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/department-justice-draft-budget-2022-25-consequences-document.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what impact the lack of an agreed 2022/23 Executive budget will have on her Department.
answer_text: My hope would be that a new Executive will re-consider the Finance Minister’s previous 2022-25 Draft Budget proposals, with the aim of agreeing a multi-year budget in due course. I am previously on record as having highlighted that the Draft Budget proposals would have left the Education sector facing well over £200 million of unfunded inescapable pressures next year. Following further assessment, this position has been revised and increased to over £300 million of unfunded inescapable pressures, with the result that it will be extremely challenging for my Department to implement a range of things, including the education actions in the Northern Ireland COVID recovery plan and any recommendations that will emanate from the independent review of education.In advance of a Final Budget being agreed by a new Executive, I have made a number of 2022-23 risk-based expenditure planning decisions to ensure minimal impact on children and young people.  This includes maintaining the Aggregated Schools Budget at the same level as in 2021-22,  providing funding to continue the Healthy Happy Minds pilot and Engage II programme until June 2022,  extending the School Holiday Food Grant payments to cover July & August 2022, and the funding of a number of other time sensitive critical areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what impact the lack of an agreed 2022/23 Executive budget will have on his Department.
answer_text: My Department is facing significant resource pressures in 2022-23, impacted by a decade of austerity budgets from Westminster, the unprecedented increases in energy costs and rising inflation. The refusal of the DUP to form an Executive means £420m of funds being held at the centre cannot be released and I therefore cannot bid for additional in year funding. The blocking of the Executive also means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan future investment.In the absence of an Executive, the Department of Finance has provided contingency planning envelopes, to assist departments in making decisions on the allocation of resources, until a Budget is established. However, the resource planning envelope falls far short of my Department’s requirements and given the quantum of pressures, and the absence of an Executive, there will likely be very limited action that I could take without directly impacting frontline services which include provision of safe drinking water and waste water treatment; public transport and maintenance of our roads and rivers network. In relation to capital, as the largest capital spending department here, the absence of an Executive and a three year budget, will have implications for my Department, risking the deliverability of its full capital requirements for 2022-23 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what impact the lack of an agreed 2022/23 Executive budget will have on his Department.
answer_text: Although there is no agreed budget for 2022-23, the Department of Finance (DoF) has provided Resource DEL and Capital DEL Contingency Planning Envelopes to departments for planning purposes.  On Resource DEL this includes the rolled over baseline from 2021-22 of £224.2m as well as Farm and Fisheries funding of £315.9m from HM Treasury.  On Capital DEL this includes £101.2m and covers existing contractual commitments as well as some funding for routine Capital maintenance.  Therefore the Contingency Planning Envelopes allow the Department to continue to progress the work that was already underway at the end of 2021-22.However, in recent months various sectors have been affected by the significant rise in the cost of living.  In particular the agri-food sector has been exposed to significant rising input costs in relation to feed, fertiliser and fuel. The fisheries sector is similarly affected by fuel increases. The Contingency Planning Envelopes do not cover such emerging pressures.  In response to the rising costs I have written to the Minister of Finance seeking support to funding the agri-food and fisheries sectors in 2022-23.  It is important that financial assistance is provided to enable these sectors to deal with such market disruptions that are substantially impacting on otherwise viable businesses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Disability Strategy.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work in the co-design of the Executive’s four social inclusion strategies including the Disability Strategy. 		The drafting of the Strategy is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive. I am committed to the delivery of a meaningful strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what impact the lack of an agreed 2022/23 budget will have on the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has been allocated a “contingency envelope” for planning purposes and there remains a degree of uncertainty regarding levels of funding.	It is critical that an Executive is established and a budget it agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive's operational preparedness in the absence of an agreed 2022/23 budget.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has been allocated a “contingency envelope” for planning purposes and there remains a degree of uncertainty regarding levels of funding.	It is critical that an Executive is established and a budget it agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the required budget to complete all planned (i) grass verge cutting; and (ii) weed spraying, against the available budget.
answer_text: The budgets for grass cutting and weed spraying cannot be confirmed until an overall budget for 2022-23 has been agreed.  However, my Department has commenced its programme of grass cutting and weed spraying for the current year, which is being kept under review in the context of the current financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: Options are currently being considered due to no functioning Executive or agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many (i) households; and (ii) individuals were on the social housing waiting list on 1 May in each of the last ten years, broken down by (a) Full Duty Applicant status; (b) housing stress; (c) local council area; and (d) length of time on the waiting list.
answer_text: Due to the large volume of data requested, tables outlining waiting list and allocations figures for March 2022 and for the previous 10 years have been placed in the Assembly Library.Figures are provided by composite household group, and broken down by Local Government District and time on list. Housing Stress and FDA applicant data is also shown.Applicant Information (all applicants, applicants in housing stress (HS) and Full Duty Applicants (FDA)) is shown as at the end of March for the relevant year.  Allocation information (all allocations and allocations to housing stress applicants) refers to the number of allocations in the preceding 12 month period.  FDA refers to Full Duty Applicants – these are applicants accepted as being homeless, to whom the Housing Executive owes a duty under the Homelessness legislation, Article 10(2) of the Housing Order, 1988.

answer_text: Housing Executive 2021/22 Pay Remits have been approved

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are grants available to cover the cost of installing a residential septic tank.
answer_text: There are no grants available to cover the cost of installing a residential septic tank.Advice or guidance on regulatory controls and compliance for domestic septic tank installation can be obtained through NIEA at nieaconsents@daera-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Inconsiderate Pavement Parking consultation.
answer_text: The consultation on pavement parking finished on 18 March 2022, and in response the Department received 1,209 replies to the online questionnaire and 28 written replies.  The responses are currently being analysed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost of the Expert Panel on Educational Underachievement.
answer_text: The cost of the Expert Panel on Educational Underachievement in 2021/22 was £271k consisting of £196k in respect of panel members and £75k in respect of secretariat costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost of the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: The Independent Review of Education commenced work in October 2021 and the Review is expected to last 18 months.  The Review is being led by a Panel of 5 individuals.  These roles are remunerated. The total costs associated with this Panel, including remuneration and travel and subsistence expenses, is estimated to be in the region of £181k.  Additional expenditure will be incurred during the course of this Review to include my Department’s Secretariat team which supports the work of the Panel; research work undertaken on behalf of the Panel; and costs associated with stakeholder engagement events.  This additional expenditure is estimated to be £913k through to end March 2023 when the Panel is due to publish its report. Recommendations made by the Panel may also have associated costs; where possible these costs will be detailed in the Panel’s final report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he intends to review the regulatory powers of the Utility Regulator.
answer_text: The regulatory powers of the Utility Regulator will be reviewed as part of the work to deliver the Executive’s Energy Strategy

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total number of reported street light outages, broken down by constituency, in each of the last twelve months.
answer_text: My Department maintains its records relating to street lighting outages by Roads Division rather by electoral constituency. The following table provides details of the number of reported street light outages within each of the Department’s four Roads Divisions during the period 1st May 2021 to 30th April 2022:EasternNorthernSouthernWesternMay252362258286June219207289144July335370236204August523489547300September734767899588October864997925724November1157102112101161December809876992711January919898970806February713696743585March613547570392April427300335213I am pleased to be able to advise the Member that my Department is currently providing a full street lighting maintenance service, with the majority of outages generally being repaired within the established target of 5 working days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the average time taken to process a firearms license application.
answer_text: My Department does not process applications for a firearms licence and does not hold this information.Applications for the grant of a firearm certificate, under Article 4 of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004, are made to the Chief Constable.  This is an operational policing matter and as such, I suggest you address this question to the Chief Constable of the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has considered the availability of departmental easements to reopen the Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed 2022-23 Budget, my Department is operating within a constrained Contingency Planning Envelope. I am fully committed to supporting people in need. I would like to see this scheme reopen, I have been assured by the Finance Minister that there is money available, however this requires Executive approval. I am deeply frustrated that the absence of a functioning Executive prevents support for this essential scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many firearms license applications are currently waiting to be processed.
answer_text: My Department does not process applications for a firearms licence and does not hold this information.Applications for the grant of a firearm certificate, under Article 4 of the Firearms (Northern Ireland) Order 2004, are made to the Chief Constable.  This is an operational policing matter and as such, I suggest that you address the question to the Chief Constable of the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total spend on the Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme.
answer_text: My Department made a contribution of £2 million to the scheme which was fully spent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland Housing Executive revitalisation.
answer_text: Options are currently being considered due to no functioning Executive or agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work in the co-design of the Executive’s social inclusion strategies. 		The drafting of the strategies is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive. I am committed to the delivery of meaningful strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cost of administering the Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme.
answer_text: My Department made a contribution of £2 million to the scheme. This grant funding was used solely to purchase the supply of fuel. Information about the cost of administering the scheme may be sought from Bryson Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of applicants awaiting a booked MOT test, broken down by the length of wait.
answer_text: As of 24 May 2022, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) confirmed that almost 195,000 vehicles have a vehicle test booking appointment up to 30 September 2022, and just over 67,000 further appointment slots are available across its network of test centres for the same period.The DVA has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they are able to change existing appointments to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The DVA does not currently hold information to enable it to accurately report the average waiting times for vehicle tests, given that this position changes on a regular basis. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the impact that the absence of an agreed 2022-23 budget will have on his Department.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed budget for 2022-23 the Finance Minister wrote to departments in April setting out a way forward which involved DoF issuing departments with contingency planning envelopes for the 2022-23 financial year.  These envelopes provided departments with an assessment of the minimum funding they could reasonably expect for 2022-23 and allowed departments to plan for expenditure until such times as a Budget could be agreed. I have agreed an approach for my Department to proceed with opening allocations which will continue to fund activity in the first quarter of 2022/23 at previous levels while controlling spending in line with the advice from the Finance Minister. However there remains a great deal of uncertainty on the future financial position. Health and Social Care’s reliance in recent years on significant levels of non-recurrent funding means that we are expecting to face an extremely challenging financial outlook. While we are anticipating significant allocations for Health once a Budget is agreed the 2022/23 budget will continue to require careful managing in order to develop a break even position. This means that the further into the year we progress, the harder it becomes to continue spending at the current rate without the certainty of additional allocations which we hope might be forthcoming from the funding that is currently being held at the centre.Beyond that, my Department needs a multi- year budget agreed to cover the next three years in order to provide a level of certainty on our future funding position and to enable us to start developing longer term plans to deliver services within the funding envelope provided and to sustain services going forward. This lack of certainty is very unhelpful to our Health and Social Care system and will likely impact on our ability to address key issues such as tackling Northern Ireland’s waiting lists and funding the cancer and mental health strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the impact that the absence of an agreed 2022-23 budget will have on her Department.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed 2022/23 Budget, my Department is operating within a constrained Contingency Planning Envelope

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps her Department is taking to replace homes demolished through the Housing Executive Tower Block Strategy.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the need for replacement homes for those blocks demolished via the Tower Blocks Strategy is assessed on a location-by-location basis and is determined by a Housing Needs Assessment.In line with the Department for Communities’ current policy on the delivery of new social housing it is anticipated that the Housing Executive will be transferring the cleared sites to Housing Associations to provide the new homes.  The timing of these transfers is influenced by progress in clearance and preparation for demolition and is included in the delivery plan for each relevant location.  No such transfers have taken place as yet.In locations where the put-back on the existing footprint of the blocks is insufficient the viability of overspill sites will be explored

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a update on the decision pertaining to Knocknagoney Avenue maisonettes.
answer_text: My Department raised further queries with the Housing Executive regarding leaseholder consultation and health and safety issues at the Knocknagoney flats/maisonettes/shops block.  The Housing Executive has advised that it is currently taking steps to address these health and safety issues before responding to the Department’s queries.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many new homes have been delivered in the last 12 months, broken down by bedroom size.
answer_text: In 2021/22, construction starts began on 224 one bedroom, 1,128 two bedroom, 309 three bedroom and 52 four bedroom new-build social homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of households on the housing waiting list.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that as of 31 March 2022 there were a total of 44,426 applicants on the waiting list, with 31,407 of those deemed to be in housing stress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures her Department is considering to support households impacted by the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive prevents measures being introduced.However, I am committed to continuing to support individuals, families and communities who are experiencing real hardship. To giving them the helping hand they need to heat their homes, to put a meal on the table.I am determined that my department will continue to deliver for all those who are in need.I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all of the options.

answer_text: During the five year period 2017/18 – 2021/22 work started on the construction of 214 social housing new-build properties (with 4 bedrooms), across the 18 Parliamentary Constituencies. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will reopen the Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme.
answer_text: I am fully committed to supporting people in need. I want to reopen this scheme and I have been assured by the Finance Minister there is funding available to do this, however this requires Executive approval. I am deeply frustrated that the absence of a functioning Executive prevents the reopening of this essential support  scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many applicants received a payment under the Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme.
answer_text: 20,142 applicants received support from this Scheme operated by Bryson Care. This information has been provided by Bryson Care.
